# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). Why is it considered the most important economic parameter of an engine?**

**Model Answer:**
BSFC is defined as the mass of fuel consumed per unit **brake power** per unit time:

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kW·h}} \right]$$

It is the most important economic parameter because:
1. **Direct consumer metric:** The end user pays for fuel to obtain useful shaft work — BSFC captures this ratio precisely.
2. **Load-independent comparison:** Unlike $\eta_{bth}$, BSFC is *not* affected by calorific value assumptions and is measured directly (fuel + dynamometer).
3. **Lower BSFC = better economy:** Typical NA diesel: 0.24–0.28 kg/kW·h; Turbocharged: 0.21–0.24 kg/kW·h.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the function of a turbocharger with a neat block diagram. What is "turbo lag"?**

**Model Answer:**
A **turbocharger** is a turbine-compressor assembly driven by exhaust gas energy. Its function is to **compress intake air above atmospheric pressure**, increasing the air mass inducted into the cylinder per cycle. This boosts power output (30–100%) and improves thermal efficiency. Components: **turbine** (driven by exhaust), **compressor** (boosts air), connected by a common shaft.

**Turbo lag** is the delay between throttle opening and turbocharger spool-up to provide useful boost. It occurs because the turbine must accelerate from low to high rpm (80,000–250,000 rpm) using only exhaust energy. Mitigated by **variable geometry turbochargers (VGT)**, twin-scroll turbines, and **anti-lag systems (ALS)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the experimental procedure to conduct a **performance test on a single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine** using a rope-brake dynamometer. List all instruments and the readings recorded at each load step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following observations were recorded during a performance test on a 4-stroke diesel engine:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Speed $N$ | 1500 rpm |
| Brake drum diameter $D$ | 1.2 m |
| Rope diameter $d$ | 0.02 m |
| Dead weight $W$ | 50 kg |
| Spring balance reading $S$ | 5 kg |
| Fuel consumption | 2.5 kg/hr |
| Air consumption | 36 kg/hr |
| Cooling water flow rate | 0.005 kg/s |
| Cooling water inlet temp | 28 °C |
| Cooling water outlet temp | 62 °C |
| Exhaust gas temperature | 420 °C |
| Ambient temperature | 32 °C |
| LCV of fuel | 42,000 kJ/kg |
| $C_{p,exh} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K, $C_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K |

**Calculate:**
1. Brake power (BP) in kW
2. Brake thermal efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$)
3. Brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)
4. Air-fuel ratio (AFR)
5. Heat lost in cooling water (kJ/s and % of input)
6. Heat lost in exhaust (kJ/s and % of input)
7. Unaccounted heat (kJ/s and % of input)
8. Draw up the **Heat Balance Sheet** per kg of fuel.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Schematic:** Refer to **Figure 4.1** (Rope-Brake Dynamometer setup with engine, fuel burette, orifice meter, manometer, tachometer, and cooling water circuit).

**Experimental Procedure:**
1. **Pre-test inspection:** Check engine oil level, fuel supply, cooling water flow, lubrication system, and battery (for starting).
2. **Start the engine** using the starter motor and allow a **warm-up of 10–15 minutes** at no load until all temperatures stabilize.
3. **Apply no-load baseline readings** first: Note fuel burette reading, time for 10 cc consumption, manometer head, RPM, water inlet/outlet temperatures, exhaust temperature.
4. **Apply load incrementally** by adding dead weights (typically 1–2 kg steps) on the rope brake. Wait 2 minutes at each load for steady-state.
5. At each load, record: **fuel consumption time, manometer $\Delta h$, RPM $N$, spring balance $S$, all temperatures.**
6. **Continue until full load** (rope just begins to slip on drum — do NOT exceed).
7. **Reduce load** to zero and shut down.
8. **Repeat for 3 trials** at each load point and average.

**Instruments Used:**
- Rope-brake dynamometer (drum + rope + dead weights + spring balance)
- Tachometer (digital or mechanical)
- Stopwatch + graduated fuel burette
- U-tube differential manometer (with orifice meter)
- Thermocouples/mercury thermometers (water in/out, exhaust)
- Rotameter for cooling water flow

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1: Effective Drum Radius**

$$R = \frac{D + d}{2} = \frac{1.2 + 0.02}{2} = 0.61 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Net Tangential Force**

$$F_{net} = (W - S) \cdot g = (50 - 5) \times 9.81 = 441.45 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Brake Power**

$$BP = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) g R}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 45 \times 9.81 \times 0.61}{60}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1500 \times 45 \times 9.81 \times 0.61}{60} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1500 \times 269.22}{60} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 403\,830}{60} \\
&= \frac{2\,537\,518}{60} \\
&= 42\,292 \text{ W} \\
&\approx \mathbf{42.29 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Brake Thermal Efficiency**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{2.5}{3600} = 6.944 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LCV} = 6.944 \times 10^{-4} \times 42\,000 = 29.167 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{42.29}{29.167} = \mathbf{1.45 \text{ or } 145\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PHYSICAL INCONSISTENCY!** $\eta_{bth}$ cannot exceed 100% (or even 50% for any practical diesel). This means the problem data is internally inconsistent — likely because at 1500 rpm a 4-stroke single-cylinder engine producing 42 kW would require a much larger fuel consumption (the fuel rate of 2.5 kg/hr is far too low). **In KTU exam solutions, you would complete all formal calculations and explicitly state the physical anomaly at the end.** This shows the examiner deep conceptual mastery and earns full method marks.

**Step 5: BSFC**

$$BSFC = \frac{2.5}{42.29} = 0.0591 \text{ kg/kW·h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** Real-world BSFC for diesel is 0.24–0.40. The computed 0.059 is unrealistically low — confirming the data inconsistency.

**Step 6: AFR**

$$AFR = \frac{36}{2.5} = \mathbf{14.4:1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** AFR = 14.4 is at the stoichiometric limit for diesel, which is physically unlikely in normal operation (diesel typically runs lean at 15–18:1). Combined with low fuel rate, this confirms the anomaly.

**Step 7: Cooling Water Heat Loss (per second)**

$$\dot{Q}_{cw} = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T = 0.005 \times 4.186 \times (62 - 28) = 0.005 \times 4.186 \times 34 = 0.7116 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 8: Exhaust Heat Loss (per second)**

$$\dot{m}_{exh} = \frac{36 + 2.5}{3600} = 0.01069 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = 0.01069 \times 1.05 \times (420 - 32) = 0.01069 \times 1.05 \times 388 = 4.358 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 9: Unaccounted Heat (per second)**

$$\dot{Q}_{unac} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{Q}_{BP} - \dot{Q}_{cw} - \dot{Q}_{exh}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{unac} &= 29.167 - 42.29 - 0.7116 - 4.358 \\
&= -18.19 \text{ kJ/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative unaccounted heat is impossible.** This mathematically proves the input data set violates the First Law. A correct dataset would have $\dot{Q}_{in}$ substantially larger than $\dot{Q}_{BP} + \dot{Q}_{cw} + \dot{Q}_{exh}$.

**Formal Heat Balance Sheet (as it would appear):**

| Item | kJ/s | % of Input |
|------|------|------------|
| Heat input from fuel | 29.167 | 100.00 |
| Brake power | 42.292 | **145.00 (anomalous)** |
| Cooling water loss | 0.712 | 2.44 |
| Exhaust gas loss | 4.358 | 14.94 |
| Unaccounted loss | −18.195 | **−62.38 (impossible)** |

**Examiner's Conclusion (write this in your answer):**
*"The given experimental data violates the First Law of Thermodynamics as the calculated brake thermal efficiency exceeds 100%. This typically indicates an error in fuel consumption measurement (e.g., burette calibration) or in the dynamometer load reading. In an actual lab test, the data should be re-verified. The complete methodology and computational framework have, however, been correctly demonstrated."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** rpm to rev/sec ($N/60$) in BP formula → **−2 marks**
> 2. **Using drum diameter D instead of effective radius R** = $(D+d)/2$ → **−2 marks**
> 3. **Neglecting rope thickness** ($d$ term) → small but consistent mark loss
> 4. **Wrong sign convention** if $S > W$ → BP becomes negative (interpret as motoring)
> 5. **Mixing per-hour and per-second** quantities in HBS → entire HBS becomes invalid → **−3 marks**
> 6. **Not stating physical anomalies** when they appear — examiners REWARD students who recognize impossible values!

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a sketch the **principle, construction, and working of a turbocharger**. Derive the expression for **compressor isentropic efficiency** and discuss how turbocharging improves the volumetric efficiency of a diesel engine.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A turbocharged diesel engine operates with the following data:
- Compressor inlet: $T_1 = 300$ K, $p_1 = 1.0$ bar
- Compressor outlet: $T_2 = 430$ K, $p_2 = 2.5$ bar
- Turbine inlet: $T_3 = 850$ K, $p_3 = 1.8$ bar
- Turbine outlet: $p_4 = 1.05$ bar, Turbine isentropic efficiency $\eta_t = 0.80$
- Assume $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- Air mass flow rate through compressor: 0.08 kg/s

**Calculate:**
1. Compressor isentropic efficiency
2. Actual turbine outlet temperature $T_4$
3. Turbine power output
4. Compressor power input
5. Net turbocharger power output
6. Comment on whether the turbocharger is self-sustaining (i.e., turbine energy > compressor demand).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Principle:** A turbocharger is an **exhaust-driven centrifugal compressor**. High-energy exhaust gases from the engine drive a radial-flow turbine, which on a common shaft drives a centrifugal compressor that pressurizes the intake air above atmospheric.

**Construction (refer Figure 4.2):**
- **Turbine housing:** Houses the radial turbine wheel. Exhaust gas enters tangentially, expands, and exits through the central volute to the tailpipe.
- **Compressor housing:** Houses the centrifugal compressor impeller. Air enters axially, is thrown radially outward by the impeller, and exits the scroll at high pressure.
- **Common shaft:** Connects turbine and compressor, supported by floating-ring or ball bearings, often oil-cooled and water-cooled. Spins at 80,000–250,000 rpm.
- **Waste gate:** A bypass valve that opens when boost pressure exceeds target (prevents over-boost).
- **Intercooler (optional):** Cools compressed air (typically to 50–60 °C) to increase density before the intake manifold.

**Working:** Exhaust gases leaving the engine cylinder at high temperature (500–700 °C) and moderate pressure pass through the turbine, expanding and doing work on the turbine blades. This work spins the shaft, which drives the compressor. The compressor draws ambient air, compresses it to 1.5–3.0 bar, and delivers it to the intake manifold. The denser charge air allows more fuel to be injected, increasing indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) and hence brake power.

**Derivation — Compressor Isentropic Efficiency:**

For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression from $T_1, p_1$ to $p_2$:

$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

The **isentropic efficiency** is defined as the ratio of minimum (isentropic) work to actual work:

$$\eta_c = \frac{W_{c,isentropic}}{W_{c,actual}} = \frac{C_p (T_{2s} - T_1)}{C_p (T_2 - T_1)} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c = \frac{(p_2/p_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}{T_2/T_1 - 1}}$$

**Effect on Volumetric Efficiency:**

Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of actual mass inducted to the swept-volume mass at ambient:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{m_{actual}}{\rho_1 V_s} = \frac{m_{actual}}{V_s \cdot \frac{p_1}{R T_1}}$$

With turbocharging, intake pressure $p_2 > p_1$, and post-intercooling temperature $T_{2,cooled} < T_2$. The inducted mass scales as:

$$m_{turbo} \approx m_{NA} \times \frac{p_2}{p_1} \times \frac{T_1}{T_{2,cooled}}$$

With $p_2/p_1 = 2.0$ and $T_{2,cooled}/T_1 = 1.2$ (typical intercooled case), the mass increases by factor $\approx 2.0/1.2 = 1.67$, i.e., a **67% improvement** in volumetric efficiency.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1: Compressor Isentropic Efficiency**

Compute isentropic outlet temperature:
$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{0.2857} = 300 \times (2.5)^{0.2857}$$

$$(2.5)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 2.5} = e^{0.2857 \times 0.9163} = e^{0.2618} = 1.2993$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.2993 = 389.8 \text{ K}$$

$$\eta_c = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{389.8 - 300}{430 - 300} = \frac{89.8}{130} = \mathbf{0.6908 \approx 69.08\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** A $\eta_c$ of 69% is **below the typical 75–85% range** of modern centrifugal compressors, indicating this might be an older design or a heavily loaded turbo. Still acceptable for calculation purposes.

**Step 2: Actual Turbine Outlet Temperature**

Compute isentropic outlet temperature:
$$T_{4s} = T_3 \left( \frac{p_4}{p_3} \right)^{0.2857} = 850 \times \left( \frac{1.05}{1.8} \right)^{0.2857}$$

$$\frac{p_4}{p_3} = \frac{1.05}{1.8} = 0.5833$$

$$(0.5833)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln(0.5833)} = e^{0.2857 \times (-0.5390)} = e^{-0.1540} = 0.8573$$

$$T_{4s} = 850 \times 0.8573 = 728.7 \text{ K}$$

Apply turbine efficiency:
$$T_4 = T_3 - \eta_t (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 850 - 0.80 \times (850 - 728.7)$$

$$T_4 = 850 - 0.80 \times 121.3 = 850 - 97.04 = \mathbf{752.96 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 3: Turbine Power Output**

$$W_t = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (850 - 752.96) = 1.005 \times 97.04 = 97.53 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{W}_t = \dot{m}_a \times W_t = 0.08 \times 97.53 = \mathbf{7.80 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4: Compressor Power Input**

$$W_c = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (430 - 300) = 1.005 \times 130 = 130.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{W}_c = 0.08 \times 130.65 = \mathbf{10.45 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5: Net Turbocharger Power**

$$\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{W}_t - \dot{W}_c = 7.80 - 10.45 = \mathbf{-2.65 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

> [!WARNING]
> **The turbocharger is NOT self-sustaining** in this case. The turbine can supply only 7.80 kW but the compressor demands 10.45 kW. There is a deficit of 2.65 kW. This energy deficit must come from somewhere — in practice it would cause:
> 1. The shaft to **decelerate** (boost pressure drops)
> 2. The compressor to operate at a **lower pressure ratio**
> 3. Or, in a real engine, additional energy would come from **higher exhaust temperatures** at part-load-to-full-load transition.

**Diagnostic conclusion:** With $T_3 = 850$ K and a back-pressure ratio $p_4/p_3 = 1.05/1.8 = 0.583$, the turbine expansion is too small. The engine designer would either (a) accept lower boost at this operating point, (b) reduce $p_4$ by enlarging the exhaust system, or (c) operate the engine at a higher load where $T_3$ reaches 950–1000 K.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

**📌 Performance Test Fundamentals:**
- **BP** = (2π × N × (W − S) × R) / 60 (in W) — rope-brake formula
- **Effective radius** R = (D + d)/2 — **never forget the rope diameter!**
- **BSFC** lower is better; diesel typical: 0.24–0.28 kg/kW·h
- **$\eta_{bth}$** = BP / ($\dot{m}_f$ × LCV); **$\eta_{ith}$** = IP / ($\dot{m}_f$ × LCV)
- **$\eta_m$** = BP/IP = 1 − FP/IP

**📌 Heat Balance Sheet Components:**
- Heat input = $\dot{m}_f$ × LCV (must be 100% of the basis)
- BP work = 25–35% of input (typical diesel)
- Cooling water loss = 25–35%
- Exhaust loss = 30–40% (largest single loss → motivates turbocharging!)
- Unaccounted = 5–15%
- **All percentages must sum to 100% ± 2%**

**📌 Turbocharger Essentials:**
- Driven by **exhaust energy only** (not crankshaft) — no parasitic loss
- Pressure ratio $r_p$ = 1.5–3.0 typical
- Turbine speeds: 80,000–250,000 rpm
- **Compressor** $\eta_c$ = $(r_p)^{0.2857} − 1) / (T_2/T_1 − 1)$
- **Turbine** $\eta_t$ = $(T_3 − T_4) / (T_3 − T_{4s})$
- Self-sustaining requires $W_t \ge W_c$ (with margin for bearing friction)

**📌 Numerical Hygiene (KTU Exam Survival Kit):**
1. **Unit consistency:** Convert all flow rates to kg/s (÷3600) and powers to kW before any HBS.
2. **Per-second vs per-hour:** Never mix them in the same equation.
3. **Sign of (W − S):** If S > W, BP is negative → engine is motored.
4. **Stoichiometric check:** If AFR < 14.5 for diesel, suspect data error.
5. **Heat balance sanity check:** $\eta_{bth}$ must be < 50% for any real CI engine.

**📌 Key Equipment & Their Purposes:**
| Equipment | Measures |
|-----------|----------|
| Rope-brake drum + dead weight | Brake torque → BP |
| Spring balance | Net rope tension → confirms BP direction |
| Tachometer | N (rpm) |
| Orifice + U-tube manometer | Air flow rate $\dot{m}_a$ → AFR |
| Fuel burette + stopwatch | Fuel consumption → BSFC |
| Thermocouples (water + exhaust) | Temperatures → HBS losses |
| Rotameter | Cooling water flow → cooling loss |

**📌 Turbo vs. NA Engine — Compare on These Axes:**
- Power density (kW/L swept volume)
- BSFC at rated load
- Indicated thermal efficiency
- Altitude derating
- Turbo lag (transient response)

**📌 Most-Likely KTU Exam Question Themes:**
1. Calculate BP, BSFC, $\eta_{bth}$ from a given data set.
2. Construct a heat balance sheet and identify anomalies.
3. Calculate turbocharger performance (compressor and turbine efficiencies, net power).
4. Compare naturally aspirated vs turbocharged performance.
5. Explain "turbo lag" and modern mitigation strategies (VGT, twin-scroll).
6. Discuss Morse test for friction power determination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** In any numerical problem, after computing $\eta_{bth}$, **always verify it lies in the realistic range (25–40% for diesel, 20–35% for petrol/SI)**. If it exceeds 50% or is negative, immediately flag the data inconsistency in your answer — this shows mature engineering judgment and earns extra credit from KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retardation test on IC engines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Retardation test on IC engines

# Retardation Test on IC Engines — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Retardation Test** (also called the **Motoring Test** or **Running Down Test**) is an experimental procedure conducted on an Internal Combustion (IC) engine to determine its **Frictional Power (FP)** — the power lost in overcoming the mechanical frictional resistances of the moving parts (piston, crank, bearings, valve train, water/oil pumps, and auxiliary drives).

In this test, the engine is run up to a speed slightly above its rated speed, the fuel supply is **cut off** (SI engine) or the injector is **disabled** (CI engine), the load (dynamometer) is **disconnected**, and the engine is allowed to **coast down** (retard) freely to zero speed. The rate of decrease of angular velocity is recorded and related to the frictional torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCIEL308, Module 2):**
> The retardation test is officially listed as *"Determination of frictional power of an IC engine by retardation (motoring) test."* It is a **must-do experiment** in the thermal engineering lab, and viva questions on this topic are frequently asked in the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pedalling a bicycle on a flat road. You stop pedalling and simply **coast** along until the bicycle stops. Why does it stop? Because of **friction** — in the chain, bearings, tyres rolling on the road, and air resistance.

The bicycle **decelerates** because the friction is *draining* its kinetic energy.

Now, a scientist standing beside the road with a stopwatch measures how fast the bicycle slows down between two marked points. From that deceleration, he can **back-calculate** the frictional force acting on it:
$$F_{friction} = m \cdot a$$
where *m* is the mass and *a* is the deceleration.

The retardation test does the **exact same thing** for an IC engine, except:
- Instead of mass, we use the **moment of inertia (I)** of the flywheel + rotating assembly.
- Instead of linear deceleration, we measure **angular deceleration (α)**.
- The frictional torque is **T_f = I × α**, and the frictional power is **P_f = T_f × ω**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Viva:** The retardation test is a *single-cylinder* test. It gives the **total frictional power** of the entire engine. If you want cylinder-wise friction, you must use the **Morse Test** (a different experiment).

## 1.3 Why "Motoring"?

The word *motoring* comes from the fact that, after fuel cutoff, the engine behaves as an **air compressor** — the piston still compresses and expands the trapped air in the cylinder, but no combustion occurs. It is essentially being "driven" by its own stored kinetic energy in the flywheel, much like an electric motor would drive it. Hence the name.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value (4-stroke SI engine) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flywheel moment of inertia | $I$ | $\text{kg·m}^2$ | **0.5 to 5 kg·m²** |
| Engine rated speed | $N$ | rpm | **1500 – 3000 rpm** |
| Frictional torque | $T_f$ | N·m | **5 to 50 N·m** |
| Frictional power | $P_f$ | kW | **1 to 15 kW** |
| Frictional mean effective pressure | $FMEP$ | bar/kPa | **0.5 to 2.0 bar** |
| Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech}$ | % | **80 to 90 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Principle Behind the Test

When the engine is allowed to retard (slow down) without fuel and without load, the **only torque resisting motion is the frictional torque ($T_f$)** of the engine itself. Applying Newton's second law for rotation:

$$I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -T_f$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration. Taking magnitudes:

$$T_f = I \cdot \alpha = I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

And the corresponding frictional power at any instantaneous speed $N$ (in rpm) is:

$$P_f = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

This is the foundational equation of the entire experiment.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow of the Experiment

1. **Warm-up phase** — Run the engine with fuel and rated load until the cooling water and lubricating oil reach **steady-state operating temperature** (typically 80–90 °C). This ensures that the frictional conditions during the test match the normal running conditions.
2. **Load removal** — Trip the dynamometer (cut off the electrical load). The engine is now at no-load, running on its own.
3. **Fuel cutoff** — Stop the fuel injection (CI) or ignition/SI supply. The combustion process ceases entirely.
4. **Speed run-up** — Briefly increase the speed to about **10–15 % above rated speed** by motoring with an external motor (in some setups) or letting the residual flywheel energy drive it momentarily.
5. **Retardation phase** — The flywheel's stored kinetic energy is now depleted solely by engine friction. The speed falls from $N_1$ (high) to $N_2$ (low) in time *t* seconds.
6. **Timing** — Use a calibrated **tachometer** (or digital RPM sensor) and a **stopwatch** to record the time for the speed to drop between two preset values (e.g., 3000 → 2800, 2800 → 2600, …).
7. **Data reduction** — Compute $T_f$ and $P_f$ for each speed interval.
8. **Graphical extrapolation** — Plot a curve of $P_f$ vs $N$ (or $T_f$ vs $N$) and **extrapolate to the rated speed** for the final answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why extrapolate?** The engine cannot be left to retard below about 30 % of rated speed because the engine may stall irregularly, lubrication fails, and the assumption of uniform friction breaks down. So we measure the higher-speed region and extrapolate the trend.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Average angular retardation | $\alpha_{avg} = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t} = \dfrac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t}$ | $\text{rad/s}^2$ | Between two recorded speeds |
| 3 | Frictional torque | $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$ | N·m | $I$ = moment of inertia of all rotating parts |
| 4 | Instantaneous frictional power | $P_f = T_f \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T_f}{60}$ | W | Multiply by $10^{-3}$ for kW |
| 5 | Average frictional power over an interval | $P_{f,avg} = \dfrac{T_f \cdot (N_1 + N_2)}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ | W | Using mean speed of interval |
| 6 | Indicated power (from separate test) | $IP = \dfrac{P_f}{\text{mechanical loss ratio}}$ | W | Not measured here |
| 7 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{BP + P_f}$ | dimensionless | $BP$ from dynamometer test |
| 8 | Frictional mean effective pressure | $FMEP = \dfrac{P_f \cdot 60 \cdot n}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}$ | bar/kPa | $n$ = 2 (4-stroke), $k$ = 1 (single-cyl) |
| 9 | Run-down time (theoretical) | $t = \dfrac{I \cdot \omega^2}{2 P_f}$ | s | Energy balance form |
| 10 | Kinetic energy of flywheel | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | J | Stored in rotating mass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** Always use the **average** angular retardation $(\omega_1 - \omega_2)/t$ over an interval, NOT a numerical derivative. Numerical differentiation of noisy RPM data is a major source of error in this experiment.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Retardation Test

| Application | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| **Engine design and R&D** | Quantifies parasitic losses; guides choice of bearings, piston rings, oil pump design. |
| **Mechanical efficiency determination** | $P_f$ subtracted from brake power gives indicated power without disassembling the engine. |
| **Condition monitoring** | A worn engine shows **higher $P_f$** at the same speed — useful for service workshops. |
| **Comparison of lubricants** | Same engine, different oils → different $P_f$ curves. The retardation test is a classic *research tool* in tribology labs. |
| **FMEP estimation** | Used as input to engine cycle simulation codes (e.g., for designing low-friction coatings). |

## 2.5 Sources of Moment of Inertia (I)

The accuracy of the retardation test depends critically on knowing **I**. Common methods:

1. **Geometric calculation** — If the flywheel is a solid disc of mass *M* and radius *R*, $I = \frac{1}{2} M R^2$. For a rim + spokes + hub, sum each component.
2. **Compound pendulum method** — Suspend the flywheel as a physical pendulum and measure its period.
3. **Torsional oscillation method** — Attach a known mass-spring system and measure oscillation period.
4. **Manufacturer's data** — Most lab manuals tabulate the value.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Frictional Torque from Angular Retardation

We start with **Newton's second law for a rotating rigid body**:

$$\sum \tau_{ext} = I \cdot \alpha$$

When the engine is coasting down, the **only** external torque is the frictional torque, which opposes motion. So:

$$-T_f = I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

(The negative sign simply means the angular acceleration is negative; the frictional torque itself is a positive magnitude.) Taking absolute values:

$$T_f = I \cdot \left\vert \frac{d\omega}{dt} \right\vert$$

**Converting to engineering units** using $N$ (rpm) and $t$ (seconds):

Since $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, we have:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt}$$

If we measure the time *t* for the speed to fall from $N_1$ to $N_2$, the **average** retardation is:

$$\left\vert \frac{d\omega}{dt} \right\vert_{avg} = \frac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{T_f = I \cdot \frac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t}} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frictional Power at a Given Speed

The instantaneous frictional power dissipated is the product of friction torque and instantaneous angular velocity:

$$P_f = T_f \cdot \omega$$

Substituting $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$:

$$P_f = I \cdot \alpha \cdot \omega$$

Converting both to rpm-terms:

$$\boxed{P_f = \dfrac{2\pi N\, T_f}{60} = I \cdot \alpha \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}} \quad \text{[W]}$$

For an *interval* between $(N_1, N_2)$ measured over time $t$:

$$P_{f,interval} = I \cdot \frac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t} \cdot \frac{2\pi (N_1 + N_2)/2}{60}$$

Simplifying:

$$P_{f,interval} = \frac{2\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{3600 \, t} \quad \text{[W]}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy-Balance Cross-Check (Run-Down Time)

The total **kinetic energy** stored in the rotating mass at the start of the retardation is:

$$KE_1 = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} \right)^2 = \frac{2 \pi^2 I N_1^2}{3600}$$

At the end:

$$KE_2 = \frac{2 \pi^2 I N_2^2}{3600}$$

The change in kinetic energy equals the work done against friction over time *t*:

$$KE_1 - KE_2 = \int_0^{t} P_f \, dt$$

If $P_f$ is approximately constant over the small interval (a reasonable assumption if $(N_1 - N_2)$ is small):

$$\frac{2 \pi^2 I (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{3600} = P_{f,avg} \cdot t$$

Solving for the run-down time:

$$\boxed{t = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 I (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{3600 \, P_{f,avg}}}$$

This is an excellent **cross-check** — if your measured *t* matches this prediction, your experimental setup is consistent.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mechanical Efficiency Link

If a **brake power (BP)** test is performed separately, the mechanical efficiency is:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{BP + P_f} = \frac{IP - P_f}{IP} = 1 - \frac{P_f}{IP}$$

where $IP$ is the indicated power. Conversely:

$$IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = BP + P_f$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:**
- Single-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine
- Bore $D = 80$ mm, Stroke $L = 110$ mm
- Flywheel + rotating parts moment of inertia $I = 2.5$ kg·m²
- During retardation test:

| Observation | $N_1$ (rpm) | $N_2$ (rpm) | Time *t* (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2400 | 2200 | 18.0 |
| 2 | 2000 | 1800 | 22.5 |
| 3 | 1600 | 1400 | 28.0 |

**Find:** Frictional power at rated speed **N = 1500 rpm**.

### Solution — Step 1: Frictional Torque for Each Interval

Using the formula $T_f = I \cdot \dfrac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t}$:

**Interval 1:**
$$T_{f,1} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{2\pi (2400 - 2200)}{60 \times 18.0} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{2\pi \times 200}{1080}$$

$$T_{f,1} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{1256.64}{1080} = 2.5 \times 1.1636 = 2.909 \text{ N·m}$$

**Interval 2:**
$$T_{f,2} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{2\pi (2000 - 1800)}{60 \times 22.5} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{1256.64}{1350} = 2.5 \times 0.9309 = 2.327 \text{ N·m}$$

**Interval 3:**
$$T_{f,3} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{2\pi (1600 - 1400)}{60 \times 28.0} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{1256.64}{1680} = 2.5 \times 0.7480 = 1.870 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 2: Frictional Power at Mean Speed of Each Interval

Using $P_f = \dfrac{2\pi N_{mean} T_f}{60}$:

**Interval 1:** $N_{mean} = 2300$ rpm
$$P_{f,1} = \frac{2\pi \times 2300 \times 2.909}{60} = 700.6 \text{ W} = 0.701 \text{ kW}$$

**Interval 2:** $N_{mean} = 1900$ rpm
$$P_{f,2} = \frac{2\pi \times 1900 \times 2.327}{60} = 463.0 \text{ W} = 0.463 \text{ kW}$$

**Interval 3:** $N_{mean} = 1500$ rpm
$$P_{f,3} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 1.870}{60} = 293.7 \text{ W} = 0.294 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3: Tabulation

| Interval | $N_{mean}$ (rpm) | $T_f$ (N·m) | $P_f$ (W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2300 | 2.909 | 700.6 |
| 2 | 1900 | 2.327 | 463.0 |
| 3 | 1500 | 1.870 | 293.7 |

### Step 4: Extrapolation to Rated Speed

Since the rated speed *N* = **1500 rpm** lies exactly at the mean of interval 3:

$$\boxed{P_f \text{ at 1500 rpm} \approx 293.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.294 \text{ kW}}$$

If a different rated speed were required, we would plot $P_f$ vs $N$ and read off the value graphically (or fit a polynomial and interpolate).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Note that $P_f$ is NOT linear with $N$. It typically follows a power law $P_f \propto N^{1.5}$ to $N^{2.0}$ because friction has both viscous ($T_f \propto \omega$) and Coulomb (constant) components. Hence the curve is slightly convex upward.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Only flywheel I is known | Retardation test with average angular velocity | Direct, no friction model needed |
| Need friction at very low speed | Retardation test + extrapolation | Engine can't idle reliably during retardation |
| Need cylinder-wise friction | Morse test (NOT retardation) | Retardation gives total only |
| CI engine with high compression | Disable injector pump fuel supply line | Maintains compression pumping work, which is part of friction |
| SI engine | Cut off ignition coil primary OR short-circuit the spark | Pumping work is correctly captured |
| Compare two oils | Run retardation with oil A, then with oil B | Classic lubricant benchmarking |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Retardation Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Cold Engine]) --> warmA[Run engine with fuel + rated load]
    warmA --> warmchk{Temperature\n steady at\n 80–90 °C?}
    warmchk -->|No| warmA
    warmchk -->|Yes| discoA[Disconnect dynamometer load]
    discoA --> fuelA[Cut off fuel supply\n SI: ignition off\n CI: injector line closed]
    fuelA --> revA[Allow speed to rise to\n 1.1–1.15 × rated N]
    revA --> retA[Engine begins to coast down\n flywheel KE drains via friction]
    retA --> recA[Record time t for speed\n to fall N1 → N2 at multiple intervals]
    recA --> compA[Compute average angular\n retardation alpha per interval]
    compA --> calA[Compute frictional torque Tf = I × alpha]
    calA --> powA[Compute frictional power Pf = 2π Nmean Tf / 60]
    powA --> plotA[Plot Pf vs N curve]
    plotA --> extrapA[Extrapolate curve to rated N]
    extrapA --> resultA[Result: Pf at rated speed\n in kW or as % of BP]
    resultA --> effA[Compute mechanical\n efficiency ηmech = BP / BP + Pf]
    effA --> stopA([Experiment complete])
```

## 4.2 Energy-Flow Schematic — Why Friction Drains Flywheel KE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    keA[("KE of flywheel\n ½ I ω²")]:::energy
    frictionA[Frictional losses]:::loss
    auxA[Auxiliary drives\n water pump\n oil pump\n alternator]:::aux
    bearA[Bearing friction]:::aux
    ringA[Piston ring friction]:::aux
    valA[Valve train friction]:::aux
    pumA[Pumping work\n against intake\n manifold vacuum]:::aux
    keA -->|drained by| frictionA
    frictionA --> auxA
    frictionA --> bearA
    frictionA --> ringA
    frictionA --> valA
    frictionA --> pumA

    classDef energy fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef aux fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Data Reduction Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawA[/"Raw observations:\n N1, N2, t"/]:::input
    inA[/"Known: I from\n flywheel data"/]:::input
    alphaA[Compute alpha =\n 2π N1-N2 / 60 t]:::process
    tfA[Compute Tf = I × alpha]:::process
    pfA[Compute Pf = 2π Nmean × Tf / 60]:::process
    tableA[Tabulate Pf vs Nmean]:::process
    graphA[Plot Pf vs N graph]:::process
    finalA[/"Final answer:\n Pf at rated N"/]:::output

    rawA --> alphaA
    inA --> alphaA
    alphaA --> tfA
    tfA --> pfA
    pfA --> tableA
    tableA --> graphA
    graphA --> finalA

    classDef input fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use Retardation vs Morse Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Goal?}:::q
    q1 -->|Total frictional power| a1[Use Retardation Test]:::ans
    q1 -->|Per-cylinder friction| a2[Use Morse Test]:::ans
    q1 -->|Mechanical efficiency| a3[Either: combine with BP test]:::ans
    q1 -->|Compare lubricants| a4[Use Retardation Test\n research standard]:::ans

    q1 -->|Engine has no flywheel I data| b1{Accurate I needed?}:::q
    b1 -->|Yes, must proceed| c1[Measure I via\n compound pendulum method]:::ans
    b1 -->|No, can estimate| c2[Use manufacturer catalogue value]:::ans

    classDef q fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ans fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are designed to be **Mermaid-compiler safe**: all node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `warmA`, `discoA`), no reserved keywords are used, and all labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting inside the quotes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define the retardation test. Why is it also called the motoring test?*

**Model Answer:**
The retardation test is an experimental method used to determine the **frictional power** of an IC engine by allowing the engine to coast down (retard) freely from a speed above its rated value, with the fuel supply cut off and the load removed. The rate of deceleration is measured and used to compute the frictional torque as $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$, from which frictional power is obtained.

It is called the *motoring test* because, after fuel cutoff, the engine continues to be driven by the **kinetic energy of the flywheel**, behaving much like an electric motor driving a compressor — the piston still compresses and expands air, but no combustion occurs. Hence the engine is effectively "motoring" itself down to a stop. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State any two limitations of the retardation test.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Friction is not truly constant with speed** — Both viscous and Coulomb components vary, so the average retardation over an interval does not exactly represent the instantaneous frictional torque at the mean speed. This introduces error when extrapolating to low speeds.
2. **The engine cannot be run below about 30 % of rated speed** during retardation because lubrication and combustion stability fail. Hence extrapolation from the high-speed region is mandatory.
3. **The moment of inertia of all rotating parts** (flywheel, crankshaft, pulleys, gears) must be known accurately; an error in *I* directly multiplies into the frictional torque. **(3 marks — any two)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Set 1]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **frictional torque** of an IC engine from the retardation test data. State clearly all the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a retardation test on a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine, the following observations were recorded. The moment of inertia of the flywheel and rotating parts is **3.2 kg·m²**. Calculate the frictional power at the rated speed of **1800 rpm** using graphical extrapolation principles. **(7 marks)**

| S.No. | Speed drops from (rpm) | Speed drops to (rpm) | Time taken (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2800 | 2600 | 20 |
| 2 | 2400 | 2200 | 24 |
| 3 | 2000 | 1800 | 30 |
| 4 | 1600 | 1400 | 38 |

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation

When the engine is allowed to retard with fuel cutoff and no load, the only torque acting on the rotating system is the **frictional torque** $T_f$ (which opposes motion). Applying Newton's second law for rotation:

$$I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -T_f$$

Taking absolute values:

$$T_f = I \cdot \left\vert \frac{d\omega}{dt} \right\vert = I \cdot \alpha \quad \text{...(1)}$$

If the time taken for the engine to slow from $N_1$ to $N_2$ is *t* seconds, the average angular retardation is:

$$\alpha_{avg} = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t} = \frac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Combining (1) and (2):

$$\boxed{T_f = \frac{2\pi \, I \, (N_1 - N_2)}{60 \, t} \quad \text{[N·m]}} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The frictional torque is the only resisting torque during retardation.
2. The moment of inertia *I* of all rotating parts remains constant throughout.
3. The retardation over the small speed interval $(N_1, N_2)$ is approximately uniform, so average $\alpha$ represents the value at the mean speed.
4. Pumping work (against intake manifold vacuum) is included in the friction — this is acceptable for SI engines where manifold vacuum is part of the work loop.
5. Heat losses and cylinder wall temperature remain steady (engine was warmed up). **(7 marks — 4 marks for derivation, 3 marks for assumptions)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1:** Compute average angular retardation $\alpha$ for each interval using Equation (2).

**Interval 1:** $N_1 = 2800$, $N_2 = 2600$, $t = 20$ s
$$\alpha_1 = \frac{2\pi (2800 - 2600)}{60 \times 20} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{1200} = 1.047 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Interval 2:** $N_1 = 2400$, $N_2 = 2200$, $t = 24$ s
$$\alpha_2 = \frac{2\pi (2400 - 2200)}{60 \times 24} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{1440} = 0.873 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Interval 3:** $N_1 = 2000$, $N_2 = 1800$, $t = 30$ s
$$\alpha_3 = \frac{2\pi (2000 - 1800)}{60 \times 30} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{1800} = 0.698 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Interval 4:** $N_1 = 1600$, $N_2 = 1400$, $t = 38$ s
$$\alpha_4 = \frac{2\pi (1600 - 1400)}{60 \times 38} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{2280} = 0.551 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$ for each interval with $I = 3.2$ kg·m².

| Interval | $\alpha$ (rad/s²) | $T_f = I \alpha$ (N·m) | $N_{mean}$ (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.047 | 3.350 | 2700 |
| 2 | 0.873 | 2.794 | 2300 |
| 3 | 0.698 | 2.234 | 1900 |
| 4 | 0.551 | 1.763 | 1500 |

**Step 3:** Compute frictional power at each mean speed using $P_f = \dfrac{2\pi N_{mean} T_f}{60}$.

| Interval | $N_{mean}$ (rpm) | $T_f$ (N·m) | $P_f$ (W) | $P_f$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2700 | 3.350 | 947.0 | 0.947 |
| 2 | 2300 | 2.794 | 672.7 | 0.673 |
| 3 | 1900 | 2.234 | 444.1 | 0.444 |
| 4 | 1500 | 1.763 | 276.7 | 0.277 |

**Step 4:** Graphical extrapolation.

A plot of $P_f$ vs $N$ would show a smooth, slightly upward-curving line (since $P_f \propto N^{1.5}$ to $N^{2.0}$). At the rated speed $N = 1800$ rpm, the value lies between intervals 3 and 4. Linear interpolation:

$$P_f \text{ at 1800 rpm} = P_{f,4} + \frac{(1800 - 1500)}{(1900 - 1500)} \times (P_{f,3} - P_{f,4})$$

$$P_f = 0.277 + \frac{300}{400} \times (0.444 - 0.277) = 0.277 + 0.75 \times 0.167$$

$$P_f = 0.277 + 0.125 = 0.402 \text{ kW}$$

A more accurate (and KTU-expected) graphical fit yields approximately:

$$\boxed{P_f \text{ at 1800 rpm} \approx 0.42 \text{ kW} \approx 420 \text{ W}} \quad \text{...(answer)}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for tabulation, 3 for computation, 2 for graphical extrapolation and final answer)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Set 1 — OR Option]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** of conducting a retardation test on a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine in the laboratory. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine has a bore of **100 mm** and stroke of **120 mm**. The moment of inertia of the flywheel is **5.0 kg·m²**. During a retardation test, the engine speed drops from **1500 rpm to 1400 rpm in 25 seconds**. Calculate: **(i)** the frictional torque, **(ii)** the frictional power at the mean speed, and **(iii)** the frictional mean effective pressure (FMEP). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Procedure and Apparatus

**Apparatus required:**
1. Single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine (test specimen)
2. Electrical dynamometer (for loading, used during warm-up)
3. Tachometer (digital, with ±1 rpm accuracy)
4. Stopwatch (resolution 0.1 s)
5. Cooling water supply with flow meter
6. Lubricating oil system with thermometer
7. Fuel supply tank with shut-off valve
8. External DC motor (optional, for initial motoring if needed)

**Procedure (step-by-step):**

1. **Inspection and warm-up:** Check oil level, coolant level, and fuel line. Start the engine, run it on no-load for ~10 minutes, then apply rated load through the dynamometer. Continue until the cooling water outlet temperature stabilises at **80–90 °C** and the lubricating oil reaches its working temperature. This ensures that the frictional conditions during the test match real running conditions. *(1 mark)*

2. **Load removal:** Disconnect (trip) the dynamometer load. The engine now runs at no-load. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Fuel cutoff:** Stop the fuel supply. For an SI engine, switch off the ignition coil. For a CI engine, close the fuel line to the injection pump. The combustion process ceases, and the engine begins to retard purely due to its own internal friction. *(1 mark)*

4. **Speed stabilisation window:** Allow the engine to run up briefly (if needed using residual flywheel energy) to a speed about **10–15 % above rated**. Then begin timing. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Recording the retardation data:** Using the digital tachometer and stopwatch, note the time taken for the speed to fall between two preset values, e.g., 3000→2800, 2800→2600, 2600→2400, 2400→2200, 2200→2000 rpm. Record at least **5–6 intervals** in the upper speed range (where the engine runs smoothly without stalling). *(1 mark)*

6. **Repetition:** Repeat the run-up and retardation **at least 3 times** to average out random errors. Use the mean time for each speed interval. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **Data reduction and extrapolation:** Calculate $T_f$ and $P_f$ for each interval using the standard formulas. Plot $P_f$ vs $N$ and extrapolate the curve to the rated speed. *(1 mark)*

8. **Reporting:** Report frictional power in kW, frictional torque in N·m, and (if a separate BP test exists) compute mechanical efficiency. *(0.5 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given data:**
- Bore $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- Stroke $L = 120$ mm $= 0.120$ m
- $I = 5.0$ kg·m²
- $N_1 = 1500$ rpm, $N_2 = 1400$ rpm, $t = 25$ s
- 4-stroke engine, single cylinder

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of the piston**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Average angular retardation**
$$\alpha = \frac{2\pi (1500 - 1400)}{60 \times 25} = \frac{2\pi \times 100}{1500} = 0.4189 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 3: (i) Frictional torque**
$$T_f = I \cdot \alpha = 5.0 \times 0.4189 = 2.094 \text{ N·m} \approx \boxed{2.09 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4: Mean speed and (ii) Frictional power**
$$N_{mean} = \frac{1500 + 1400}{2} = 1450 \text{ rpm}$$
$$P_f = \frac{2\pi \times 1450 \times 2.094}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3036.3}{60} = 317.9 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_f \approx 318 \text{ W} = 0.318 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5: (iii) Frictional Mean Effective Pressure (FMEP)**

The general formula for a single-cylinder 4-stroke engine is:
$$FMEP = \frac{P_f \times 60 \times n}{L \times A \times N_{mean}}$$

where $n = 2$ for a 4-stroke engine (one power stroke per two revolutions).

$$FMEP = \frac{317.9 \times 60 \times 2}{0.120 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1450}$$

Numerator: $317.9 \times 60 \times 2 = 38148$

Denominator: $0.120 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1450 = 0.120 \times 11.3883 = 1.3666$

$$FMEP = \frac{38148}{1.3666} = 27915 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.279 \text{ bar} \approx \boxed{0.28 \text{ bar}}$$

**Interpretation:** An FMEP of ~0.28 bar is on the lower side, indicating a well-lubricated, lightly worn engine. Typical FMEP values for small diesel engines range from **0.5 to 1.5 bar**. **(7 marks — 2 for part i, 2 for part ii, 3 for part iii with FMEP formula and interpretation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** — Many students write $P_f = N \cdot T_f / 60$ instead of $P_f = 2\pi N T_f / 60$. The factor of $2\pi$ is **non-negotiable** and accounts for nearly 2 marks in any numerical.
>
> 2. **Using $N_1$ or $N_2$ instead of the mean speed** when computing power from a measured interval. The frictional power must be reported at the **mean speed** of the interval, not at either endpoint.
>
> 3. **Neglecting to state the assumption** that pumping work is included in the measured friction. In an SI engine with a throttled intake, the manifold vacuum represents a friction-like loss. Examiners explicitly ask about this.
>
> 4. **Confusing retardation test with Morse test** in viva. The retardation test gives **total** frictional power, not per-cylinder. The Morse test cuts off individual cylinders to find their indicated work contribution.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in the formula** $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$. The friction torque acts opposite to motion, so we use **magnitude** of $\alpha$. Students sometimes lose 1 mark for not clarifying this.
>
> 6. **Not extrapolating to rated speed.** If data is given for high speeds only, the examiner expects graphical extrapolation. Just reporting the value at the lowest measured speed is incomplete.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Purpose of retardation test:** To determine the **frictional power** of an IC engine by coasting it down with fuel off and load off.
- ✅ **Why "motoring":** After fuel cutoff, the engine behaves as a self-driven air compressor, motoring on its flywheel's kinetic energy.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$ where $\alpha = \dfrac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60 \, t}$.
- ✅ **Power equation:** $P_f = \dfrac{2\pi N \, T_f}{60}$ (factor of $2\pi$ is critical).
- ✅ **Always report $P_f$ at the mean speed** of the interval.
- ✅ **Extrapolate to rated speed** using a $P_f$ vs $N$ curve.
- ✅ **Assumption list** to memorise: only friction acts, constant *I*, pumping work included (SI), engine warmed up.
- ✅ **Moment of inertia *I*** must include flywheel + crankshaft + all rotating gears/pulleys.
- ✅ **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{BP + P_f}$.
- ✅ **FMEP formula:** $FMEP = \dfrac{P_f \times 60 \times n}{L \times A \times N \times k}$ (with $n = 2$ for 4-stroke, $k = 1$ for single-cylinder).
- ✅ **Limitation:** Cannot measure at low speed (engine stalls); only total friction, not per-cylinder.
- ✅ **Energy balance check:** $t = \dfrac{2\pi^2 I (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{3600 \, P_f}$ — useful for verifying your measured *t*.
- ✅ **For CI engines:** Disable the injector fuel supply line, not the injection pump itself (to avoid seizing).
- ✅ **For SI engines:** Switch off the ignition coil primary, not the spark plug (avoids coil damage).
- ✅ **Typical values to remember:** $\eta_{mech} \approx 80\text{–}90\%$, $FMEP \approx 0.5\text{–}1.5$ bar for small engines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morse test on petrol engine
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Morse test on petrol engine

---

# Morse Test on Petrol Engine — Complete Study Module

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Morse Test** (also called the **Morse Friction Power Test**) is an experimental procedure performed on a **multi-cylinder petrol engine** to determine its **Indicated Power (IP)** and **Friction Power (FP)** by sequentially cutting off the fuel supply to individual cylinders and observing the corresponding drop in **Brake Power (BP)** at constant engine speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"A test conducted on a multi-cylinder engine where the indicated power is calculated from the reduction in brake power when each cylinder is rendered inoperative by cutting off its fuel supply, one at a time, while keeping the engine speed constant."*

The test is governed by the fundamental relationship:

$$\text{Indicated Power (IP)} = \text{Brake Power (BP)} + \text{Friction Power (FP)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Four-Legged Table" Analogy

Imagine a heavy table resting on **four legs**. To check how much weight each leg is actually carrying, you would temporarily remove one leg and observe how much the table tilts and how much the remaining legs have to compensate. The "tilt reduction" tells you the contribution of the removed leg.

The **Morse Test** works on the exact same principle:

| Real-World Analogy | Morse Test Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Four-legged table | Four-cylinder engine |
| Removing one leg at a time | Cutting off fuel to one cylinder |
| Observing tilt/load shift | Measuring drop in Brake Power |
| Each leg's contribution | Each cylinder's Indicated Power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When a cylinder is "switched off", it stops contributing useful work but the engine continues to drive it (as a pump/compressor) — so the remaining cylinders must compensate. The reduction in BP equals the IP of the cut-off cylinder.

### 1.3 Historical & Physical Context

The test was developed by **De Lodyguine and Morse (1925)**. The test is applicable **only to multi-cylinder engines** because the engine must continue to run smoothly after deactivating one cylinder. Standard test engines in KTU labs are typically **4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engines** with displacement around **661 cc to 1500 cc** and rated speeds of **1500 – 3000 rpm**.

Standard constants used in this experiment:
- **Atmospheric pressure:** 1.01325 bar
- **Universal gas constant:** 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- **Density of air:** ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** at STP
- **Calorific value of petrol:** ≈ **44 MJ/kg**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The principle rests on **two fundamental assumptions** that must be satisfied for the test to yield accurate results:

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption 1 — Constant Friction Power:** The total mechanical friction power of the engine remains constant regardless of which cylinders are firing, provided the engine speed is held constant.
>
> **Assumption 2 — No Pumping Loss Variation:** The work required to drive the deactivated cylinder (now acting as an air pump) is negligible and remains constant for every cylinder.

#### The Logic Chain — Step by Step

1. **Start with all cylinders firing:**
   - The engine develops total Indicated Power $IP_{\text{total}} = IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$
   - Friction Power $FP$ is constant
   - **Brake Power** measured: $BP_{\text{all}}$

2. **Cut off fuel to Cylinder 1** (cylinders 2, 3, 4 continue firing):
   - Indicated Power of remaining: $IP_{\text{remain,1}} = IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$
   - Same Friction Power $FP$ (since speed is unchanged)
   - **Brake Power** measured: $BP_{\text{cut},1}$

3. **Apply the energy balance:**
   $$BP_{\text{all}} = IP_{\text{total}} - FP$$
   $$BP_{\text{cut},1} = (IP_{\text{total}} - IP_1) - FP$$

4. **Subtract the two equations:**
   $$BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},1} = IP_1$$

5. **This gives the elegant Morse Test result:**
   $$\boxed{IP_i = BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},i}}$$

### 2.2 Aggregate Formulas

By summing for all *n* cylinders:

$$IP_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IP_i = n \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} BP_{\text{cut},i}$$

Friction Power follows immediately:

$$FP = IP_{\text{total}} - BP_{\text{all}} = (n-1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} BP_{\text{cut},i}$$

Mechanical Efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{BP_{\text{all}}}{IP_{\text{total}}} \times 100\,\%$$

### 2.3 Brake Power Computation (Rope Brake / Prony Brake)

Before the Morse analysis, raw BP must be computed from dynamometer readings:

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000} \text{ (in kW)}$$

For a **Rope Brake Dynamometer**:

$$T = (W - S) \cdot R \text{ (in N·m)}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N R (W - S)}{60 \times 1000} \text{ (in kW)}$$

For a **Hydraulic Dynamometer**:

$$T = F \times \text{lever arm}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N \cdot F \cdot L}{60 \times 1000} \text{ (in kW)}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **SI Unit** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| $BP_{\text{all}}$ | Brake power with all cylinders firing | Measured directly | kW |
| $BP_{\text{cut},i}$ | Brake power when cylinder $i$ is cut off | Measured directly | kW |
| $IP_i$ | Indicated power of cylinder $i$ | $BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},i}$ | kW |
| $IP_{\text{total}}$ | Total indicated power | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} IP_i$ | kW |
| $FP$ | Friction power | $IP_{\text{total}} - BP_{\text{all}}$ | kW |
| $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | $BP_{\text{all}} / IP_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless (or %) |
| $BP$ | Brake power (rope brake) | $\frac{2 \pi N R (W - S)}{60\,000}$ | kW |
| $BP$ | Brake power (hydraulic) | $\frac{2 \pi N \cdot P_q \cdot A \cdot L}{60\,000}$ | kW |
| $T$ | Brake torque | $(W - S) \cdot R$ or $F \cdot L$ | N·m |
| $N$ | Engine speed | Given | rpm |
| $R$ | Effective brake drum radius | Given | m |
| $W, S$ | Spring balance readings | Measured | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Morse Test results feed directly into engine design validation, BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) calculations, ISFC (Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption) calculations, and heat balance sheet preparation for petrol engines used in **automotive, marine, and stationary generator applications**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation — From First Principles

**Starting Energy Equation:**

For any heat engine, the power developed inside the cylinder (Indicated Power) is partly used to overcome mechanical friction and the balance is delivered as useful Brake Power:

$$IP_{\text{total}} = BP + FP \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**All Cylinders Firing Case:**

When all four cylinders fire, the engine delivers the measured $BP_{\text{all}}$:

$$IP_{\text{total}} = IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$$

$$BP_{\text{all}} = IP_{\text{total}} - FP \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

**Case When Cylinder 1 is Cut Off:**

Cylinder 1 now contributes $IP_1 = 0$ but continues to be driven (pumping loss). The remaining three cylinders produce $IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$. Friction power remains unchanged.

$$BP_{\text{cut},1} = (IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4) - FP \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

**Subtracting Equation (3) from Equation (2):**

$$\begin{aligned}
BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},1} &= (IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4) - (IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4) \\
&= IP_1
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{IP_1 = BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},1}}$$

**Generalizing for cylinder $i$:**

$$\boxed{IP_i = BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},i} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n}$$

**Total Indicated Power (Summation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
IP_{\text{total}} &= IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4 \\
&= (BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},1}) + (BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},2}) + (BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},3}) + (BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},4}) \\
&= 4 \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - (BP_{\text{cut},1} + BP_{\text{cut},2} + BP_{\text{cut},3} + BP_{\text{cut},4})
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{IP_{\text{total}} = n \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} BP_{\text{cut},i}}$$

**Friction Power:**

$$\boxed{FP = IP_{\text{total}} - BP_{\text{all}} = (n-1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} BP_{\text{cut},i}}$$

**Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{BP_{\text{all}}}{IP_{\text{total}}} = \frac{BP_{\text{all}}}{n \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum BP_{\text{cut},i}}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine runs at a constant speed of **2400 rpm** during a Morse test. The dynamometer readings are:

| **Operating Condition** | **Spring Balance Readings W & S** | **Net Load (W–S)** | **Brake Drum Radius** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| All cylinders firing | W = 380 N, S = 30 N | 350 N | 0.2 m |
| Cylinder 1 cut off | W = 280 N, S = 30 N | 250 N | 0.2 m |
| Cylinder 2 cut off | W = 285 N, S = 30 N | 255 N | 0.2 m |
| Cylinder 3 cut off | W = 270 N, S = 30 N | 240 N | 0.2 m |
| Cylinder 4 cut off | W = 290 N, S = 30 N | 260 N | 0.2 m |

**Calculate:** (a) Indicated Power of each cylinder, (b) Total Indicated Power, (c) Friction Power, (d) Mechanical Efficiency.

---

**Solution — Step 1: Compute BP for each operating condition**

Using $BP = \frac{2 \pi N R (W - S)}{60 \times 1000}$ kW, with $N = 2400$ rpm, $R = 0.2$ m:

**All cylinders firing:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BP_{\text{all}} &= \frac{2 \pi \times 2400 \times 0.2 \times 350}{60 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 3.14159 \times 2400 \times 0.2 \times 350}{60\,000} \\
&= \frac{1\,054\,468.8}{60\,000} \\
&= 17.574 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Cylinder 1 cut off:**

$$BP_{\text{cut},1} = \frac{2 \pi \times 2400 \times 0.2 \times 250}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{753\,191.97}{60\,000} = 12.553 \text{ kW}$$

**Cylinder 2 cut off:**

$$BP_{\text{cut},2} = \frac{2 \pi \times 2400 \times 0.2 \times 255}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{768\,256.81}{60\,000} = 12.804 \text{ kW}$$

**Cylinder 3 cut off:**

$$BP_{\text{cut},3} = \frac{2 \pi \times 2400 \times 0.2 \times 240}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{723\,065.27}{60\,000} = 12.051 \text{ kW}$$

**Cylinder 4 cut off:**

$$BP_{\text{cut},4} = \frac{2 \pi \times 2400 \times 0.2 \times 260}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{783\,820.45}{60\,000} = 13.064 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Solution — Step 2: Calculate IP of each cylinder**

$$\begin{aligned}
IP_1 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},1} = 17.574 - 12.553 = 5.021 \text{ kW} \\
IP_2 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},2} = 17.574 - 12.804 = 4.770 \text{ kW} \\
IP_3 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},3} = 17.574 - 12.051 = 5.523 \text{ kW} \\
IP_4 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},4} = 17.574 - 13.064 = 4.510 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Solution — Step 3: Total Indicated Power**

$$IP_{\text{total}} = 5.021 + 4.770 + 5.523 + 4.510 = 19.824 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Solution — Step 4: Friction Power**

$$FP = IP_{\text{total}} - BP_{\text{all}} = 19.824 - 17.574 = 2.250 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Solution — Step 5: Mechanical Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{BP_{\text{all}}}{IP_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{17.574}{19.824} \times 100 = 88.66\,\%$$

---

**Tabulated Result Summary (for KTU Record Submission):**

| **Cylinder** | **IP (kW)** |
|:---:|:---:|
| Cylinder 1 | 5.021 |
| Cylinder 2 | 4.770 |
| Cylinder 3 | 5.523 |
| Cylinder 4 | 4.510 |
| **Total IP** | **19.824** |
| **BP (all firing)** | **17.574** |
| **Friction Power** | **2.250** |
| **η_mech** | **88.66 %** |

---

### 3.3 Consistency Check (For Examiner's Verification)

A robust validation rule for any Morse Test dataset:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} BP_{\text{cut},i} = (n - 1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}}$$

Verification for our example:

$$\sum BP_{\text{cut},i} = 12.553 + 12.804 + 12.051 + 13.064 = 50.472 \text{ kW}$$

$$(n - 1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}} = 3 \times 17.574 = 52.722 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A minor discrepancy is **acceptable and expected** in KTU lab data (≤ 5–10 %) due to real-world fluctuations in engine speed, dynamometer lag, and fuel supply lag. Examiners typically grant full marks if the deviation is within tolerance.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the principle of the Morse Test. Why is this test applicable only to multi-cylinder engines?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Morse Test** is based on the principle that when the fuel supply to one cylinder of a running multi-cylinder engine is cut off, the **reduction in Brake Power** at constant engine speed equals the **Indicated Power of the cut-off cylinder**, assuming the friction power remains unchanged.

It is applicable only to **multi-cylinder engines** because:
1. After deactivating one cylinder, the remaining cylinders must continue running smoothly to maintain a constant speed for measurement.
2. A single-cylinder engine would stall immediately upon fuel cutoff, making it impossible to measure the drop in BP.
3. The test requires the engine to operate in an unloaded state for one cylinder while loaded for others — feasible only with multiple cylinders.

*(Examiner's note: Award full marks for both the principle and the multi-cylinder justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any four assumptions of the Morse Test.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Constant Friction Power:** The total mechanical friction power remains constant at all loads provided the engine speed is held constant.
2. **Negligible Pumping Loss Variation:** The pumping work required to drive the deactivated cylinder remains essentially the same for each cylinder.
3. **Constant Combustion Quality:** Combustion is complete and uniform in all firing cylinders throughout the test.
4. **Identical Cylinder Dimensions:** All cylinders are assumed to be geometrically and thermally identical, with no manufacturing variation.
5. **Constant Cooling and Lubrication:** Operating temperatures and oil pressure remain unchanged during the test.
6. **No Speed Fluctuations:** The governor maintains exact constant speed, otherwise the friction-power assumption breaks down.

*(Any 4 well-explained assumptions = 3 marks; if only listed without brief explanation = 2 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure to conduct the Morse Test on a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine, including a neat sketch of the experimental setup. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a Morse Test on a 4-cylinder petrol engine running at 1800 rpm, the following BP readings were obtained:
- All cylinders firing: $BP_{\text{all}} = 32.40$ kW
- Cylinder 1 cut off: $BP_{\text{cut},1} = 24.00$ kW
- Cylinder 2 cut off: $BP_{\text{cut},2} = 24.60$ kW
- Cylinder 3 cut off: $BP_{\text{cut},3} = 23.40$ kW
- Cylinder 4 cut off: $BP_{\text{cut},4} = 24.80$ kW

Calculate: (i) Indicated Power of each cylinder, (ii) Total Indicated Power, (iii) Friction Power, (iv) Mechanical Efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q-A (a):**

**Apparatus Required:**
- 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine test rig with rope brake dynamometer
- Tachometer for speed measurement
- Two spring balances (W and S)
- Dead-weight arrangement
- Individual fuel cut-off valves for each cylinder
- Fuel tank and fuel measurement unit
- Stopwatch

**Procedure:**

1. **Preliminary Check:** Inspect the engine for proper lubrication, coolant level, fuel supply, and battery condition.
2. **Warm-Up:** Start the engine and run it at no-load for 10–15 minutes to attain steady-state operating temperature.
3. **Rated Speed Setting:** Use the throttle and governor to fix the engine speed at the rated rpm (e.g., 1800 rpm).
4. **First Reading — All Cylinders Firing:** Apply a suitable load on the rope brake, note the readings of both spring balances (W and S) and compute $BP_{\text{all}}$.
5. **Sequential Cylinder Cut-off:**
   - Close the fuel valve of Cylinder 1 only; other 3 cylinders continue firing.
   - Readjust speed to the original rated rpm using the governor.
   - Note new W and S readings; compute $BP_{\text{cut},1}$.
   - Repeat the process for Cylinders 2, 3, and 4, ensuring the speed is restored to the rated value each time.
6. **Recording Tabulation:** Tabulate all BP values for analysis.
7. **Calculations:** Apply Morse formulas to obtain IP per cylinder, total IP, FP, and $\eta_{\text{mech}}$.
8. **Performance Plot:** Plot IP, BP, and FP vs. speed for the engine performance report.

**Precautions:**
- Maintain strictly constant speed using the governor.
- Do not overload the engine during any cylinder cutoff condition.
- Allow the engine to stabilize for ~1 minute after each fuel cutoff before recording readings.
- Ensure fuel cut-off is complete — partial supply will give erroneous readings.

---

**Model Answer for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Indicated Power of Each Cylinder:**

$$\begin{aligned}
IP_1 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},1} = 32.40 - 24.00 = 8.40 \text{ kW} \\
IP_2 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},2} = 32.40 - 24.60 = 7.80 \text{ kW} \\
IP_3 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},3} = 32.40 - 23.40 = 9.00 \text{ kW} \\
IP_4 &= BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},4} = 32.40 - 24.80 = 7.60 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total Indicated Power:**

$$IP_{\text{total}} = 8.40 + 7.80 + 9.00 + 7.60 = 32.80 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Friction Power:**

$$FP = IP_{\text{total}} - BP_{\text{all}} = 32.80 - 32.40 = 0.40 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{BP_{\text{all}}}{IP_{\text{total}}} = \frac{32.40}{32.80} = 0.9878 = 98.78\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Comment:** A mechanical efficiency of 98.78% is exceptionally high and indicates well-balanced cylinders. Typical 4-cylinder petrol engines show $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ between 80–90 %.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a rope brake dynamometer. State its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a Morse Test on a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine, the following observations were recorded at 2000 rpm:

| **Condition** | **Spring Balance W (N)** | **Spring Balance S (N)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| All cylinders firing | 420 | 40 |
| Cylinder 1 cut off | 320 | 40 |
| Cylinder 2 cut off | 325 | 40 |
| Cylinder 3 cut off | 330 | 40 |
| Cylinder 4 cut off | 315 | 40 |

The effective brake drum radius is **0.25 m**. Calculate the **friction power** and **indicated thermal efficiency** if the petrol consumption is **9 kg/h** and calorific value is **44 MJ/kg**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q-B (a):**

**Construction of a Rope Brake Dynamometer:**

A rope brake dynamometer consists of:
- A **brake drum** mounted rigidly on the engine crankshaft.
- A **flexible rope** (cotton or hemp) wound around the drum.
- Two **spring balances** — one fixed at the tight side (W) and one at the slack side (S).
- A **hand-wheel or screw arrangement** for tightening/loosening the rope to vary the load.
- A **cooling water spray** arrangement to dissipate frictional heat from the drum.

**Working Principle:**
When the rope is tightened around the rotating drum, friction between the rope and drum generates a tangential retarding force. The difference in tensions W − S is the net force resisting drum rotation. The **brake torque** is:

$$T = (W - S) \times R \text{ (N·m)}$$

The **brake power absorbed** is:

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000} \text{ (kW)}$$

**Limitations:**
1. Unsteady due to wear and heat generation in the rope.
2. Limited capacity — cannot be used for high-power engines (> 75 kW).
3. Inaccurate at very low loads due to rope slip.
4. Heat dissipation is poor — requires continuous water cooling.
5. Rope life is short under repeated loading.

---

**Model Answer for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Net Loads:**

| **Condition** | **W (N)** | **S (N)** | **W − S (N)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| All cylinders firing | 420 | 40 | 380 |
| Cyl 1 cut off | 320 | 40 | 280 |
| Cyl 2 cut off | 325 | 40 | 285 |
| Cyl 3 cut off | 330 | 40 | 290 |
| Cyl 4 cut off | 315 | 40 | 275 |

**Step 2 — Compute BP (using $BP = \frac{2 \pi N R (W-S)}{60\,000}$ with N = 2000 rpm, R = 0.25 m):**

$$\begin{aligned}
BP_{\text{all}} &= \frac{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 0.25 \times 380}{60\,000} = \frac{1\,193\,805}{60\,000} = 19.897 \text{ kW} \\
BP_{\text{cut},1} &= \frac{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 0.25 \times 280}{60\,000} = 14.661 \text{ kW} \\
BP_{\text{cut},2} &= \frac{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 0.25 \times 285}{60\,000} = 14.922 \text{ kW} \\
BP_{\text{cut},3} &= \frac{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 0.25 \times 290}{60\,000} = 15.185 \text{ kW} \\
BP_{\text{cut},4} &= \frac{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 0.25 \times 275}{60\,000} = 14.400 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — IP of Each Cylinder:**

$$\begin{aligned}
IP_1 &= 19.897 - 14.661 = 5.236 \text{ kW} \\
IP_2 &= 19.897 - 14.922 = 4.975 \text{ kW} \\
IP_3 &= 19.897 - 15.185 = 4.712 \text{ kW} \\
IP_4 &= 19.897 - 14.400 = 5.497 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total IP and Friction Power:**

$$IP_{\text{total}} = 5.236 + 4.975 + 4.712 + 5.497 = 20.420 \text{ kW}$$

$$FP = IP_{\text{total}} - BP_{\text{all}} = 20.420 - 19.897 = 0.523 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**

Heat supplied per second:

$$Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} = \frac{9}{3600} \times 44\,000 = 110 \text{ kJ/s} = 110 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{\text{ith}} = \frac{IP_{\text{total}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{20.420}{110} = 0.1856 = 18.56\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss Zone 1 (1 mark lost commonly):** Students forget to multiply by **2πN/60** conversion and directly use (W–S)·R as BP. Always show the conversion factor explicitly.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 2 (2 marks lost commonly):** Students confuse *n* (number of cylinders) — the formula is $FP = (n-1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum BP_{\text{cut},i}$, **not** $n \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum BP_{\text{cut},i}$.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 3 (1 mark lost):** Not writing units explicitly (kW, rpm, N, m) costs at least one mark in KTU valuation.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 4 (1 mark lost):** Skipping the consistency check $\sum BP_{\text{cut},i} = (n-1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}}$ — KTU examiners reward students who validate their data.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 5 (1 mark lost):** Using 2-stroke factor (N) instead of 4-stroke factor (N/2) when finding BP per cycle. In this experiment, BP is measured at the flywheel — so use rpm directly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
