> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Morse Test definition:** A test on multi-cylinder engines to find IP by sequential fuel cutoff.
- ✅ **Core Formula 1:** $IP_i = BP_{\text{all}} - BP_{\text{cut},i}$ (gold standard for KTU problems).
- ✅ **Core Formula 2:** $IP_{\text{total}} = n \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} BP_{\text{cut},i}$.
- ✅ **Core Formula 3:** $FP = (n-1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} BP_{\text{cut},i}$.
- ✅ **Core Formula 4:** $\eta_{\text{mech}} = BP_{\text{all}} / IP_{\text{total}}$.
- ✅ **BP Rope Brake:** $BP = \frac{2 \pi N R (W - S)}{60\,000}$ kW (must be in SI units).
- ✅ **Key Assumption:** Friction power is **constant** at constant speed — without this, Morse equations fail.
- ✅ **Multi-cylinder only:** Single-cylinder engines cannot undergo this test.
- ✅ **Consistency Check:** $\sum BP_{\text{cut},i} \approx (n-1) \cdot BP_{\text{all}}$ — used to validate experimental data.
- ✅ **Limitations:** (1) Friction power is not strictly constant; (2) Pumping loss varies between cylinders; (3) Heat balance shifts when one cylinder is off — all affect accuracy.
- ✅ **Examiner pet topic:** Almost every KTU Thermal Lab paper since 2020 has had at least one Morse Test problem in Part B (14 marks). Memorize the derivation, not just the formula.
- ✅ **Practical tip:** During the actual lab exam, always **record speed, fuel flow rate, and ambient temperature** along with BP — examiners award marks for these.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat balance test on petrol/diesel engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Heat balance test on petrol/diesel engines ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) The **Heat Balance Test** is a standard laboratory procedure conducted on an Internal Combustion (I.C.) engine to quantitatively account for the distribution of total chemical energy supplied by the fuel into various useful and loss components. The test mathematically closes the First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation) applied to a steady-flow engine system. Mathematically, it is the reconciliation between the **Heat Supplied** (chemical energy released by combustion of fuel) and the **Heat Utilized + Heat Rejected** (work output + cooling water loss + exhaust gas loss + unaccounted losses). $$\boxed{Q_{\text{supplied}} = Q_{\text{work}} + Q_{\text{water}} + Q_{\text{exhaust}} + Q_{\text{unaccounted}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The heat balance test is the *most frequently repeated experiment* in KTU Thermal Engineering Lab ESE. Students must know every heat term, its sign convention, and its percentage share by heart. ### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Water Bucket Analogy" Imagine you pour **10 litres of water** (fuel energy) into a complex bucket system (the engine) containing several smaller buckets: - One bucket captures useful spinning motion (work output). - One bucket is connected to a radiator that warms up cooling water (heat to jacket water). - One bucket is the tailpipe that blows hot gases (exhaust heat). - Some water splashes on the floor, drips through cracks, or evaporates unseen (radiation, friction, incomplete combustion losses). The **Heat Balance Test** simply measures how much water went into *each* bucket. If you add up all the buckets and the spills, the total must equal exactly 10 litres — this is the principle of energy conservation. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Required for this Experiment:** > - Calorific Value of Petrol (Gasoline): approximately **44,000 kJ/kg** > - Calorific Value of Diesel: approximately **43,000 kJ/kg** > - Specific heat of water (Cp_w): **4.186 kJ/kg·K** > - Specific heat of exhaust gas (Cp_g): **1.005 kJ/kg·K** (≈ that of air) > - Mechanical Equivalent of Heat: **1 kcal = 4.186 kJ** (or **1 kJ = 0.2388 kcal**) > - Density of water: **1000 kg/m³** ### 1.3 Why is this Test Important in Engineering? A heat balance sheet reveals the **thermal efficiency profile** of an engine. Without it, an engineer cannot identify whether the engine is losing too much heat through the cooling system (poor combustion chamber design), exhaust (poor scavenging), or unaccounted losses (radiation/friction/mechanical defects). It is the cornerstone of **engine performance mapping**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Breakdown of the Energy Flow in an Engine The chemical energy of the fuel undergoes a series of transformations, and at the steady state, the input equals the sum of outputs. Each output term has a specific physical origin and a specific calculation method. **Step-by-step logic of energy flow:** 1. **Fuel is burned** in the combustion chamber → chemical energy converts to high-temperature gas energy. The total heat *supplied* per second is: $Q_s = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}$ (in kJ/s or kW) 2. **Part of this energy pushes the piston** → converted to Brake Power (BP) at the crankshaft. This is the *useful output*. $Q_{\text{bp}} = \text{BP} \times 1$ (when BP is in kW, heat equivalent is numerically the same in kJ/s). 3. **A portion is absorbed by the cylinder walls** → removed by circulating cooling water. The water enters cold and exits hot, carrying away energy. $Q_w = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p_w} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})$ 4. **Hot exhaust gases leave the cylinder** carrying both sensible enthalpy and the chemical enthalpy of any unburned fuel. $Q_{\text{ex}} = \dot{m}_{\text{ex}} \cdot C_{p_{\text{ex}}} \cdot (T_{\text{ex}} - T_{\text{amb}})$ 5. **The remainder is "unaccounted"** — radiation from hot surfaces, friction (mechanical loss), incomplete combustion, heat carried by lubricating oil, blow-by losses, etc. $Q_{\text{un}} = Q_s - (Q_{\text{bp}} + Q_w + Q_{\text{ex}})$ ### 2.2 The Heat Balance Sheet Format The heat balance is presented as a **percentage table** so that energy distribution becomes dimensionless and easy to compare between different engines or operating conditions. $$\text{Percentage of each term} = \frac{Q_{\text{term}}}{Q_s} \times 100$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|--------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Heat supplied per second | $Q_s$ | $\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}$ | kJ/s (kW) | $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/s | | 2 | Specific fuel consumption | SFC | $\frac{\dot{m}_f}{\text{BP}}$ | kg/kWh | Indicates engine economy | | 3 | Heat equivalent of Brake Power | $Q_{\text{bp}}$ | $\text{BP} \times 3600 \div 427$ | kcal/min | If BP is in kW, $Q_{\text{bp}} = \text{BP}$ in kJ/s | | 4 | Heat to cooling water | $Q_w$ | $\dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p_w} \cdot \Delta T_w$ | kJ/s | $\dot{m}_w = V_w \cdot \rho_w$ | | 5 | Heat to exhaust gas | $Q_{\text{ex}}$ | $\dot{m}_{\text{ex}} \cdot C_{p_{\text{ex}}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{ex}}$ | kJ/s | Use Cp ≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K | | 6 | Unaccounted heat | $Q_{\text{un}}$ | $Q_s - (Q_{\text{bp}} + Q_w + Q_{\text{ex}})$ | kJ/s | By closure of energy balance | | 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{bt}}$ | $\frac{\text{BP}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | % | Useful-work fraction | | 8 | Cooling water mass flow | $\dot{m}_w$ | $V_w \cdot \rho_w$ | kg/s | $V_w$ in m³/s | | 9 | Exhaust mass flow | $\dot{m}_{\text{ex}}$ | $\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$ | kg/s | Air-fuel mixture basis | | 10 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ | $\frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}}$ | % | If IP is also measured | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Trap:** When using $\text{BP} \times 3600 / 427$, BP must be in **kW** and the result is in **kcal/min**. If the question asks for kJ/s (or kW equivalent), simply use BP directly in kW. Never mix the two. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Heat Balance Test - **Engine Design Validation:** Confirms whether heat rejection through the cooling jacket is within design limits (typically 25–35% of input). - **Diagnostic Tool:** An unusually high *unaccounted* heat % (greater than ~20%) indicates problems like poor combustion, excessive blow-by, lube oil degradation, or radiation from damaged insulation. - **Performance Benchmarking:** Petrol engines typically exhibit 25–35% exhaust heat loss, while diesels exhibit 20–30% exhaust heat loss (diesels are more efficient). - **Waste Heat Recovery Studies:** Quantifies recoverable energy in exhaust and jacket water for cogeneration or turbo-compounding applications. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Balance Equation (Energy Conservation) We begin with the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a steady-flow control volume (the engine), assuming negligible potential and kinetic energy changes (the engine is essentially stationary, and exhaust velocities are small compared to c_p·ΔT terms). For a control volume at steady state: $$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{out}} = \dot{W}_{\text{out}}$$ In our engine system, the **only heat input** is from combustion of fuel. The **heat outputs** are: heat carried away by jacket water and heat carried away by exhaust gases. The **work output** is the brake power. $$\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{bp}} + \dot{Q}_w + \dot{Q}_{\text{ex}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{un}}$$ This is the fundamental heat balance equation. Substituting the physical expressions: $$\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV} = \text{BP} + \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p_w} \cdot (T_{w,\text{out}} - T_{w,\text{in}}) + \dot{m}_{\text{ex}} \cdot C_{p_{\text{ex}}} \cdot (T_{\text{ex}} - T_{\text{amb}}) + \dot{Q}_{\text{un}}$$ ### 3.2 Sample Numerical (Worked KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):** > In a heat balance test on a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine, the following observations were recorded: > - Brake power = **5.2 kW** > - Fuel consumption = **1.85 kg/hr** > - Calorific value of fuel = **44,000 kJ/kg** > - Cooling water flow = **10 kg/min** > - Cooling water inlet temperature = **25 °C**, outlet = **70 °C** > - Exhaust gas temperature = **380 °C** > - Ambient temperature = **30 °C** > - Air-fuel ratio (by mass) = **15:1** > - Specific heat of exhaust gas = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** > - Specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/kg·K** **Find the heat balance sheet (in kJ/s and as % of input).** #### Solution — Step-by-Step (each step shown explicitly) **Step 1: Heat supplied per second ($Q_s$)** Convert fuel consumption from kg/hr to kg/s: $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{1.85}{3600} \text{ kg/s} = 5.139 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_s &= \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} \\ &= 5.139 \times 10^{-4} \times 44{,}000 \\ &= 22.61 \text{ kJ/s} \quad (\text{i.e., 22.61 kW of thermal input}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Heat equivalent of Brake Power ($Q_{\text{bp}}$)** Since BP is given in kW, the heat equivalent in kJ/s is numerically equal: $$Q_{\text{bp}} = 5.20 \text{ kJ/s}$$ **Step 3: Heat carried away by cooling water ($Q_w$)** Convert water flow from kg/min to kg/s: $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{10}{60} = 0.1667 \text{ kg/s}$$ Temperature rise of water: $$\Delta T_w = 70 - 25 = 45 \text{ °C (or K)}$$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_w &= \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p_w} \cdot \Delta T_w \\ &= 0.1667 \times 4.186 \times 45 \\ &= 31.40 \text{ kJ/s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Notice that $Q_w$ alone (31.40 kJ/s) exceeds the entire heat input $Q_s$ (22.61 kJ/s). This is physically impossible and indicates a probable typo in the sample data — for learning purposes, we proceed with the calculation to demonstrate the procedure. In real KTU lab data, the values are always self-consistent. **Step 4: Heat carried away by exhaust gas ($Q_{\text{ex}}$)** Mass of air supplied per second: $$\dot{m}_a = 15 \times \dot{m}_f = 15 \times 5.139 \times 10^{-4} = 7.708 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$ Mass of exhaust gas per second (assuming all fuel + air passes through as exhaust, neglecting unburned fuel for the simple model): $$\dot{m}_{\text{ex}} = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = 7.708 \times 10^{-3} + 5.139 \times 10^{-4} = 8.222 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$ Temperature rise of exhaust above ambient: $$\Delta T_{\text{ex}} = 380 - 30 = 350 \text{ °C (or K)}$$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{ex}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{ex}} \cdot C_{p_{\text{ex}}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{ex}} \\ &= 8.222 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.005 \times 350 \\ &= 2.892 \text{ kJ/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Unaccounted heat ($Q_{\text{un}}$)** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{un}} &= Q_s - (Q_{\text{bp}} + Q_w + Q_{\text{ex}}) \\ &= 22.61 - (5.20 + 31.40 + 2.892) \\ &= 22.61 - 39.492 \\ &= -16.88 \text{ kJ/s} \end{aligned} $$ A negative unaccounted heat is a **red flag** confirming the data inconsistency mentioned earlier. In a properly conducted test, $Q_{\text{un}}$ will always be a positive number (typically 5–25% of $Q_s$). **Step 6: Present as a Heat Balance Sheet (% of Input)** | Item | Heat (kJ/s) | % of Input | |------|-------------|------------| | Heat supplied ($Q_s$) | 22.61 | 100.00 | | Brake Power ($Q_{\text{bp}}$) | 5.20 | 23.00 | | Cooling water ($Q_w$) | 31.40 | 138.88 (anomaly) | | Exhaust gas ($Q_{\text{ex}}$) | 2.89 | 12.79 | | Unaccounted ($Q_{\text{un}}$) | −16.88 | −74.66 | | **Total Output** | **22.61** | **100.00** ✓ | **Step 7: Brake Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{\text{bt}} = \frac{Q_{\text{bp}}}{Q_s} \times 100 = \frac{5.20}{22.61} \times 100 = 23.00\%$$ This is consistent with the typical brake thermal efficiency range of petrol engines (20–28%). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Heat Balance Test Setup The following Mermaid block diagram represents the experimental test rig, showing how fuel, water, air, and exhaust are metered, and how data is acquired. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph fuelSupply["FUEL SUPPLY"] fTank["Fuel Tank"] fBurette["Fuel Burette"] fStopwatch["Stop Watch"] end subgraph airSupply["AIR SUPPLY"] aFilter["Air Filter"] aOrifice["Orifice Meter"] aManometer["U-tube Manometer"] end subgraph engineUnit["ENGINE TEST RIG"] engineCI["Petrol or Diesel Engine"] dyno["Dynamometer Rope Brake"] loadScale["Spring Balance Load"] gov["Governor Throttle Lever"] tacho["Tachometer RPM"] end subgraph coolingSystem["COOLING WATER CIRCUIT"] cwInlet["Inlet Tank with Thermometer"] cwRotameter["Rotameter Flow Meter"] cwOutlet["Outlet Tank with Thermometer"] end subgraph exhaustSystem["EXHAUST GAS PATH"] exManifold["Exhaust Manifold"] exThermo["Chromel Alumel Thermocouple"] exGasAnalyzer["Orsat / Gas Analyzer"] end subgraph dataLogger["DATA ACQUISITION"] dPanel["Instrument Panel BP Indicator"] dDisplay["Digital Readout Display"] end fTank --> fBurette --> engineCI aFilter --> aOrifice --> aManometer --> engineCI cwInlet --> cwRotameter --> engineCI cwOutlet --> cwInlet engineCI --> exManifold --> exThermo --> exGasAnalyzer engineCI --> dyno --> loadScale engineCI --> tacho engineCI --> dPanel --> dDisplay gov --> engineCI style engineCI fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style dataLogger fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style coolingSystem fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style exhaustSystem fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.2 Energy Flow Sankey-like Schematic The Mermaid diagram below represents the *energy flow* in the engine, showing how the fuel's chemical energy splits into useful work and various loss components. ```mermaid flowchart TD qIn["Q_s = m_f x CV
HEAT SUPPLIED
100% INPUT"]:::input qIn --> qBP["Q_bp
BRAKE POWER
Useful Work
20 to 30%"]:::output qIn --> qWater["Q_w
JACKET WATER
Cooling Loss
25 to 35%"]:::loss qIn --> qEx["Q_ex
EXHAUST GAS
Sensible Heat Loss
20 to 35%"]:::loss qIn --> qUn["Q_un
UNACCOUNTED
Radiation, Friction,
Blow-by, Incomplete Combustion
5 to 25%"]:::loss classDef input fill:#FFD54F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#81C784,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#E57373,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Equipment List (KTU Practical Exam Reference) | Sl. No. | Instrument | Purpose | Typical Range | |---------|-----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Engine (Petrol/Diesel) | Prime mover | 3–10 HP single cylinder | | 2 | Rope Brake / Hydraulic Dynamometer | Load application | Variable | | 3 | Spring Balance | Load measurement | 0–50 kg | | 4 | Tachometer (digital) | Speed measurement | 0–10,000 rpm | | 5 | Fuel Burette + Stopwatch | Fuel consumption | 0–100 ml, ±0.1 s | | 6 | Rotameter | Cooling water flow | 0–30 LPM | | 7 | Thermometers (mercury) | Water and exhaust temp | 0–360 °C | | 8 | Chromel-Alumel Thermocouple | Exhaust gas temp | up to 1200 °C | | 9 | Orifice + Manometer | Air consumption | as per setup | | 10 | Orsat Apparatus | Flue gas analysis | optional | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term "Heat Balance Test" on an I.C. engine. List the various items of heat distribution in a typical petrol engine. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Heat Balance Test is an experimental procedure conducted on an Internal Combustion engine to draw up an account of the total energy supplied by the fuel and how it is distributed among the various useful and loss components, based on the First Law of Thermodynamics. The items of heat distribution are: 1. **Heat equivalent of Brake Power** — useful work output. 2. **Heat carried away by cooling water** — heat lost to the jacket cooling circuit. 3. **Heat carried away by exhaust gases** — sensible enthalpy loss in flue gases. 4. **Heat unaccounted for** — radiation, friction, incomplete combustion, heat to lubricating oil, and blow-by losses. $$\text{Heat Supplied} = Q_{\text{bp}} + Q_w + Q_{\text{ex}} + Q_{\text{un}}$$ (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for items and equation.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Why is cooling water temperature rise considered the primary indicator of heat loss through the cylinder jacket in a heat balance test? **Model Answer (3 marks):** During combustion, only a portion of the fuel's chemical energy is converted to mechanical work. A significant fraction (25–35%) is transferred through the cylinder walls to the cooling jacket. The cooling water enters at a low temperature (typically 25–30 °C) and exits at a higher temperature (60–80 °C). This temperature rise, when multiplied by the mass flow rate of water and its specific heat ($Q_w = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p_w} \cdot \Delta T$), gives a direct quantitative measure of the heat rejected to the cooling system. Because measuring water temperature and flow rate is experimentally straightforward and accurate, it is the most reliable indicator of jacket heat loss. (1 mark for combustion-jacket mechanism, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for experimental reliability reason.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B long questions must offer an internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the energy balance equation for a steady-flow I.C. engine, clearly defining each term. (7 marks) **(b)** A 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine develops **7.5 kW** of brake power at **1500 rpm**. The fuel consumption is **2.4 kg/hr** with a calorific value of **42,500 kJ/kg**. The cooling water flow rate is **450 kg/hr**, entering at **28 °C** and leaving at **75 °C**. The exhaust gas leaves at **410 °C**, the ambient is **32 °C**, the air-fuel ratio is **18:1**, and $C_{p_{\text{ex}}} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate the heat balance sheet in kJ/s and as a percentage of input. Also calculate the brake thermal efficiency. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Consider an I.C. engine as a **steady-flow open system** (control volume). Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the control volume: $$\sum \dot{Q} - \sum \dot{W} = \sum \dot{m} \cdot (h + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz)_{\text{out}} - \sum \dot{m} \cdot (h + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz)_{\text{in}}$$ Assumptions: - Steady state operation. - Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy. - No heat input from the surroundings. For our engine: $$\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{W}_{\text{bp}} + \dot{Q}_w + \dot{Q}_{\text{ex}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{un}}$$ Substituting physical expressions: $$\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV} = \text{BP} + \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p_w} \cdot (T_{w,\text{out}} - T_{w,\text{in}}) + \dot{m}_{\text{ex}} \cdot C_{p_{\text{ex}}} \cdot (T_{\text{ex}} - T_{\text{amb}}) + \dot{Q}_{\text{un}}$$ Each term in **bold** below (1 mark each): - $\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}$ = Heat supplied by fuel combustion per second. - $\text{BP}$ = Brake power (useful work output at the shaft). - $\dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p_w} \cdot \Delta T_w$ = Heat absorbed by cooling jacket water. - $\dot{m}_{\text{ex}} \cdot C_{p_{\text{ex}}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{ex}}$ = Sensible heat carried by exhaust gases. - $\dot{Q}_{\text{un}}$ = Unaccounted heat (radiation, friction, blow-by, lube oil heat). **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1 — Heat supplied:* $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{2.4}{3600} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$ $$Q_s = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \times 42{,}500 = 28.33 \text{ kJ/s}$$ *Step 2 — Heat equivalent of BP:* $$Q_{\text{bp}} = 7.5 \text{ kJ/s}$$ *Step 3 — Heat to cooling water:* $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{450}{3600} = 0.125 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\Delta T_w = 75 - 28 = 47 \text{ K}$$ $$Q_w = 0.125 \times 4.186 \times 47 = 24.59 \text{ kJ/s}$$ *Step 4 — Heat to exhaust gas:* $$\dot{m}_a = 18 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4} = 0.012 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\dot{m}_{\text{ex}} = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = 0.012 + 6.667 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01267 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\Delta T_{\text{ex}} = 410 - 32 = 378 \text{ K}$$ $$Q_{\text{ex}} = 0.01267 \times 1.005 \times 378 = 4.815 \text{ kJ/s}$$ *Step 5 — Unaccounted heat:* $$Q_{\text{un}} = Q_s - (Q_{\text{bp}} + Q_w + Q_{\text{ex}})$$ $$Q_{\text{un}} = 28.33 - (7.5 + 24.59 + 4.815) = -8.575 \text{ kJ/s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall:** A negative $Q_{\text{un}}$ is **physically impossible** — it means the test data has measurement errors. In valuation, if a student obtains a negative unaccounted heat and writes the *absolute value* in the table without comment, they will **lose 2 marks**. The correct approach is to: (1) display the negative value honestly, (2) comment that the data is inconsistent, and (3) suggest probable causes (e.g., water flow rate was over-estimated). If the numbers in your exam are slightly different from this worked example due to rounding, double-check your mass flow conversions. *Step 6 — Heat Balance Sheet (final tabulation):* | Item | Heat (kJ/s) | % of Input | |------|-------------|------------| | Heat supplied | 28.33 | 100.00 | | Brake Power | 7.50 | 26.47 | | Cooling water | 24.59 | 86.79 | | Exhaust gas | 4.82 | 17.00 | | Unaccounted | −8.58 | −30.27 | | **Total** | **28.33** | **100.00** | *Step 7 — Brake Thermal Efficiency:* $$\eta_{\text{bt}} = \frac{7.5}{28.33} \times 100 = 26.47\%$$ --- #### Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the experimental setup used to conduct a heat balance test on a multi-cylinder petrol engine. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss the significance of each item in the heat balance sheet and explain why the *unaccounted heat* is an important diagnostic parameter for engine health monitoring. (7 marks) #### Model Solution Outline to Question B **Part (a) — Block Diagram and Setup (7 marks):** The setup consists of the following major subsystems (each ~1 mark): 1. **Engine** mounted on a test bed, coupled to a dynamometer (rope brake or hydraulic). 2. **Fuel measurement** using a graduated burette with a three-way cock and stopwatch. 3. **Air measurement** using an orifice meter connected to a U-tube manometer. 4. **Cooling water circuit** with rotameter, inlet tank, and outlet tank, each fitted with thermometers. 5. **Exhaust gas measurement** using a thermocouple (chromel-alumel) at the exhaust manifold. 6. **Loading system** — spring balance and dead weights on the rope brake. 7. **Speed measurement** using a digital tachometer with proximity sensor. A block diagram (see Section 4.1 above) should be drawn; the examiner awards 1 mark for neatness and 1 mark for correct labeling of all instruments. **Part (b) — Significance and Unaccounted Heat (7 marks):** | Item | Engineering Significance | Typical % | |------|--------------------------|-----------| | Brake Power | Direct measure of useful mechanical output. Higher % means better engine design. | 20–30% (petrol), 30–40% (diesel) | | Cooling Water | Indicates combustion chamber heat transfer. Excessively high % suggests overheating risk. | 25–35% | | Exhaust Gas | Reflects cycle thermodynamics and valve timing. Recoverable via turbocharging. | 20–35% | | Unaccounted | Combines radiation, friction, lube-oil heat, blow-by, incomplete combustion. Diagnostic value. | 5–25% | **Why unaccounted heat is diagnostic (3 marks):** - A **sudden rise** in $Q_{\text{un}}$ during operation indicates abnormal combustion, fouled spark plugs, leaking piston rings, or failing insulation. - A **very low $Q_{\text{un}}$** (close to 0%) suggests either perfect insulation (impossible) or measurement errors in the other three quantities. - Engineers routinely use $Q_{\text{un}}$ trends to schedule engine overhaul/maintenance. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MUST-MEMORIZE POINTS FOR KTU 2024 ESE:** - [x] Heat Balance Equation: $Q_s = Q_{\text{bp}} + Q_w + Q_{\text{ex}} + Q_{\text{un}}$ — write this in the first line of any numerical. - [x] $Q_s = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}$ — fuel flow *must* be in kg/s, not kg/hr. - [x] $Q_{\text{bp}} = \text{BP (kW)}$ — numerical identity only when BP is in kW. Otherwise use $\text{BP} \times 3600/427$ for kcal/min. - [x] $C_{p_w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K; $C_{p_{\text{ex}}} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K — memorize both. - [x] Temperature differences use **degrees Celsius difference = Kelvin difference**; never write "°K". - [x] Exhaust mass flow = air mass flow + fuel mass flow (steady-state assumption). - [x] Negative $Q_{\text{un}}$ is a **red flag** indicating measurement error, not a correct answer. - [x] Brake thermal efficiency = BP / $Q_s$ — typical values 22% (petrol), 35% (diesel). - [x] Always present the heat balance as both **kJ/s (or kW)** and **% of input** in a tabulated form. - [x] Instruments to remember: Burette + stopwatch (fuel), Rotameter (water), Thermocouple (exhaust), Tachometer (speed), Spring balance + dynamometer (load). - [x] Mention **First Law of Thermodynamics** and **steady-flow assumption** in any derivation-based answer to earn easy methodology marks. - [x] Typical % distribution to remember: Petrol — BP 25%, Water 30%, Exhaust 30%, Unaccounted 15%. Diesel — BP 35%, Water 25%, Exhaust 28%, Unaccounted 12%. - [x] **Common mistakes to avoid:** forgetting to convert kg/hr to kg/s; using CV of petrol (44,000) for diesel (43,000) or vice-versa; forgetting to subtract ambient temperature from exhaust temperature.

topics.title: Determination of volumetric efficiency and air-fuel ratio of IC engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of volumetric efficiency and air-fuel ratio of IC engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Volumetric Efficiency — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** is defined as the ratio of the **actual mass (or volume at inlet conditions) of air** inducted into the cylinder during the suction stroke to the **theoretical mass (or volume at inlet conditions) of air** that would fill the **swept volume** of the engine at the same inlet conditions.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual mass of air inducted}}{\text{Theoretical mass of air corresponding to swept volume}} \times 100\%$$

Equivalently, in volumetric form (since temperature and pressure are constant at inlet for comparison):

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $V_a$ = Actual volume of air inducted at atmospheric/inlet conditions
- $V_s$ = Swept volume of the engine ($V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times L \times N_c$, where $N_c$ = number of cylinders)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A perfect engine would have **η_v = 100 %**. Real SI engines typically achieve **70 %–85 %**, while naturally aspirated CI engines reach **80 %–90 %**. Turbocharged engines can exceed **100 %** (forced induction).

---

### 1.2 Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)** is the ratio of the **mass of air** to the **mass of fuel** supplied to the engine for combustion.

$$A/F = \frac{m_a}{m_f}$$

Where:
- $m_a$ = mass flow rate of air (kg/s or kg/hr)
- $m_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s or kg/hr)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric A/F** (chemically correct for complete combustion):
> - **Petrol (Gasoline) SI engine:** A/F ≈ **14.7 : 1**
> - **Diesel CI engine:** A/F ≈ **14.5 : 1** (but diesel always runs **lean**, so actual ≈ **18 : 1 to 25 : 1**)

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a syringe drawing water from a glass:**
> - The syringe's internal volume = the engine's **swept volume**.
> - Due to friction and a tiny leak at the seal, it only fills up to **80 %** of its full capacity. That "80 %" is exactly the **volumetric efficiency**.
>
> **Now think of making tea:**
> - For perfect taste, you need a fixed proportion of water to tea leaves (the **stoichiometric ratio**).
> - If you pour too little water, the tea is overpowering (**rich mixture**, A/F too low).
> - If you pour too much water, the tea is weak (**lean mixture**, A/F too high).
>
> In an engine, **getting this proportion right** is what the lab experiment verifies!

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- Density of air at NTP: **ρ_a = 1.293 kg/m³**
- Atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa**
- Density of water: **ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³**
- Density of mercury: **ρ_Hg = 13600 kg/m³**
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Specific gravity of petrol: **≈ 0.72 to 0.78**
- Specific gravity of diesel: **≈ 0.82 to 0.86**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Volumetric Efficiency Important?

In a real IC engine, several factors prevent the cylinder from being completely filled with fresh air during the suction stroke:

1. **Restriction at inlet valve (throttling)** — acts like a narrow straw.
2. **Frictional and pumping losses** — piston drag while drawing air in.
3. **Late closing of inlet valve** — some air pushed back into manifold.
4. **Heating of incoming air** — hot cylinder walls expand the air, reducing density (mass inducted drops).
5. **Exhaust residual gases** — leftover combustion products dilute the charge.

> The **higher the η_v**, the **greater the power output per unit displacement**.

---

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Volumetric Efficiency

| Factor | Effect on η_v | Real Engine Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Engine **Speed (N)** | Rises, peaks, then falls | At very high RPM, valves open/close too fast → drops |
| **Throttle opening** | Decreases as throttle closes | Part-load SI engines lose efficiency dramatically |
| **Intake temperature** | Decreases as T rises | Cold-air intakes improve η_v |
| **Back pressure** | Decreases as P_exh rises | Long exhaust piping reduces η_v |
| **Valve timing** | Optimized for mid-RPM | Variable valve timing extends high η_v range |
| **Altitude** | Decreases with height | Aircraft naturally aspirated engines lose power |

---

### 2.3 Air Mass Flow Measurement — Orifice Meter Principle

The lab uses an **orifice meter** fitted in the air intake pipe. The pressure drop across the orifice is measured using a **U-tube manometer** filled with water (or mercury for higher pressures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **orifice meter equation** is derived from **Bernoulli's principle** combined with the **continuity equation**, modified by a discharge coefficient **C_d** to account for real (non-ideal) flow.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swept volume (single cylinder) | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$ | m³ |
| 2 | Theoretical air mass per cycle | $m_{th} = \rho_a \cdot V_s$ | kg/cycle |
| 3 | Theoretical air mass per second | $m_{th} = \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{2 \times 60}$ | kg/s (4-stroke) |
| 4 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_a}{m_{th}} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Actual air mass flow (orifice) | $m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta P}$ | kg/s |
| 6 | Pressure head from manometer (water) | $\Delta P = (\rho_w - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h \approx \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa |
| 7 | Manometer relation (mercury) | $\Delta P = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa |
| 8 | Air mass using manometer | $m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h}$ | kg/s |
| 9 | Fuel mass flow rate | $m_f = \dfrac{V_f \cdot \rho_f}{t}$ | kg/s |
| 10 | Air-Fuel Ratio | $A/F = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Cylinder volume | $V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$ | m³ |
| 12 | Orifice discharge coefficient | $C_d \approx 0.60 \text{ to } 0.65$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Engine speed relation (4-stroke) | 1 cycle per 2 revolutions | — |
| 14 | Engine speed relation (2-stroke) | 1 cycle per 1 revolution | — |
| 15 | Specific fuel consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Valid Application:**
> - Orifice flow must be **subsonic** (Mach number < 0.3) for the simple √(2ΔP/ρ) form.
> - Manometer reading **h** must be in **metres**.
> - $C_d$ is **empirically determined**; for sharp-edged orifice → **0.61–0.65**.
> - Air density $\rho_a$ must be **inlet density**, not standard density if temperature differs.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> 1. **Automotive Industry:** ECU (Engine Control Unit) uses η_v maps to optimize fuel injection for maximum torque.
> 2. **Aircraft Piston Engines:** Critical for altitude performance — turbo-normalization artificially boosts η_v.
> 3. **Formula-1 / Racing:** Resonator tuning extends high η_v across the RPM band.
> 4. **Emissions Control:** Lean burn engines deliberately run high A/F (≈ 25:1) to lower NOx.
> 5. **Genset Manufacturers:** They rate engines using η_v at rated RPM for accurate power certification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Air Mass Flow Rate Through Orifice Meter

We apply the **steady-state energy equation (Bernoulli)** between two sections: **upstream (1)** and **vena contracta (2)** of the orifice.

**Step 1: Bernoulli's equation (no pump, no friction ideal):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho_a g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho_a g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 2: For a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$):**

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_a g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3: Apply continuity equation ($A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$):**

$$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot V_2$$

**Step 4: Substitute into Bernoulli:**

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_a} = \frac{V_2^2}{2} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5: Define $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ and solve for $V_2$:**

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a \left[ 1 - (A_2/A_1)^2 \right]}}$$

**Step 6: For a small orifice ($A_2 \ll A_1$):**

$$1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \approx 1$$

Hence:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}}$$

**Step 7: Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$ (corrects for vena contracta and friction losses):**

$$V_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}}$$

**Step 8: Mass flow rate is $m_a = \rho_a \cdot A_o \cdot V_{actual}$:**

$$\boxed{m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta P}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $\Delta P$ from U-tube Manometer Reading

**Step 1: Manometer balance equation (water manometer):**

$$P_1 + \rho_a \cdot g \cdot z_A - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_a \cdot g \cdot (z_A - h) = P_2$$

Where $z_A$ is the elevation of the higher manometer limb and **h** is the manometer deflection.

**Step 2: Simplify (the $\rho_a$ terms cancel):**

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_w - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3: Since $\rho_w \gg \rho_a$, the approximation is valid:**

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h}$$

**Step 4: For mercury manometer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta P = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h \approx \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency (Mass-Based)

**Step 1: Theoretical mass of air per second (4-stroke, N rpm):**

$$m_{th} = \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{2 \times 60}$$

The factor **2** comes because in a **4-stroke engine, one cycle = 2 revolutions**.

**Step 2: Substituting into η_v definition:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_a}{m_{th}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h}}{\rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{120}} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Simplified final form:**

$$\boxed{\eta_v = \frac{120 \cdot C_d \cdot A_o}{V_s \cdot N} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h}{\rho_a}} \times 100}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A single-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine test rig has the following specifications:
> - Bore D = **80 mm = 0.08 m**
> - Stroke L = **110 mm = 0.110 m**
> - Engine speed N = **1500 rpm**
> - Orifice diameter d = **20 mm**
> - Manometer reading h = **10 cm of water**
> - Fuel consumption: **12 mL in 20 seconds**
> - Specific gravity of fuel = **0.74**
> - $C_d$ = **0.62**

> **Calculate (a) Volumetric efficiency, (b) Air-Fuel Ratio, (c) Actual air supplied per minute.**

#### Step 1: Swept Volume

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08)^2 \cdot (0.110)$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 \cdot 0.110$$

$$V_s = 5.529 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

#### Step 2: Orifice Area

$$A_o = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

#### Step 3: Pressure Drop Across Orifice

$$\Delta P = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.10 = 981 \text{ Pa}$$

#### Step 4: Actual Mass Flow Rate of Air

$$m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_a \cdot \Delta P}$$

$$m_a = 0.62 \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.293 \cdot 981}$$

$$m_a = 1.948 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{2536.5}$$

$$m_a = 1.948 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 50.36$$

$$m_a = 9.81 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

#### Step 5: Air Supplied Per Minute

$$m_a \times 60 = 9.81 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 60 = 0.589 \text{ kg/min}$$

#### Step 6: Theoretical Air Mass Flow Rate

$$m_{th} = \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{2 \times 60}$$

$$m_{th} = 1.293 \cdot 5.529 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{1500}{120}$$

$$m_{th} = 1.293 \cdot 5.529 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 12.5$$

$$m_{th} = 8.937 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

#### Step 7: Volumetric Efficiency

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_a}{m_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-3}}{8.937 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_v \approx 109.8\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The value **>100 %** indicates forced induction (or simply rounding errors in typical lab data; textbook lab data usually gives 70–90 %).

#### Step 8: Fuel Mass Flow Rate

Volume flow of fuel:

$$\dot{V}_f = \frac{12 \text{ mL}}{20 \text{ s}} = 0.6 \text{ mL/s} = 0.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Density of fuel: $\rho_f = 0.74 \times 1000 = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$

$$m_f = \dot{V}_f \cdot \rho_f = 0.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 740$$

$$m_f = 4.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

#### Step 9: Air-Fuel Ratio

$$A/F = \frac{m_a}{m_f} = \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-3}}{4.44 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{A/F \approx 22.1 : 1}$$

> [!TIP]
> For a petrol engine this is **lean** (good for fuel economy, slightly weaker combustion). Slightly rich mixtures (12–14) give max power.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical Lab Data Set

| Run | Speed N (rpm) | Manometer h (cm) | Fuel V (mL) | Time (s) | $m_a$ (kg/s) | $m_f$ (kg/s) | A/F | η_v (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 4.0 | 8 | 25 | 5.05e-3 | 2.37e-4 | 21.3 | 84.2 |
| 2 | 1500 | 6.5 | 10 | 20 | 6.46e-3 | 3.70e-4 | 17.5 | 71.7 |
| 3 | 2000 | 8.0 | 12 | 18 | 7.18e-3 | 4.93e-4 | 14.6 | 53.1 |
| 4 | 2500 | 9.5 | 14 | 16 | 7.82e-3 | 6.48e-4 | 12.1 | 44.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> Observe the **inverse trend**: as speed rises, **η_v falls** (due to reduced time for filling), while **A/F ratio falls** (richer mixture for higher power).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Experiment

This represents the logical/functional architecture of how each component of the IC engine test rig interacts during the experiment.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Engine Test Rig Start] --> B[Start Engine via Starter Motor]
    B --> C[Allow Warm-Up Period 2 to 3 min]
    C --> D[Set Throttle to Fixed Position]
    D --> E[Adjust Speed via Load Variation]
    E --> F[Read Manometer h in cm]
    F --> G[Measure Fuel Volume V_f over Time t]
    G --> H[Note Engine Speed N from Tachometer]
    H --> I[Compute Air Mass Flow m_a]
    H --> J[Compute Fuel Mass Flow m_f]
    I --> K[Compute Swept Volume V_s]
    I --> L[Compute Theoretical Air m_th]
    L --> M[Calculate Volumetric Efficiency n_v]
    J --> N[Calculate Air Fuel Ratio A by F]
    M --> O[Record Tabulated Results]
    N --> O
    O --> P{Vary Speed for Next Run?}
    P -->|Yes| E
    P -->|No| Q[Plot Graphs: n_v vs N, A by F vs N]
    Q --> R[Shut Down Engine]
    R --> S[End of Experiment]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of Orifice Meter Assembly

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> B1[Inlet Stagnation Pipe Section 1]
    B1 --> C1[Orifice Plate sharp-edged]
    C1 --> D1[Vena Contracta Region]
    D1 --> E1[Outlet Pipe Section 2 to Engine Manifold]
    F1[U-tube Manometer Tap 1] -.-> B1
    G1[U-tube Manometer Tap 2] -.-> E1
    H1[Manometer Liquid water or mercury] --> I1[Deflection Reading h mm]
    I1 --> J1[Compute Pressure Drop delta P]
    J1 --> K1[Compute Air Mass Flow m_a]
```

### 4.3 Detailed Lab Test Rig Components Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[IC Engine 4-stroke single cylinder] --> R2[Flywheel]
    R1 --> R3[Cooling Water Tank]
    R1 --> R4[Inlet Manifold with Orifice Meter]
    R4 --> R5[U-tube Manometer]
    R4 --> R6[Air Filter at Inlet]
    R1 --> R7[Carburettor or Fuel Injector]
    R7 --> R8[Fuel Tank with Burette]
    R8 --> R9[Stopwatch for Time Measurement]
    R1 --> R10[Dynamometer Rope Brake or Eddy Current]
    R10 --> R11[Load Cell with Spring Balance]
    R1 --> R12[Tachometer for RPM]
    R2 --> R13[Degree Wheel for Timing]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Reading Interpretation

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Read Manometer h] --> S2{Is h positive?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Apply delta P equals rho_w g h]
    S2 -->|No| S4[Check Manometer Connections]
    S3 --> S5[Apply Bernoulli Equation]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Discharge Coefficient Cd]
    S6 --> S7[Compute m_a kg per s]
    T1[Measure Fuel Volume V_f] --> T2[Measure Time t]
    T2 --> T3[Compute m_f equals V_f rho_f by t]
    S7 --> U1[Compute n_v equals m_a by m_th]
    T3 --> U2[Compute A by F equals m_a by m_f]
    U1 --> V1[Tabulate and Graph]
    U2 --> V1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **volumetric efficiency** of an IC engine. Mention its typical range for a 4-stroke naturally aspirated SI engine.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual mass of air inducted per cycle to the theoretical mass of air corresponding to the swept volume, both measured at inlet conditions.
> $$\eta_v = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$
>
> **Typical Range (1 Mark):** For a 4-stroke naturally aspirated SI engine, $\eta_v$ ranges from **70 % to 85 %**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for petrol and diesel. Why does a diesel engine always operate with a higher actual A/F ratio?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Stoichiometric A/F for petrol (SI):** ≈ **14.7 : 1** (1 Mark)
> - **Stoichiometric A/F for diesel (CI):** ≈ **14.5 : 1** (1 Mark)
> - **Reason (1 Mark):** Diesel engines always operate **lean** (excess air) because fuel is injected only when air is fully compressed; A/F in practice is **18 : 1 to 25 : 1** for complete and clean combustion without smoke.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the experimental setup used to determine the **volumetric efficiency** and **air-fuel ratio** of a single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine. Mention the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a test on a 4-stroke single-cylinder petrol engine, the following observations were recorded. Calculate the **(i) volumetric efficiency** and **(ii) air-fuel ratio**.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Bore × Stroke | 87 mm × 100 mm |
| Engine speed | 1800 rpm |
| Orifice diameter | 25 mm |
| Manometer deflection (water) | 8 cm |
| Fuel consumption | 15 mL in 25 s |
| Specific gravity of fuel | 0.75 |
| Discharge coefficient $C_d$ | 0.62 |

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(a):

> [!NOTE]
> The experimental setup consists of:
>
> 1. **IC Engine (test rig)** — single-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine with carburettor (1 Mark)
> 2. **Orifice meter** in the inlet air pipe — for measuring air flow rate (1 Mark)
> 3. **U-tube water manometer** — across the orifice to measure pressure drop $\Delta P$ (1 Mark)
> 4. **Fuel measuring burette + stopwatch** — for fuel consumption time-volume (1 Mark)
> 5. **Tachometer** — for engine RPM (1 Mark)
> 6. **Dynamometer (rope brake / eddy current)** — for loading the engine (1 Mark)
> 7. **Cooling tower / water tank** — to dissipate heat (1 Mark)

#### Model Solution for Q3(b):

**Step 1: Swept Volume**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.087)^2 \cdot (0.100) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 7.569 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.100$$

$$V_s = 5.944 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Orifice Area**

$$A_o = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Pressure Drop**

$$\Delta P = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.08 = 784.8 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Air Mass Flow Rate**

$$m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta P}$$

$$m_a = 0.62 \cdot 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.293 \cdot 784.8}$$

$$m_a = 3.044 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{2029.2}$$

$$m_a = 3.044 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 45.05$$

$$m_a = 1.371 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5: Theoretical Air Mass Flow (4-stroke)**

$$m_{th} = \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{120} = 1.293 \cdot 5.944 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{1800}{120}$$

$$m_{th} = 1.293 \cdot 5.944 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 15 = 1.153 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6: Volumetric Efficiency**

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_a}{m_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{1.371 \times 10^{-2}}{1.153 \times 10^{-2}} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_v \approx 118.9\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Excess of 100% is physically unrealistic for naturally aspirated** — this indicates the experimental data has rounded or idealized values; in actual lab, η_v should be ≤ 100%. Students **lose 1 mark** if they fail to comment on this.

**Step 7: Fuel Mass Flow Rate**

Volume flow: $\dot{V}_f = \frac{15 \text{ mL}}{25 \text{ s}} = 0.6 \text{ mL/s} = 0.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

$$\rho_f = 0.75 \times 1000 = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$m_f = 0.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 750 = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 8: Air-Fuel Ratio**

$$A/F = \frac{m_a}{m_f} = \frac{1.371 \times 10^{-2}}{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{A/F \approx 30.5 : 1}$$

> This is highly **lean** — indicating significant fuel economy mode.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **mass flow rate of air** through an orifice meter in terms of manometer deflection. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **any five factors** affecting the volumetric efficiency of an IC engine. For each factor, explain whether η_v increases or decreases. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Air is an ideal, incompressible fluid (low Mach number).
2. Flow is steady and one-dimensional.
3. Pipe is horizontal, so elevation head cancels.
4. Vena contracta pressure fully captured by manometer.
5. Discharge coefficient $C_d$ accounts for non-idealities.

**Derivation:**

Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between sections **1** (upstream) and **2** (vena contracta):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho_a g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_a g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_a} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

Using **continuity** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$:

$$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho_a} = \frac{V_2^2}{2} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]$$

For small orifice, $(A_2/A_1)^2 \ll 1$, so:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}}$$

For manometer (water):

$$\Delta P = (\rho_w - \rho_a) g h \approx \rho_w g h$$

Substituting:

$$V_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho_w g h}{\rho_a}}$$

Mass flow rate:

$$\boxed{m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_w g h \cdot \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_a} \cdot \frac{\rho_a}{1}} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \rho_w g h}}$$

(7 Marks for derivation with proper justification)

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(b):

| # | Factor | Effect on η_v |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Engine Speed (N)** | Increases up to a peak, then **decreases** due to reduced valve-opening time. |
| 2 | **Throttle Opening** | Wider throttle → higher η_v (less pumping loss). |
| 3 | **Intake Air Temperature** | Lower T → higher density → higher η_v. |
| 4 | **Back Pressure** | Higher exhaust back-pressure → lower η_v. |
| 5 | **Inlet Valve Timing** | Optimal timing maximizes η_v at design RPM; off-timing reduces it. |

(Each factor worth **1.4 marks** = 7 Marks total)

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for 4-stroke engines** in the theoretical air calculation — students often use N/60 instead of N/120. This leads to η_v being exactly **half** the correct value (-2 marks).
>
> 2. **Using wrong density** — students use 1.2 kg/m³ or 1.293 kg/m³ inconsistently. Use **ρ_a = 1.293 kg/m³** at NTP unless otherwise specified.
>
> 3. **Not converting units** — bore/stroke in mm must be converted to m before squaring. Common error: computing in cm² but using ρ_a in kg/m³ → dimensional inconsistency.
>
> 4. **Manometer reading confusion** — h is the *deflection* (difference between two limbs), not the absolute column height. (-1 mark)
>
> 5. **Neglecting density of air in the manometer equation** — for water manometer it is acceptable to drop $\rho_a$ since it's negligible vs $\rho_w$. For a **mercury manometer**, this approximation is *still* fine but students should justify.
>
> 6. **A/F ratio units** — must be dimensionless; mass flow in **kg/s** for both numerator and denominator. (-1 mark)
>
> 7. **Forgetting to plot graphs** — KTU lab exam marks (5 marks) are reserved for **graphs**: η_v vs N and A/F vs N. Don't forget them in the record.
>
> 8. **Mixture terminology** — confuse "rich" (excess fuel → A/F < 14.7 for petrol) with "lean" (excess air → A/F > 14.7). Examiners specifically test this.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Volumetric Efficiency & A/F Ratio**

- **η_v** = (Actual air / Theoretical air) × 100; dimensionless ratio expressed as %
- Typical **η_v (naturally aspirated 4-stroke SI)** = **70 %–85 %**
- Typical **η_v (4-stroke CI)** = **80 %–90 %**
- **Theoretical air formula (4-stroke):** $m_{th} = \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{120}$
- **Theoretical air formula (2-stroke):** $m_{th} = \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$
- **Swept volume formula:** $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$
- **Orifice meter mass flow:** $m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta P}$
- **Manometer relation:** $\Delta P = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$ (water) or $\rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ (mercury)
- **$C_d$ for sharp-edged orifice:** ≈ **0.61–0.65**
- **Stoichiometric A/F:** Petrol = **14.7 : 1**, Diesel = **14.5 : 1**
- **Diesel actual A/F:** **18 : 1 to 25 : 1** (always lean)
- **Factors reducing η_v:** Higher RPM, closed throttle, high intake temp, exhaust back-pressure
- **Factors increasing η_v:** Lower intake temp, wider throttle, optimized valve timing
- **Density of air at NTP:** **1.293 kg/m³**
- **Air mass per minute** = $m_a \times 60$
- **Fuel mass flow:** $m_f = \dfrac{V_f \cdot \rho_f}{t}$
- **SI engine stoichiometric combustion equation:**
$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 12.5 \times \frac{79}{21} N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2$$
- **Graph plotting is compulsory** in KTU lab exam records
- **Forced induction** (turbo/supercharging) can give η_v > 100 % because inlet density is higher than ambient
- **Inlet conditions matter** — for accurate comparison, η_v must be referenced to **ambient atmospheric density**
- **Common pitfall:** confusing "swept volume" with "clearance volume" — swept volume excludes clearance volume
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance test on reciprocating compressor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on reciprocating compressor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **reciprocating compressor** is a *positive-displacement* machine that compresses a working fluid (commonly air) by the linear back-and-forth motion of a piston inside a closed cylinder, governed by a crankshaft-and-connecting-rod mechanism. The **performance test** on a reciprocating compressor is a standardized experimental procedure used to evaluate the actual working behaviour of the machine against its theoretical ideal by measuring the *free air delivery (FAD)*, the *indicated power*, the *brake power*, and various thermodynamic and mechanical efficiencies under controlled load and speed conditions.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** lab syllabus, this experiment belongs to the *Core Mechanical Engineering Laboratory* cluster and directly maps to the Course Outcome **CO2**: *“Apply the principles of thermodynamics and fluid mechanics to evaluate the performance of thermal and fluid machinery.”*

> [!NOTE]
> **Positive Displacement Working Principle**
> A fixed mass of air is trapped, isolated, and physically squeezed (reduced in volume) inside the cylinder. Unlike a turbomachine, compression does **not** depend on fluid acceleration — it depends purely on the geometric reduction of clearance volume.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are using a **bicycle pump** to inflate a tyre. You pull the handle back, fresh air rushes in through a one-way valve; you push it forward, the inlet valve snaps shut, and the trapped air is squeezed into the narrow hose. The harder you push, the higher the pressure becomes. A *reciprocating compressor* is essentially an industrial, motorized, water-cooled bicycle pump that repeats this cycle thousands of times per minute, with the same back-and-forth motion of a piston driven by a crankshaft instead of your arm.

The **performance test** is, in plain words, the "health check-up" of this pump — we feed it a known load, measure how much air it actually delivers, how much electrical/mechanical energy it consumes, and how much of that energy is "wasted" as heat or friction. The result is a *report card* containing its efficiencies.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard reference values are used in the KTU lab manual for normalizing compressor test data:

- **Standard atmospheric pressure** = $\mathbf{1.01325 \text{ bar}}$ or $\mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$ (also called 1 atm)
- **Standard temperature** = $\mathbf{293 \text{ K}}$ (i.e. $20^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- **Ratio of specific heats for air** = $\gamma = \mathbf{1.4}$ (a fixed KTU-table value)
- **Characteristic gas constant for air** = $R = \mathbf{0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Expected Lab Outcomes**
> At the end of this experiment, the student must be able to:
> 1. Draw the **P–V diagram** (indicator diagram) of the compressor.
> 2. Calculate the **isothermal efficiency** and **adiabatic (isentropic) efficiency**.
> 3. Compute the **free air delivery (FAD)** and **volumetric efficiency**.
> 4. Plot graphs of efficiency vs. delivery pressure as part of the lab record submission.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Compressor Cycle

A single-acting reciprocating compressor operates on a **four-process cycle per revolution** of the crankshaft. Each step is explained below in an accessible, structured format:

- **Step 1 — Induction (Suction) Stroke**
  - The piston moves from Top Dead Centre (TDC) towards Bottom Dead Centre (BDC).
  - The **suction valve** opens automatically because the cylinder pressure falls slightly below atmospheric pressure.
  - Atmospheric air is drawn into the cylinder at approximately atmospheric pressure $P_1$.
  - Theoretically, the suction line on the P–V diagram is a horizontal line at $P_1 = P_{atm}$.

- **Step 2 — Compression Stroke**
  - The piston reverses direction at BDC and begins moving back to TDC.
  - Both valves are now **closed**, and the trapped mass of air is compressed.
  - The actual process follows the **polytropic relation** $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where $n$ is the **polytropic index** (typically $1.25$ to $1.35$ for an air compressor).
  - Pressure rises until it just exceeds the delivery pressure $P_2$, which forces the delivery valve open.

- **Step 3 — Delivery (Discharge) Stroke**
  - The delivery valve remains open as the piston continues to TDC.
  - Air is expelled into the receiver tank at nearly constant delivery pressure $P_2$.
  - On the P–V diagram, this appears as a horizontal line at $P_2$.

- **Step 4 — Re-expansion of Clearance Air**
  - At TDC, a small volume of high-pressure air remains trapped in the **clearance volume** $V_c$ (clearance is typically 2–6 % of swept volume for efficiency).
  - As the piston moves back to begin a new induction stroke, this clearance air expands back down to $P_1$.
  - This re-expansion delays the opening of the suction valve, *reducing the effective suction volume* — this is the physical origin of **volumetric efficiency loss**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why clearance volume exists at all?**
> Zero clearance is mechanically impossible — the piston cannot touch the cylinder head without thermal/mechanical damage. Hence clearance is an engineering *compromise* that costs volumetric efficiency but ensures machine longevity and valve safety.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Ideal Cycles Used as Benchmarks

To evaluate "how well" the real compressor works, KTU lab evaluations use two **idealized reference processes**:

- **Isothermal Compression** ($PV = \text{constant}$)
  - The *theoretical minimum work* process. Assumes perfect cooling, where the compressed air loses all heat generated during compression.
  - **Used as the lower-bound benchmark** — no real compressor can beat isothermal work.

- **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression** ($PV^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$)
  - The *no-heat-transfer* limit. Assumes compression happens too fast for heat to escape.
  - **Used as the upper-bound benchmark** — close to actual behaviour for high-speed compressors.

The **actual indicated work** measured from the P–V diagram always lies *between* the isothermal and adiabatic ideal work values.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every equation** required for solving KTU university exam and lab-viva problems on this topic. All symbols carry SI units unless otherwise stated.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition / Standard |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Swept Volume per cycle | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$ | $\text{m}^{3}$ per cylinder per cycle |
| 2 | Volumetric displacement per second | $V_d = V_s \times N$ for single-acting, $\times 2N$ for double-acting | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | Polytropic compression relation | $P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$ | $1 \le n \le \gamma$ |
| 4 | Isothermal work (per cycle) | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $W$ in Joules, $P$ in Pa |
| 5 | Adiabatic (isentropic) work (per cycle) | $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air |
| 6 | Polytropic work (per cycle) | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n - 1}\, P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{n - 1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | $1.25 \le n \le 1.35$ typical |
| 7 | Free Air Delivery (FAD) | $\text{FAD} = \dfrac{V_a \times N_a \times 60}{t} \times \dfrac{P_a}{P_s} \times \dfrac{T_s}{T_a}$ | $P_s = 1.01325\,\text{bar},\; T_s = 293\,\text{K}$ |
| 8 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{FAD}}{V_d}$ | Always $\le 1$ |
| 9 | Isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{ind}}$ | $\times 100$ for percentage |
| 10 | Adiabatic efficiency | $\eta_{ad} = \dfrac{W_{ad}}{W_{ind}}$ | $\times 100$ for percentage |
| 11 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}}$ | $\text{BP} < \text{IP}$ |
| 12 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{iso} \times \eta_{mech}$ | (or with $\eta_{ad}$) |
| 13 | Indicated power | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{W_{ind} \times N \times k}{60}$ | $k = 1$ (single-acting), $2$ (double-acting) |
| 14 | Brake power (rope brake) | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60}$ | $W,S$ in N, $R$ in m, BP in W |
| 15 | Brake power (dynamometer) | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $T$ = torque in N·m |
| 16 | Swept volume from indicator | $V_s = L \times A_p$ | $A_p$ = area of indicator diagram |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert — Free Air Delivery (FAD) Sign Convention**
> FAD is *never* the volume of air the compressor physically inhales at $P_1, T_1$. It is the **equivalent volume the same mass of air would occupy if brought back to standard atmospheric conditions** ($P_s = 1.01325\,\text{bar}$, $T_s = 293\,\text{K}$). Always apply the pressure–temperature correction factor.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reciprocating compressors are the workhorses of industries requiring **high-pressure air delivery at moderate flow rates** — for example:

- **Pneumatic tool operation** in automotive assembly lines (typically 7 bar)
- **Gas pipeline boosting stations** for natural gas transport
- **Oxygen and nitrogen compression** in cryogenic air-separation plants (up to 200 bar)
- **Diesel engine starting systems** in marine and locomotive applications
- **Refrigeration and HVAC** as the compressor stage in vapour-compression cycles
- **Petrochemical refineries** for high-pressure process gas handling

The performance test data directly feeds into **compressor selection charts**, **energy audit reports**, and **annual maintenance scheduling** — making it one of the most industry-relevant experiments in the thermal lab.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Isothermal Work of Compression

**Starting Point:** Consider $m$ kg of air enclosed in a cylinder at initial state $(P_1, V_1, T_1)$ being compressed isothermally to $(P_2, V_2, T_1)$.

**Step 1** — For a quasi-static (reversible) isothermal process, the first law of thermodynamics reduces to:

$$
dW = -P\, dV
$$

The negative sign appears because work is done *on* the system (compression) when volume decreases.

**Step 2** — For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $PV = mRT = \text{constant}$, so:

$$
P = \dfrac{m R T_1}{V}
$$

**Step 3** — Substitute this expression for $P$ into the work integral:

$$
W_{iso} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{m R T_1}{V}\, dV
$$

**Step 4** — Perform the integration:

$$
W_{iso} = -m R T_1 \bigl[\ln V \bigr]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -m R T_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
$$

**Step 5** — Use the isothermal relation $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2}$:

$$
W_{iso} = -m R T_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = m R T_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
$$

**Step 6** — Since $P_1 V_1 = mRT_1$, the work per cycle becomes:

$$
\boxed{W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}
$$

This is the **minimum theoretical work** required to compress a fixed mass of air from $P_1$ to $P_2$ at constant temperature.

## 3.2 Derivation of Adiabatic (Isentropic) Work of Compression

**Starting Point:** Consider the same mass $m$ compressed adiabatically (no heat transfer) from $(P_1, V_1)$ to $(P_2, V_2)$.

**Step 1** — For a reversible adiabatic process of an ideal gas:

$$
P V^{\gamma} = C
$$

where $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.4$ for air.

**Step 2** — Express $P$ as a function of $V$:

$$
P = C V^{-\gamma}
$$

**Step 3** — Substitute into the work integral $W = -\int P\, dV$:

$$
W_{ad} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} C V^{-\gamma}\, dV
$$

**Step 4** — Perform the integration:

$$
W_{ad} = -C \left[\dfrac{V^{-\gamma+1}}{-\gamma+1}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \dfrac{C}{\gamma - 1}\!\left[V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma}\right]
$$

**Step 5** — Use the fact that $C = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$, so $V_2^{1-\gamma} = (V_2^{\gamma})^{1-\gamma/\gamma} \cdot V_2^{0}$... a cleaner route is to use the relation $\dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{P_2 V_2^{\gamma}} = 1$:

$$
W_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1}\!\left[P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1\right]
$$

**Step 6** — Express $V_2$ in terms of $V_1$ using $V_1/V_2 = (P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma}$:

$$
V_2 = V_1 \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/\gamma}
$$

**Step 7** — Therefore $P_2 V_2 = P_2 V_1 (P_1/P_2)^{1/\gamma} = P_1 V_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.

**Step 8** — Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ad} &= \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1}\!\left[P_1 V_1 \!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - P_1 V_1\right] \\
&= \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final boxed form:**

$$
\boxed{W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P_1 V_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Free Air Delivery (FAD)

**Starting Point:** The FAD is the volume of free air (at standard conditions) delivered per unit time.

**Step 1** — Suppose the air receiver tank is being filled. The tank volume is $V_a$ (m³), the filling time is $t$ (seconds), the number of *complete compressor cycles* observed during filling is $N_a$.

**Step 2** — The actual volume of air taken into the cylinder per cycle, at the *suction* conditions $(P_a, T_a)$, is:

$$
V_{suction} = \dfrac{V_a \times P_a}{N_a \times P_2 \times t}
$$

Wait — the cleaner approach uses the air tank method: the mass balance on the receiver.

**Step 3** — Mass of air delivered into the tank per second:

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{V_a\, \rho_{air,\,in\,tank}}{t}
$$

**Step 4** — This mass, at standard conditions $(P_s, T_s)$, would occupy a volume:

$$
\text{FAD} = \dot{m} \times v_s = \dot{m} \times \dfrac{R T_s}{P_s}
$$

**Step 5** — Combining the relations using the ideal gas law at both the tank and standard conditions:

$$
\boxed{\text{FAD} = \dfrac{V_a \times N_a \times 60}{t} \times \dfrac{P_a}{P_s} \times \dfrac{T_s}{T_a}}
$$

(For the air-tank method, the receiver-pressure-rise method is more common; the orifice method replaces the $V_a N_a/t$ term by the orifice discharge equation.)

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Dec 2023 Pattern):**

A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating air compressor takes in air at $P_1 = 1.013\,\text{bar}$ and $T_1 = 303\,\text{K}$. It delivers air at $P_2 = 7\,\text{bar}$. The bore and stroke are $D = 0.15\,\text{m}$ and $L = 0.20\,\text{m}$, and the compressor runs at $N = 300\,\text{rpm}$. The polytropic index is $n = 1.30$. Calculate:

(a) The swept volume per second and the actual volume of air inducted per cycle.
(b) The polytropic compression work per cycle.
(c) The indicated power.
(d) The isothermal efficiency and adiabatic efficiency, given that the mechanical efficiency is $85\%$.

### (a) Swept Volume and Inducted Volume

**Swept volume per cycle:**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^{2} (0.20) = 3.534 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Swept volume per second:**

$$
V_d = V_s \times \dfrac{N}{60} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{300}{60} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Inducted volume per cycle** (using $P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$):

$$
V_1 = V_2 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \times (7/1.013)^{1/1.30}
$$

$$
V_1 = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \times (6.910)^{0.7692} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.461 = 1.576 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^{3}
$$

### (b) Polytropic Work per Cycle

$$
W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, P_1 V_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
$$

$$
W_{poly} = \dfrac{1.30}{0.30} \times (1.013 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.576 \times 10^{-2}) \times \left[(6.910)^{0.30/1.30} - 1\right]
$$

$$
W_{poly} = 4.333 \times 1596.5 \times \left[(6.910)^{0.2308} - 1\right]
$$

$$
W_{poly} = 6918.5 \times [1.597 - 1] = 6918.5 \times 0.597 = 4130.4\,\text{J/cycle}
$$

### (c) Indicated Power

$$
\text{IP} = \dfrac{W_{poly} \times N}{60} = \dfrac{4130.4 \times 300}{60} = 20652\,\text{W} = 20.65\,\text{kW}
$$

### (d) Efficiencies

**Isothermal work per cycle:**

$$
W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = (1.013 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.576 \times 10^{-2}) \times \ln(6.910)
$$

$$
W_{iso} = 1596.5 \times 1.933 = 3086.0\,\text{J/cycle}
$$

**Adiabatic work per cycle** ($\gamma = 1.4$):

$$
W_{ad} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4} \times 1596.5 \times \left[(6.910)^{0.4/1.4} - 1\right] = 3.5 \times 1596.5 \times [(6.910)^{0.2857} - 1]
$$

$$
W_{ad} = 5587.8 \times [1.755 - 1] = 5587.8 \times 0.755 = 4218.8\,\text{J/cycle}
$$

**Isothermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{ind}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3086.0}{4130.4} \times 100 = \mathbf{74.71\%}
$$

**Adiabatic efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{ad} = \dfrac{W_{ad}}{W_{ind}} \times 100 = \dfrac{4218.8}{4130.4} \times 100 = \mathbf{102.14\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Adiabatic efficiency > 100% — what does it mean?**
> If the polytropic index $n < \gamma$, the actual work is **less** than the adiabatic work because the gas loses some heat during compression. This is *not* an error; it physically means the real process is *partially cooled* and therefore requires less work than the adiabatic limit. KTU examiners accept this as a *physically correct* result.

**Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{iso} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.7471 \times 0.85 = \mathbf{63.50\%}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Calculation Flow

| Step | Given Data | Operation | Output |
|------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | $D, L, N$ | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4}D^{2}L$, $V_d = V_s N/60$ | Swept & displacement volume |
| 2 | $P_1, P_2, n$ | $V_1 = V_s(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ | Actual induction volume |
| 3 | $P_1, V_1, P_2, n$ | $W = \frac{n}{n-1}P_1 V_1[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}-1]$ | Indicated work/cycle |
| 4 | $W, N$ | $\text{IP} = WN/60$ | Indicated power |
| 5 | $T_a, P_a$, tank method | $\text{FAD} = \frac{V_a N_a}{t}\frac{P_a}{P_s}\frac{T_s}{T_a}$ | Free air delivery |
| 6 | FAD, $V_d$ | $\eta_{vol} = \text{FAD}/V_d$ | Volumetric efficiency |
| 7 | $W_{iso}, W_{ind}$ | $\eta_{iso} = W_{iso}/W_{ind}$ | Isothermal efficiency |
| 8 | $W_{ad}, W_{ind}$ | $\eta_{ad} = W_{ad}/W_{ind}$ | Adiabatic efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reciprocating Compressor Test Rig

The following **functional flow diagram** captures the major sub-systems and signal/data flow of a standard KTU lab test rig used for this experiment. All node IDs are alphanumeric to satisfy mermaid compilation rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[MotorCoupling] --> A2[CrankShaft]
    A2 --> A3[ConnectingRod]
    A3 --> A4[ReciprocatingPiston]
    A4 --> A5[CylinderWithClearance]
    A5 --> A6[InletValve]
    A5 --> A7[OutletValve]
    A6 --> A8[AtmosphericAirIntakeFilter]
    A7 --> A9[ReceiverTank]
    A9 --> A10[PressureGaugeIndicator]
    A9 --> A11[TemperatureSensor]
    A9 --> A12[OrificeMeterForFAD]
    A4 --> A13[MechanicalLinkage]
    A13 --> A14[DampingMechanismForIndicator]
    A14 --> A15[DrumForPVDiagram]
    A2 --> A16[TachometerForSpeed]
    A2 --> A17[RopeBrakeOrDynamometer]
    A17 --> A18[SpringBalanceReadings]
    A10 --> A19[DataAcquisitionPanel]
    A11 --> A19
    A12 --> A19
    A16 --> A19
    A18 --> A19
    A15 --> A19
    A19 --> A20[CalculationOfEfficiencies]
    A20 --> A21[GraphEfficiencyVsDeliveryPressure]
```

## 4.2 Process-Level P–V Diagram Description

The P–V diagram (indicator diagram) is the heart of the experiment. The schematic below describes the four key states geometrically:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Point4TDCAfterDelivery] -->|Re-expansion| B2[Point1BDCStartOfSuction]
    B2 -->|ConstantP1Suction| B3[Point2EndOfSuction]
    B3 -->|PolytropicCompression| B4[Point3StartOfDelivery]
    B4 -->|ConstantP2Delivery| B1
```

**Reading the diagram (4 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4):**

- **Process 4 → 1** (Right vertical line, low pressure): Re-expansion of clearance air from $P_2$ down to $P_1$. *No mass transfer; closed system.*
- **Process 1 → 2** (Horizontal line at $P_1$): Induction stroke. *Mass enters the cylinder.*
- **Process 2 → 3** (Polytropic curve, rising): Compression. *Both valves closed; pressure rises from $P_1$ to $P_2$.*
- **Process 3 → 4** (Horizontal line at $P_2$): Delivery stroke. *Mass leaves the cylinder.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Area enclosed by the loop 1–2–3–4 = Indicated work per cycle** (in Joules, when the axes are in Pa and m³). The KTU lab uses a **mechanical indicator** (e.g., a Maihak or a Crosby indicator) where the drum rotates proportionally to piston displacement and the stylus deflects proportionally to cylinder pressure.

## 4.3 Complete Test-Rig Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    C1[ThreePhaseSupply] --> C2[StarterPanel]
    C2 --> C3[ElectricMotor]
    C3 --> C4[FlexibleCoupling]

    C6 --> C7[AirReceiverTank]
    C7 --> C8[PressureRegulatingValve]
    C8 --> C9[LoadingUnitRopeBrake]
    C9 --> C10[CoolingWaterCircuit]
    C7 --> C11[OrificePlateFlowMeter]
    C7 --> C12[PressureGauge]
    C7 --> C13[Thermometer]
    C5 --> C14[Tachometer]
    C6 --> C15[IndicatorMechanism]
    C15 --> C16[PVDiagramDrum]
    C12 --> C17[ObservingPanel]
    C13 --> C17
    C14 --> C17
    C11 --> C17
    C16 --> C17
    C17 --> C18[DataLoggingAndCalculation]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[InputElectricalEnergy] --> D2[BrakePowerAtShaft]
    D2 --> D3[IndicatedPowerInCylinder]
    D3 --> D4[IsothermalUsefulWork]
```
