# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the gases measured by a 5-gas exhaust gas analyser and state the working principle used for the detection of each gas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 5-gas exhaust gas analyser measures the following gases:

| Gas | Sensor Principle |
|-----|-----------------|
| **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** | Non-Dispersive Infra-Red (NDIR) — CO absorbs IR at 4.6 µm wavelength |
| **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** | NDIR — absorbs IR at 4.26 µm |
| **HC (Unburnt Hydrocarbons)** | NDIR or FID (Flame Ionization Detector) |
| **O₂ (Oxygen)** | Electrochemical sensor (galvanic cell) or paramagnetic |
| **NOₓ (Oxides of Nitrogen)** | Chemiluminescence — NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂; NO₂* emits light |

Each sensor relies on a **specific physical or chemical interaction** between the gas molecule and the sensing element, converting concentration into an **electrical signal** proportional to gas volume fraction. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between automobile exhaust gas and flue gas analysis. Why is dry-basis analysis preferred in standard exhaust gas analysers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Difference:**
- *Automobile exhaust gas* comes from **IC engines (SI/CI)** at the tailpipe; the species of interest are CO, CO₂, HC, O₂, and NOₓ, used to evaluate A/F ratio and engine tuning.
- *Flue gas* comes from **boilers, furnaces, and combustors** via the chimney; the species of interest are O₂, CO₂, CO, NOₓ, and SO₂, used to evaluate excess air and combustion efficiency.

**Why dry basis?**
Water vapour condenses inside the analyser, dissolves CO₂ (forming carbonic acid), and fouls sensors. By passing the sample through a **water trap/condenser and desiccant**, only dry gas reaches the sensors, ensuring **stable, accurate, and repeatable readings**. NDIR and electrochemical sensors are calibrated for dry gas. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B (A) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** A petrol engine exhaust gas analyser gives the following dry-basis volumetric analysis:
$$\text{CO}_2 = 11.2\%,\;\;\text{CO} = 3.4\%,\;\;\text{O}_2 = 0.8\%,\;\;\text{N}_2 = 84.6\%$$
The exhaust gas temperature is **460 °C** and the ambient temperature is **32 °C**. Calculate the:
  (i) Air-Fuel ratio
  (ii) Equivalence ratio (assume stoichiometric A/F = 14.7)
  (iii) Combustion efficiency using the Siegert formula. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 5-gas exhaust gas analyser. List the typical applications of exhaust gas analysis. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Given:** CO₂ = 11.2%, CO = 3.4%, O₂ = 0.8%, N₂ = 84.6%; $T_g$ = 460 °C; $T_a$ = 32 °C; $(A/F)_{st} = 14.7$.

**Step 1: Take 100 mol dry exhaust basis**
$n_{CO_2} = 11.2$, $n_{CO} = 3.4$, $n_{O_2} = 0.8$, $n_{N_2} = 84.6$ mol.

**Step 2: Moles of air from N₂ balance**
$$n_{air} = \dfrac{n_{N_2}}{0.79} = \dfrac{84.6}{0.79} = 107.09\;\text{mol}$$

**Step 3: Mass of air**
$$m_{air} = 107.09 \times 28.97 = 3102.0\;\text{g}$$

**Step 4: Carbon atoms in dry exhaust**
$$n_C = 11.2 + 3.4 = 14.6\;\text{mol}$$

**Step 5: Moles of fuel (assume fuel is gasoline with average C₁₂H₂₆, x = 12)**
$$n_{fuel} = \dfrac{14.6}{12} = 1.217\;\text{mol}$$

For C₁₂H₂₆: $M_{fuel} = 12 \times 12 + 26 = 170$ g/mol
$$m_{fuel} = 1.217 \times 170 = 206.8\;\text{g}$$

**Step 6: Air-Fuel ratio**
$$\boxed{\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{3102.0}{206.8} = 15.0}$$

**Step 7: Equivalence ratio**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{15.0}{14.7} = 1.020\;\;(\text{slightly lean})$$

**Step 8: Combustion efficiency (Siegert)**
For petrol, $A_1 = 0.682$, $B_1 = 0.007$:
$$\eta = 100 - (460 - 32)\left[\dfrac{0.682}{11.2} + 0.007\right]$$
$$\eta = 100 - 428 \times [0.0609 + 0.007]$$
$$\eta = 100 - 428 \times 0.0679$$
$$\eta = 100 - 29.06 = \mathbf{70.94\%}$$

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Working of 5-Gas Exhaust Gas Analyser (Block Diagram):**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Sampling Probe** | Inserts into tailpipe; draws exhaust |
| **Particulate Filter** | Removes soot and dust |
| **Water Trap** | Removes moisture (condenser) |
| **Pump** | Maintains steady sample flow |
| **NDIR Cells** | Detect CO, CO₂, HC via IR absorption |
| **Electrochemical Sensor** | Detects O₂ via galvanic reaction |
| **Chemiluminescence Module** | Detects NOₓ via ozone reaction |
| **Microprocessor** | Computes concentrations, λ, efficiency |
| **Display** | Shows all values in real-time |

**Working Principle:**
1. Exhaust gas is drawn from the tailpipe via probe.
2. After filtration and drying, the sample is split to each sensor.
3. **NDIR:** IR source → sample cell → detector. Differential absorption gives concentration.
4. **Electrochemical:** O₂ diffuses into cell; current output is proportional to O₂ concentration.
5. **Chemiluminescence:** NO reacts with O₃ to form excited NO₂*; emitted photon flux is measured.
6. The microprocessor converts signals to **volumetric %** and calculates **λ, A/F, and Siegert efficiency**.

**Applications:**
- Periodic vehicle emission testing (PUC centres)
- BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance certification
- Engine tuning and carburetion calibration
- Three-way catalytic converter health check
- Industrial boiler efficiency audit
- Research on alternative fuels (CNG, ethanol, hydrogen blends)

---

### Question B (B) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A 4-stroke diesel engine's dry exhaust gas composition (by volume) is:
$$\text{CO}_2 = 7.8\%,\;\;\text{O}_2 = 12.5\%,\;\;\text{CO} = 0.4\%,\;\;\text{N}_2 = 79.3\%$$
The exhaust temperature is **380 °C** and ambient is **28 °C**. Calculate the:
  (i) Excess air percentage
  (ii) Air-Fuel ratio (fuel = C₁₂H₂₃, $M_{fuel}$ = 167 g/mol)
  (iii) Combustion efficiency using Siegert formula for diesel ($A_1 = 0.682$, $B_1 = 0.007$). **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the procedure for conducting an exhaust gas analysis experiment on a multicylinder petrol engine. What safety precautions must be observed? **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Verify total of dry exhaust**
$$7.8 + 0.4 + 12.5 + 79.3 = 100.0\%\;\;✓$$

**Step 2: Excess air from O₂**
$$E_a = \dfrac{12.5}{21 - 12.5} \times 100 = \dfrac{12.5}{8.5} \times 100 = \mathbf{147.06\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **very high** excess air is typical of **lightly loaded diesel engines** that always run lean.

**Step 3: Take 100 mol dry exhaust basis**
$n_{CO_2} = 7.8$, $n_{CO} = 0.4$, $n_{O_2} = 12.5$, $n_{N_2} = 79.3$ mol.

**Step 4: Moles of air**
$$n_{air} = \dfrac{79.3}{0.79} = 100.38\;\text{mol}$$

**Step 5: Mass of air**
$$m_{air} = 100.38 \times 28.97 = 2908.0\;\text{g}$$

**Step 6: Carbon balance**
$n_C = 7.8 + 0.4 = 8.2$ mol
For C₁₂H₂₃: $n_{fuel} = 8.2/12 = 0.6833$ mol
$$m_{fuel} = 0.6833 \times 167 = 114.1\;\text{g}$$

**Step 7: Hydrogen in fuel**
$y/x = 23/12 = 1.917$
$n_H = 8.2 \times 1.917 = 15.72$ mol
$n_{H_2O} = 7.86$ mol (formed but not in dry exhaust)

**Step 8: A/F ratio**
$$\boxed{\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{2908.0}{114.1} = 25.49}$$

**Step 9: Stoichiometric A/F check** (for verification)
Reaction: $C_{12}H_{23} + (12 + 23/4)\,O_2 = C_{12}H_{23} + 17.75\,O_2 \rightarrow 12\,CO_2 + 11.5\,H_2O$
$(A/F)_{st} = 17.75 \times 4.76 \times 28.97 / 167 = 14.67$
$\lambda = 25.49/14.67 = 1.737$ ✓ (consistent with 147% excess air)

**Step 10: Combustion efficiency (Siegert)**
$$\eta = 100 - (380 - 28)\left[\dfrac{0.682}{7.8} + 0.007\right]$$
$$\eta = 100 - 352 \times [0.0874 + 0.007]$$
$$\eta = 100 - 352 \times 0.0944$$
$$\eta = 100 - 33.23 = \mathbf{66.77\%}$$

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Procedure for Exhaust Gas Analysis of a Multicylinder Petrol Engine:**

1. **Pre-test inspection:** Verify the analyser is calibrated, fuel tank is filled, battery is charged, and cooling water/lubricant levels are adequate.

2. **Engine warm-up:** Start the engine and run at idle for **5–10 minutes** until it reaches normal operating temperature (~80 °C coolant).

3. **Probe insertion:** Insert the **stainless-steel sampling probe** into the tailpipe to a depth of ~30 cm and connect the hose to the analyser inlet.

4. **Power ON:** Switch on the analyser; allow ~60 seconds for sensor stabilisation. Verify zero calibration with reference gas.

5. **Load application:** Apply progressive loads using a **dynamometer (eddy current or hydraulic)**. At each load step, allow 2–3 minutes for steady-state and record:
   - CO, CO₂, O₂, HC, NOₓ concentrations
   - Exhaust gas temperature ($T_g$)
   - Engine RPM and brake load (kW)

6. **Tabulation:** Record values in a tabular form, with columns for **Load, RPM, CO, CO₂, O₂, HC, NOₓ, T_g**.

7. **Calculations:** Compute **A/F ratio, λ, excess air, and Siegert combustion efficiency** for each load.

8. **Graphs:** Plot (i) λ vs. load, (ii) efficiency vs. load, (iii) CO/HC vs. λ.

9. **Repeat** for at least 3 throttle positions and 2 different spark timings.

10. **Post-test:** Switch off engine, remove probe, allow analyser to purge with clean air for 2 minutes.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Ensure exhaust is vented to a safe area; do not run engine in closed garage (**CO poisoning risk**).
- Wear **heat-resistant gloves** when handling the hot probe.
- Keep **fire extinguisher** nearby (CO is flammable).
- Do not touch rotating parts (belts, fan, dynamometer coupling).
- Ensure analyser electrical connections are dry and grounded.
- Handle fuel carefully; no smoking near the test cell.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose 2–5 marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 100 from the dry exhaust sum** (always verify 100% after taking basis).
> 2. **Using wet-basis CO₂ in Siegert formula** — Siegert requires DRY basis; converting if given wet.
> 3. **Wrong A_1/B_1 values** — petrol uses 0.682/0.007, NOT the gas (0.642) or coal (0.751) constants.
> 4. **Confusing equivalence ratio φ with excess air %** — φ is a **ratio**, E_a is a **percentage**; they are related by E_a = (λ−1) × 100.
> 5. **Ignoring CO and HC in C-balance** — failing to add CO to CO₂ when computing total carbon.
> 6. **Molar mass errors** — using 29 instead of 28.97 for air loses 1 mark in numerical problems.
> 7. **Units in Siegert formula** — both temperatures must be in °C, not Kelvin; gas % must be on dry basis.
> 8. **Neglecting to draw a labelled block diagram** in the (b) part of long questions — 2 marks reserved.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ A **5-gas analyser** measures **CO, CO₂, HC, O₂, NOₓ**; sensors are **NDIR, electrochemical, chemiluminescence**.
- ✅ A **flue gas analyser** typically measures **O₂, CO₂, CO, NOₓ, SO₂** and **temperature**.
- ✅ Always use **dry-basis** composition; **water trap + desiccant** removes moisture.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F**: Petrol = **14.7**, Diesel ≈ **14.5** (by mass).
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio** $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual} / (A/F)_{st}$; $\lambda > 1$ lean, $\lambda < 1$ rich.
- ✅ **Excess air %** $E_a = \dfrac{O_2\%}{21 - O_2\%} \times 100$ (for complete combustion).
- ✅ **Siegert formula**: $\eta = 100 - (T_g - T_a)\left[\dfrac{A_1}{CO_2} + B_1\right]$.
- ✅ **Memorise constants** for petrol/diesel (0.682, 0.007), gas (0.642, 0.007), coal (0.751, 0.008).
- ✅ **Working principles**: NDIR → IR absorption; Electrochemical → O₂ + Pb cell; Chemiluminescence → NO + O₃ → light; Paramagnetic → O₂'s magnetic susceptibility; ZrO₂ → EMF at high T.
- ✅ **Calculation sequence**: Normalise dry exhaust (100%) → C-balance → N₂ balance → air moles → A/F → λ → Siegert η.
- ✅ **Typical values**: Stoichiometric CO = 0.5–1%, CO₂ = 12–14%, O₂ = 0.2–0.5%, HC < 100 ppm.
- ✅ **High CO** → rich mixture; **High O₂ + High NOₓ** → lean mixture; **High HC** → misfire or incomplete combustion.
- ✅ Applications: **PUC testing, BS-VI compliance, engine tuning, three-way catalyst check, boiler efficiency audit**.
- ✅ **NDIR cannot detect O₂ or N₂** (they are diatomic, homonuclear, IR-inactive).
- ✅ **Higher excess air lowers efficiency** (heat lost in heating the unused air to flue temperature).
- ✅ Always state **assumed fuel formula** (e.g., C₈H₁₈, C₁₂H₂₆) explicitly in numerical answers.
- ✅ Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in $\LaTeX$ tables, never raw `\|`, to avoid table breakage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling curve of IC engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Cooling curve of IC engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
The **Cooling Curve of an Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** is the experimentally determined graphical relationship between the **temperature rise of the cooling medium (water)** as it circulates through the engine jacket and the **operating time / load condition** of the engine. It is a key output of the heat balance test conducted on a multi-cylinder/multi-fuel IC engine test rig and is used to compute the **percentage of total heat rejected to the cooling water (Qw)** from the fuel input energy.

In KTU lab records, the cooling curve is typically plotted in two forms:
1. **Transient form**: Jacket-outlet water temperature (°C) vs Time (s) — captures the warm-up phase.
2. **Steady-state form**: Heat rejected to cooling water (kJ/s or kW) vs Load/Brake Power (kW).

### Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the engine block as a "pressure cooker" filled with exploding fuel.**
> Every time the fuel burns, only ~30–35% becomes useful shaft work. The rest is heat that must be dumped somewhere, or the metal will melt. The cooling jacket is the engine's **"sweating skin"** — cold water flows in, absorbs heat from the hot cylinder walls, and flows out warmer. The cooling curve simply asks: *"How much warmer did the water get, and how fast did it carry that heat away?"*

If you measure the water flow rate and its temperature change, you can do an energy audit — **just like measuring how much electricity your geyser uses by watching the inlet and outlet water temperatures**.

### Key Standard Metrics & Constants
> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Specific heat of water (Cp_w)** = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** (universally accepted lab value).
> - **Density of water (ρ_w)** ≈ **1000 kg/m³** at 30 °C.
> - **1 kWh** = **3600 kJ**.
> - **Calorific Value (CV)** of diesel ≈ **42,000 kJ/kg**; petrol ≈ **44,000 kJ/kg**.
> - **Energy balance closure** must yield ~100% ± 5% to validate the experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Framework (Heat Balance of an IC Engine)
The first law of thermodynamics applied to the engine cylinder gives the fundamental energy balance. If **Qf** is the heat supplied by fuel combustion, it is distributed into four sinks:

$$
Q_f \;=\; Q_b \;+\; Q_w \;+\; Q_e \;+\; Q_u
$$

Where:
- **Qf** = Total heat supplied from fuel [kJ/s or kW]
- **Qb** = Heat converted to brake work (brake power, BP) [kW]
- **Qw** = Heat rejected to cooling water [kW]
- **Qe** = Heat carried away by exhaust gases [kW]
- **Qu** = Unaccounted heat losses (radiation, convection from block surfaces) [kW]

The **cooling curve experiment specifically targets Qw**, the largest single loss (~30% of fuel energy in SI engines, ~12–15% in CI engines).

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Experiment

1. **Start the engine** without load and let the cooling water circulate until jacket-outlet temperature stabilises (steady state at no-load).
2. **Record** the inlet water temperature (T1), outlet water temperature (T2), and the mass flow rate of water (mw) using a collecting tank + stopwatch.
3. **Apply incremental loads** on the rope brake / eddy-current dynamometer.
4. At each load, allow the engine to reach thermal steady state (~3–5 minutes), then record all temperatures, fuel consumption (mf), time for fuel consumption, and load.
5. **Calculate Qw** at each load using the energy absorbed by water.
6. **Plot the cooling curve**: T2 vs time (transient) and Qw vs BP (steady-state).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Cooling Curve Experiment**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Heat supplied by fuel | $Q_f = \dfrac{m_f \times CV}{t}$ | kW | $m_f$ = fuel mass [kg], $t$ = time [s] |
| 2 | Brake power | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_b}{60{,}000}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm, $T_b$ = brake torque [N·m] |
| 3 | **Heat rejected to cooling water** | $Q_w = \dot{m}_w \, C_{p_w} \, (T_2 - T_1)$ | kW | **Core formula for the cooling curve** |
| 4 | Mass flow rate of water | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{V_w \times \rho_w}{t_w}$ | kg/s | $V_w$ = volume collected [m³] |
| 5 | % Heat lost to water | $\%Q_w = \dfrac{Q_w}{Q_f} \times 100$ | % | Typically 25–35% (SI), 12–18% (CI) |
| 6 | Specific fuel consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP \times t} \times 3600$ | kg/kWh | Indicator of engine efficiency |
| 7 | Heat balance closure | $Q_b + Q_w + Q_e + Q_u \equiv Q_f$ | kW | Must close within ± 5% |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Engine design validation**: Compare experimental Qw against design-cooling predictions.
- **Cooling system sizing**: Radiator capacity, water pump rating, and jacket flow velocity are designed from peak Qw.
- **Thermal management of EVs/hybrids**: Even electric motors and battery packs use the same energy-balance logic for coolant-loop sizing.
- **Emission correlation**: Higher jacket temperatures → higher NOx; cooling curve informs after-treatment design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 Derivation of the Heat-Rejected-to-Cooling-Water Formula
The cooling water enters the jacket at temperature $T_1$ (°C) and leaves at $T_2$ (°C), absorbing the entire heat rejected through the cylinder walls. By the energy conservation principle applied to a control volume around the engine jacket:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy gained by water per second} &= \text{Energy lost by engine block per second} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_w \, C_{p_w} \, \Delta T &= \dot{Q}_w \\[4pt]
\therefore \dot{Q}_w &= \dot{m}_w \, C_{p_w} \, (T_2 - T_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

The mass flow rate is obtained from a volumetric collection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{\text{mass of water collected}}{\text{collection time}} = \frac{V_w \cdot \rho_w}{t_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{Q}_w = \frac{V_w \, \rho_w \, C_{p_w} \, (T_2 - T_1)}{t_w}\;}
$$

This is the **master equation** students must reproduce in viva for full marks.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example
> [!NOTE]
> **Observation Table (typical KTU lab data)**

| Load (% of full) | Fuel mass $m_f$ (kg) | Fuel time $t_f$ (s) | Water volume $V_w$ (L) | Water time $t_w$ (s) | $T_1$ (°C) | $T_2$ (°C) | Spring balance diff. (N) | Engine speed N (rpm) | Brake drum dia. $D$ (m) | Rope dia. $d$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No load | 0.050 | 30 | 5.0 | 30 | 28 | 36 | 0 | 1500 | 0.40 | 0.015 |
| 25 % | 0.060 | 30 | 6.5 | 30 | 28 | 41 | 30 | 1500 | 0.40 | 0.015 |
| 50 % | 0.075 | 30 | 8.0 | 30 | 28 | 46 | 60 | 1500 | 0.40 | 0.015 |
| 75 % | 0.090 | 30 | 9.5 | 30 | 28 | 51 | 90 | 1500 | 0.40 | 0.015 |
| 100 % | 0.110 | 30 | 11.5 | 30 | 28 | 56 | 120 | 1500 | 0.40 | 0.015 |

Assume: CV of diesel = **42,000 kJ/kg**, $C_{p_w} = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K), $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³.

#### Sample Calculation @ 50 % Load

**Step 1 — Heat supplied by fuel:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_f &= \frac{m_f \times CV}{t_f} = \frac{0.075 \times 42{,}000}{30} \\[4pt]
\dot{Q}_f &= \frac{3150}{30} = 105.00 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate of water:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{V_w \times \rho_w}{t_w} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{30} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{8.0}{30} = 0.2667 \;\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected to cooling water (Qw):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_w &= \dot{m}_w \, C_{p_w} \, (T_2 - T_1) \\[4pt]
&= 0.2667 \times 4.186 \times (46 - 28) \\[4pt]
&= 0.2667 \times 4.186 \times 18 \\[4pt]
&= 20.09 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage heat lost to water:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%Q_w &= \frac{\dot{Q}_w}{\dot{Q}_f} \times 100 = \frac{20.09}{105.00} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\%Q_w &= 19.13 \;\% \quad (\text{within expected CI-engine range})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Brake power (using rope-brake dynamometer):**

The effective brake-drum radius = $(D + d)/2 = (0.40 + 0.015)/2 = 0.2075$ m

Net load $W = 60$ N (spring-balance difference). Torque $T_b = W \times R = 60 \times 0.2075 = 12.45$ N·m

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{2 \pi N T_b}{60{,}000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 12.45}{60{,}000} \\[4pt]
BP &= \frac{117{,}310}{60{,}000} = 1.955 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Heat equivalent of brake work (Qb):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_b &= BP \times 1 \;(\text{already in kW}) = 1.955 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Unaccounted heat (Qu):** After also computing $\dot{Q}_e$ from exhaust gas enthalpy, the closure check:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_f &= \dot{Q}_b + \dot{Q}_w + \dot{Q}_e + \dot{Q}_u \\[4pt]
105.00 &= 1.955 + 20.09 + \dot{Q}_e + \dot{Q}_u
\end{aligned}
$$

Once $\dot{Q}_e$ is computed (typically using exhaust gas cp × ΔT method), $\dot{Q}_u$ is the **closing balance** item. A small positive value (~5%) confirms a successful experiment.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Expected Trend

| Load ↑ | $T_2 - T_1$ | $\dot{m}_w$ | $\dot{Q}_w$ | %Qw |
|--------|------------|-------------|-------------|------|
| No-load | Small | Small | Small | Small |
| Full-load | **Largest** | Largest | **Largest** | **Highest** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cooling curve (Qw vs BP) must be a monotonically increasing curve.** Any dip or non-monotonic behaviour indicates experimental error — most commonly a leaking cooling jacket, inaccurate flow measurement, or insufficient warm-up time at that load.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Cooling-Curve Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENG[IC Engine Assembly]
        A1[Fuel Tank] --> A2[Carburettor / Injector]
        A2 --> A3[Cylinder Combustion Chamber]
        A3 --> A4[Piston and Crankshaft]
        A3 -- Heat Rejection Through Walls --> A5[Water Jacket Surrounding Cylinder]
        A6[Exhaust Manifold] --> A7[Exhaust Gas Outlet]
        A3 --> A6
    end

    subgraph CW[Cooling Water Loop]
        B1[Overhead Sump / Storage Tank] -- Cold Inlet T1 --> A5
        A5 -- Hot Outlet T2 --> B2[Measuring Cylinder / Collecting Tank]
        B2 -- Volume Vw over Time tw --> B3[Stopwatch]
    end

    subgraph DYN[Dynamometer Loading System]
        A4 -- Shaft --> C1[Rope Brake / Eddy Current Dynamometer]
        C1 --> C2[Spring Balance Pair]
        C1 --> C3[Tachometer for Engine Speed N]
    end

    subgraph DAQ[Data Acquisition Station]
        A1 --> D1[Fuel Measuring Burette]
        D1 --> D2[Stopwatch for Fuel Time tf]
        A5 --> D3[Jacket Thermocouples T1 and T2]
        B2 --> D4[Volumetric Scale]
        C2 --> D5[Brake Load Reading W]
    end

    D2 --> E[Heat Balance Calculation Worksheet]
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    C3 --> E
    E --> F[Plot: Cooling Curve Qw vs BP]
    E --> G[Plot: T2 vs t Transient Curve]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow for Calculating Qw

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Engine, Warm Up to Steady State] --> B[Set Load on Dynamometer]
    B --> C[Wait for Thermal Steady State at Load]
    C --> D[Record mf, tf from Fuel Burette]
    C --> E[Collect Vw of Cooling Water for tw seconds]
    C --> F[Note Jacket Inlet T1 and Outlet T2]
    C --> G[Record Spring Balance Readings and Speed N]
    D --> H[Compute Qf]
    E --> I[Compute mw_dot]
    F --> J[Compute Qw = mw_dot x Cp x T2 minus T1]
    G --> K[Compute BP from Torque and Speed]
    H --> L[Plot Cooling Curve]
    I --> J
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compare %Qw with Theoretical Range]
    M --> N[Validate Heat Balance Closure]
```

### 4.3 Energy Distribution Sankey-Style Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Qf - Fuel Energy Input 100 percent] --> W[Qb - Brake Work 25 to 35 percent]
    P --> X[Qw - Heat to Cooling Water 25 to 35 percent]
    P --> Y[Qe - Exhaust Heat 30 to 40 percent]
    P --> Z[Qu - Unaccounted Losses 3 to 8 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The cooling curve experiment isolates the branch **X (Qw)** and measures it directly. The remaining three branches are computed by complementary methods to close the energy audit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**A1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the term "cooling curve" as applied to IC engines. What does its slope indicate?*

**Model Answer:**
The cooling curve of an IC engine is a plot of the **heat rejected to the cooling water (Qw)** against **brake power (BP)** at various steady-state loads. It is obtained by measuring the inlet and outlet temperatures of the jacket cooling water, the water mass flow rate, and the corresponding brake power at each load. The **slope of the cooling curve** indicates the rate at which heat is dumped to the coolant per unit increase in useful work output — essentially the **sensitivity of cooling demand to engine loading**. A steeper curve implies poor combustion efficiency, while a flatter curve indicates better conversion of fuel energy to work.

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is cooling water temperature rise used to compute heat loss instead of directly measuring jacket-wall temperature?*

**Model Answer:**
Direct measurement of cylinder-wall temperature using surface thermocouples is **invasive, gives only localised point readings, and suffers from high thermal gradients**. In contrast, measuring the **bulk temperature rise of cooling water (T2 − T1)** is a non-invasive, integral method that captures the **total heat transferred through the entire jacket surface**. Using the relation $\dot{Q}_w = \dot{m}_w C_{p_w}(T_2 - T_1)$ converts this simple temperature difference into an accurate, engine-wide heat-rejection rate, making it the standard experimental technique.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for **heat rejected to cooling water** in an IC engine, clearly stating the assumptions. Mention the standard value of specific heat of water used in lab calculations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The following observations were recorded during a heat-balance test on a 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine (CV = 42,000 kJ/kg, $C_{p_w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³):

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Fuel consumed | 0.084 kg in 30 s |
| Cooling water collected | 9.0 L in 30 s |
| Inlet water temperature | 30 °C |
| Outlet water temperature | 49 °C |
| Net brake load (rope) | 80 N |
| Brake-drum diameter | 0.40 m |
| Rope diameter | 0.02 m |
| Engine speed | 1500 rpm |

Calculate **(i)** heat supplied per second, **(ii)** heat lost to cooling water per second and its percentage, and **(iii)** brake power. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]**
*(Write this exactly as in Section 3.1 — full marks require the derivation block, the substituted boxed equation, and listing of assumptions.)*

> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Steady-state, uniform jacket-water temperature at inlet and outlet.
> 2. No phase change in cooling water (single-phase sensible heating only).
> 3. Specific heat of water is constant at 4.186 kJ/(kg·K).
> 4. Water density constant at 1000 kg/m³.
> 5. No heat leaks from external jacket surfaces to surroundings.

Final boxed expression:
$$
\dot{Q}_w = \frac{V_w \, \rho_w \, C_{p_w} \, (T_2 - T_1)}{t_w}
$$

**(b) Numerical [7 Marks]**

**(i) Heat supplied per second:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_f &= \frac{0.084 \times 42{,}000}{30} = \frac{3528}{30} = 117.60 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Heat lost to cooling water:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_w &= \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 4.186 \times (49-30)}{30} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.300 \times 4.186 \times 19}{30} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{23.860}{30} = 0.795 \;\text{kW} \quad \longleftarrow \textbf{[Examiner note: check mass flow]}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Re-evaluating the mass-flow interpretation correctly:* For 9.0 L collected in 30 s:
$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{30} = 0.300 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_w = 0.300 \times 4.186 \times 19 = 23.86 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
\%Q_w = \frac{23.86}{117.60} \times 100 = 20.29 \;\% \quad (\text{acceptable for CI engine})
$$

**(iii) Brake Power:**
Effective radius $R = (0.40 + 0.02)/2 = 0.21$ m

Torque $T_b = W \times R = 80 \times 0.21 = 16.80$ N·m

$$
BP = \frac{2\pi N T_b}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 16.80}{60{,}000} = 2.639 \;\text{kW}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Module Choice]*

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **experimental procedure** to obtain the cooling curve of a multi-cylinder petrol engine test rig. List the instruments used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** During a heat-balance test on a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine (CV = 44,000 kJ/kg), the following readings were taken at full load:

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Fuel consumed | 0.120 kg in 30 s |
| Cooling water collected | 12 L in 30 s |
| Inlet / outlet water temperature | 28 °C / 54 °C |
| Net spring balance reading | 100 N |
| Brake drum effective radius | 0.20 m |
| Engine speed | 1800 rpm |
| Exhaust gas temperature | 380 °C |
| Ambient temperature | 32 °C |
| Exhaust gas mass flow | 0.015 kg/s, Cp = 1.005 kJ/kg·K |

Compute the **complete heat balance** (Qf, Qw, Qb, Qe, Qu) and verify energy closure. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Procedure [7 Marks]**
Write in steps:
1. Check water level in overhead tank, fuel level, lubrication, dynamometer cooling.
2. Start engine, set to rated speed with no load, allow warm-up until jacket-outlet temperature stabilises (steady state).
3. Apply load in steps of 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% on the dynamometer.
4. At each load, wait 3–5 minutes for thermal steady state.
5. Simultaneously trigger: (i) fuel consumption timing, (ii) water collection for fixed $t_w$ (e.g. 30 s).
6. Record T1, T2, spring-balance difference, engine speed.
7. Tabulate and compute Qw at each load.
8. Plot Qw vs BP → **cooling curve**.

**Instruments:** Stopwatch (×2), measuring cylinder, digital thermocouples (T1, T2), spring balance pair, tachometer, fuel burette, rope-brake / eddy-current dynamometer.

**(b) Heat Balance [7 Marks]**

$$
\dot{Q}_f = \frac{0.120 \times 44{,}000}{30} = 176.00 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_w = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 4.186 \times (54-28)}{30} = \frac{0.400 \times 4.186 \times 26}{30} = 14.51 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{2\pi \times 1800 \times (100 \times 0.20)}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800 \times 20}{60{,}000} = 3.770 \;\text{kW} = \dot{Q}_b
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_e \cdot C_{p_e} \cdot (T_e - T_{amb}) = 0.015 \times 1.005 \times (380-32) = 0.015 \times 1.005 \times 348 = 5.246 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_u = \dot{Q}_f - (\dot{Q}_b + \dot{Q}_w + \dot{Q}_e) = 176.00 - (3.770 + 14.51 + 5.246) = 152.47 \;\text{kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Students frequently lose marks here:**
> 1. **Unit error in BP**: Forgetting that BP formula already gives kW when N is in rpm and $T_b$ in N·m; do not multiply by 3600 again.
> 2. **Wrong radius**: Using $D/2$ instead of $(D+d)/2$ — rope diameter **must** be added because the rope sits on top of the drum.
> 3. **Inlet–outlet confusion**: Qw formula needs (T_out − T_in); reversing gives negative Qw, which is logically impossible.
> 4. **%Qw range check**: A SI engine showing only 5–8% is suspicious — either leak in jacket or temperature probe error.
> 5. **Heat balance closure**: If $|Qu/Qf| > 10\%$, the experiment is rejected — re-check instrument calibration.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Cooling curve plots **Qw vs BP** at steady-state loads.
- ✅ Master equation: $\dot{Q}_w = \dot{m}_w \, C_{p_w} \, (T_2 - T_1)$.
- ✅ Use $C_{p_w} = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ in **all** KTU calculations.
- ✅ Brake-drum effective radius = $(D + d)/2$, never $D/2$.
- ✅ BP formula: $2\pi N T_b / 60{,}000$ → directly in kW.
- ✅ Allow **3–5 minutes** between load changes for thermal steady state.
- ✅ Expected %Qw: **25–35%** for SI engines, **12–18%** for CI engines.
- ✅ Heat balance must close within **± 5%** for a valid experiment.
- ✅ Plot must be **monotonically increasing**; any dip = experimental error.
- ✅ Always **cross-check** mass flow rate of water with the burette reading (1 L = 1 kg).
- ✅ Viva-favourite question: *"Why is exhaust heat (Qe) usually the largest loss in a CI engine?"* — because of high excess air and elevated exhaust temperature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of flash and fire points of petroleum products
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of flash and fire points of petroleum products

# Determination of Flash and Fire Points of Petroleum Products

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Flash Point – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Point** is defined as the *lowest temperature* at which the vapours of a petroleum product momentarily ignite (produce a brief "flash" of flame) in the presence of an external ignition source (a small test flame), under specified test conditions defined by the relevant ASTM/IP standard.

The flash represents a **transient combustion** — the vapour-air mixture above the liquid is at its *lower flammability limit (LFL)* and is momentarily ignited. However, the heat released is not sufficient to sustain continuous combustion.

### 1.2 Fire Point – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Point** is the *lowest temperature* at which the vapours of a petroleum product **sustain combustion** for a minimum period (usually **5 seconds** as per ASTM D92) when a small test flame is applied across the surface, under specified test conditions.

The fire point is always **higher** than the flash point, typically by **5 °C to 30 °C**, depending on the product.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of lighting a campfire with wet kindling:

- When you first hold a match near damp wood, you may see a **brief spark or puff** of flame — this is the **flash** (ignition of vapour, but no sustained burning).
- Only when the wood is dry and hot enough will the flame **stick and keep burning** — this is the **fire point** (sustained combustion).

In the same way, petroleum products release flammable vapours as they heat. At a certain temperature, vapours will *flash* but not stay lit. At a slightly higher temperature, the rate of vapour evolution is high enough that the flame *sticks* — that's the fire point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Temperatures (for KTU lab work)**
> - **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC):** flash point for liquids in the range **40 °C to 360 °C** (ASTM D93)
> - **Cleveland Open Cup (COC):** flash and fire points for liquids with flash point **> 79 °C** (ASTM D92)
> - **Abel Closed Cup:** flash point for liquids **< 50 °C** (IP 170)

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Atmospheric pressure during test:** 101.325 kPa (760 mm Hg) — any deviation requires pressure correction.
- **Heating rate (COC):** 14 °C to 17 °C per minute initially; **5 °C to 6 °C per minute** near the flash point.
- **Test flame application interval:** every **2 °C** rise in temperature near the expected flash point.
- **Sustained burning duration for fire point:** at least **5 seconds**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Do Petroleum Products Flash and Fire?

Petroleum products are complex mixtures of hydrocarbons with a wide range of molecular weights and boiling points. As temperature rises:

1. **Vapour Pressure Build-up:** The lighter (more volatile) fractions evaporate, increasing the partial pressure of flammable vapours above the liquid surface.
2. **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) Reached:** When the vapour concentration in air reaches the LFL, the mixture becomes ignitable — this corresponds to the **flash point**.
3. **Sustained Combustion Threshold:** The vapour evolution rate must be high enough to continuously feed the flame, leading to the **fire point**.

The entire phenomenon is governed by the **vapour-liquid equilibrium** and the **Clausius-Clapeyron** relation for vapour pressure.

### 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

1. **Sample Preparation:** The petroleum product is filled into a **brass test cup** up to a prescribed mark (e.g., 70 mL for PMCC, 70 mL for COC up to a fill line).
2. **Controlled Heating:** The cup is heated at a prescribed rate using an electric heater or gas burner — *slow heating is critical* to allow vapour-liquid equilibrium to establish.
3. **Stirring:** A stirrer rotates at **90–120 rpm** in the closed cup method to maintain uniform temperature and avoid local superheating.
4. **Test Flame Application:** A small test flame (diameter ≈ 3.8 mm) is passed across the cup at regular temperature intervals.
5. **Flash Detection:** The instant a brief **blue flame** appears over the entire surface, the **flash point** is recorded.
6. **Fire Detection:** Heating continues. The temperature at which the flame **sustains for ≥ 5 seconds** is the **fire point**.
7. **Atmospheric Pressure Correction:** The observed value is corrected to standard pressure (101.325 kPa) using a correction factor.

### 2.3 Open Cup vs Closed Cup — Key Difference

| Feature | Open Cup (COC) | Closed Cup (PMCC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Vapour accumulation** | Vapours escape freely | Vapours are contained |
| **Flash point value** | Higher (typically 5–10 °C) | Lower |
| **Application** | Lubricating oils, high-flash fuels | Gasoline, diesel, kerosene |
| **Standard** | ASTM D92 / IP 36 | ASTM D93 / IP 34 |
| **Safer operation** | Less safe (vapours disperse) | Safer (vapours contained) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed cup** values simulate **storage/transportation** conditions (vapours trapped), while **open cup** values simulate **spill/leakage** scenarios.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Clausius-Clapeyron (Vapour Pressure)** | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$ | P in Pa, T in K | Foundation of flash point |
| 2 | **Antoine Equation** | $\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | P in mmHg, T in °C | Empirical, more accurate |
| 3 | **Pressure Correction to Flash Point** | $T_{c} = T_{o} + 0.25 \cdot (101.3 - P_{a})$ | T in °C, P in kPa | $P_{a}$ = observed barometric pressure |
| 4 | **Fire Point = Flash Point + ΔT** | $T_{fire} = T_{flash} + \Delta T$ | °C | ΔT typically **5 to 30 °C** |
| 5 | **Heat input to sample (energy balance)** | $Q = m\,c_{p}\,\Delta T$ | J | For estimating heating rate |
| 6 | **LFL Mixture Rule (Raoult's law for blends)** | $P_{total} = \sum x_{i}\,P_{i}^{sat}(T)$ | Pa | Used for multi-component fuels |
| 7 | **Lower Flammable Limit (typical)** | LFL ≈ 1 % for many hydrocarbons | vol % | Determines ignitability |
| 8 | **Required vapour concentration** | $C = \dfrac{P^{sat}(T_{flash})}{P_{atm}}\times 100$ | % | Must equal LFL at flash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** For the 14-mark problem, the **pressure correction formula** is asked almost every year. Memorise: $T_{c} = T_{o} + 0.25\,(101.3 - P_{a})$ where $P_{a}$ is the barometric pressure in kPa.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Storage and Transport Safety:** Petroleum products with low flash points (< 21 °C) are classified as **Class IA/IB Flammable Liquids** under NFPA — governs tank design, ventilation, and zoning.
- **Legal and Insurance Compliance:** Flash point is a regulatory parameter (e.g., Customs Tariff Act, PESO rules in India).
- **Engine Performance:** In a diesel engine, the **flash point of the fuel** affects cold-start, atomisation, and combustion quality.
- **Lubricant Quality:** A high flash point in lubricants indicates resistance to thermal degradation — critical for **turbine oils, transformer oils**.
- **Aviation Fuel Specification:** Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF/AT-Jet A1) has a flash point of **≥ 38 °C** — a strict safety specification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Clausius–Clapeyron Relation and Flash Point

**Starting from first principles of thermodynamic phase equilibrium:**

At the liquid-vapour interface, the chemical potentials of the two phases are equal. For an ideal gas vapour and an incompressible liquid:

$$
\mu^{vap}(T,P) = \mu^{liq}(T,P)
$$

Differentiating along the coexistence curve:

$$
\left(\dfrac{\partial \mu^{vap}}{\partial T}\right)_{P} dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial \mu^{vap}}{\partial P}\right)_{T} dP = \left(\dfrac{\partial \mu^{liq}}{\partial T}\right)_{P} dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial \mu^{liq}}{\partial P}\right)_{T} dP
$$

Using the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -s$ (entropy per mole) and $\left(\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = v$ (volume per mole):

$$
-s^{vap}\,dT + v^{vap}\,dP = -s^{liq}\,dT + v^{liq}\,dP
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(v^{vap} - v^{liq})\,dP = (s^{vap} - s^{liq})\,dT
$$

Since $v^{vap} \gg v^{liq}$, $v^{liq}$ is neglected. For ideal gas, $v^{vap} = \dfrac{RT}{P}$. Also, $s^{vap} - s^{liq} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}$ (from the definition of latent heat):

$$
\dfrac{RT}{P}\,dP = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}\,dT
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\cdot \dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$

Integrating with $\Delta H_{vap}$ assumed constant over a small temperature range:

$$
\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C
$$

This is the **integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation**, which directly governs the vapour pressure rise that triggers the **flash point**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Correction to Flash Point

The flash point varies with ambient pressure. The empirical relation (ASTM D93 / D92) for petroleum products is:

$$
T_{c} = T_{o} + 0.25 \cdot (101.3 - P_{a})
$$

**Where:**
- $T_{c}$ = corrected flash point (°C)
- $T_{o}$ = observed flash point (°C)
- $P_{a}$ = actual barometric pressure during test (kPa)
- 0.25 = empirical correction factor (°C/kPa)

**Step-by-step numerical example (KTU-style):**

> A lubricant sample gives an observed flash point of 215 °C when barometric pressure is 99.0 kPa. Find the corrected flash point.

**Step 1:** Identify the variables.

$$
T_{o} = 215^{\circ}C,\quad P_{a} = 99.0\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the pressure difference.

$$
\Delta P = 101.3 - 99.0 = 2.3\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the correction formula.

$$
T_{c} = 215 + 0.25 \times 2.3
$$

$$
T_{c} = 215 + 0.575 = 215.575^{\circ}C
$$

**Step 4:** Report the answer (typically rounded to nearest 0.5 °C).

$$
\boxed{T_{c} \approx 215.5^{\circ}C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the barometric pressure is **higher** than standard (e.g., 102.5 kPa), the correction is **subtracted** (a lower flash point is observed at higher altitudes or higher pressure → correction must be **added** when $P_a < 101.3$ and **subtracted** when $P_a > 101.3$).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Input Required for Flash Test

The energy supplied by the heater to raise the sample temperature must be controlled. Energy balance on the test cup:

$$
Q_{heater} = Q_{sample} + Q_{loss}
$$

Where:

$$
Q_{sample} = m\,c_{p}\,(T_{flash} - T_{initial})
$$

For a **COC test** with 70 mL of oil (density ≈ 880 kg/m³, $c_p$ ≈ 2000 J/kg·K) being heated from 25 °C to a flash point of 220 °C:

**Step 1:** Mass of sample.

$$
m = \rho \times V = 880 \times 70 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0616\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Temperature rise.

$$
\Delta T = 220 - 25 = 195^{\circ}C
$$

**Step 3:** Heat absorbed by sample.

$$
Q_{sample} = 0.0616 \times 2000 \times 195 = 24{,}024\ \text{J} \approx 24\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4:** For a 10-minute test, required heating power.

$$
P_{heater} = \dfrac{24{,}024}{10 \times 60} \approx 40\ \text{W}
$$

This justifies the **slow heating rate (5–6 °C/min near flash)** — too fast would prevent vapour equilibrium and give erroneous (lower) flash points.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Antoine Equation Application

For a specific hydrocarbon (e.g., n-decane, a diesel surrogate), Antoine constants (A, B, C) give accurate vapour pressure:

$$
\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For n-decane, $A = 6.998$, $B = 1508.0$, $C = 211.0$ (P in mmHg, T in °C).

**Step 1:** Find vapour pressure at T = 65 °C.

$$
\log_{10} P^{sat} = 6.998 - \dfrac{1508.0}{211.0 + 65}
$$

$$
\log_{10} P^{sat} = 6.998 - \dfrac{1508.0}{276.0} = 6.998 - 5.4638 = 1.5342
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $P^{sat}$.

$$
P^{sat} = 10^{1.5342} \approx 34.2\ \text{mmHg}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare to LFL. The lower flammable limit of n-decane ≈ 0.7 % vol. Vapour mole fraction at flash:

$$
y = \dfrac{34.2}{760} = 0.045 = 4.5\%
$$

This is well above the LFL, so 65 °C is more than sufficient for n-decane to flash.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Test Method

| Property / Condition | Recommended Test |
|---|---|
| Flash point < 50 °C (gasoline, naphtha) | **Abel / Tag Closed Cup** |
| Flash point 40 °C – 360 °C (diesel, kerosene, lube oils) | **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** |
| Flash point > 79 °C (heavy fuel oil, bitumen) | **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** |
| Highly viscous sample | **PMCC with stirrer** (better heat transfer) |
| Regulatory compliance for transport | **Closed cup** value used |
| Lubricating oil quality check | **Open cup** value used |

### 3.6 Sources of Error in the Experiment

1. **Heating rate too fast** → premature flash, **lower** observed value.
2. **Test flame too large or held too long** → local superheating, false flash.
3. **Stirring inadequate** → thermal gradient, inaccurate reading.
4. **Sample contamination** with low-flash solvent → erroneously low reading.
5. **Barometer not read** → uncorrected value differs from standard.
6. **Cup not cleaned** → residue from previous sample alters vapour equilibrium.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Apparatus — Block Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram describes the functional block architecture of the Cleveland Open Cup apparatus (ASTM D92), as physical drawings are not natively supported.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HeatingAssembly["Heating Assembly"]
        H1["Gas Burner OR Electric Plate"]
        H2["Sand Bath for Uniform Heat Distribution"]
        H3["Thermometer Holder Position"]
    end

    subgraph TestCupAssembly["Test Cup Assembly"]
        C1["Brass Test Cup Capacity 70 mL"]
        C2["Sample Liquid Petroleum Product"]
        C3["Fill Level Mark on Cup"]
        C4["Vapour Air Mixture Region"]
    end

    subgraph IgnitionAssembly["Ignition Assembly"]
        I1["Pilot Test Flame Diameter 3.8 mm"]
        I2["Flame Sweep Mechanism Horizontal Pass"]
        I3["Ignition Source Pivot Arm"]
    end

    subgraph MeasurementAssembly["Measurement Assembly"]
        M1["ASTM Thermometer Range 0 to 400 C"]
        M2["Barometer for Pressure Correction"]
        M3["Stopwatch for Fire Point 5 Second Hold"]
    end

    H2 --> C2
    H1 --> H2
    H3 --> M1
    C2 --> C4
    C4 --> I1
    I2 --> C4
    I1 --> I2
    I3 --> I2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    M1 --> H3
    M2 --> MeasurementAssembly
    M3 --> MeasurementAssembly
```

**Working Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Heating Assembly** | Provides controlled, uniform thermal energy to the sample. Sand bath in COC prevents local hot spots. |
| **Test Cup Assembly** | Holds the petroleum product; the vapour space above the liquid is where ignition occurs. |
| **Ignition Assembly** | A small pilot flame is swept across the cup at regular temperature intervals to detect vapour ignition. |
| **Measurement Assembly** | Thermometer, barometer, and stopwatch provide the temperature, pressure, and time data needed for recording flash and fire points. |
