## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define the terms **flash point** and **fire point** of a petroleum product. How are they different?

**Model Answer:**

- **Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which the vapours of a petroleum product give a momentary flash (ignite briefly) when an external flame is applied, under standard test conditions.
- **Fire Point:** The lowest temperature at which the vapours sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds when an external flame is applied.
- **Difference:** Flash is a momentary ignition; fire is a sustained burning. The fire point is always **higher** than the flash point (typically 5–30 °C above), and is measured in an **open cup**, whereas the flash point is often measured in a **closed cup**.

*(3 marks — 1 each for definition and 1 for distinction)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Why is the **closed cup** flash point usually lower than the **open cup** flash point for the same oil sample?

**Model Answer:**

In a **closed cup** (e.g., Pensky-Martens), the vapours are **trapped** in the sealed cup above the liquid. This accumulation rapidly reaches the lower flammable limit at a relatively **lower temperature**. In an **open cup** (e.g., Cleveland), the vapours **escape freely** into the atmosphere, so a higher temperature (and higher vapour pressure) is required for the vapour concentration at the surface to reach the LFL. Hence, the closed cup value is lower.

*(3 marks — 2 for explanation, 1 for correct terminology)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** method for the determination of flash and fire points of a petroleum product, with a neat block diagram. State the significance of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a COC test on a sample of lubricating oil, the observed **flash point** was **218 °C** when the barometric pressure was **98.2 kPa**. Calculate the **corrected flash point** and state whether the oil meets the **IS 1448** specification of minimum **210 °C** flash point (corrected to standard pressure). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Apparatus components** (COC, ASTM D92): brass test cup (70 mL), heating plate (electric or gas), sand bath, ASTM thermometer (range 0–400 °C, 0.5 °C subdivisions), pilot flame (3.8 mm diameter), flame sweep arm, support frame.
2. **Procedure:**
   - Cup is cleaned, dried and filled with sample up to the fill mark.
   - Thermometer is placed with bulb immersed in the sample.
   - Heating is started at **14–17 °C/min**; near the expected flash point, rate reduced to **5–6 °C/min**.
   - Test flame is passed across the cup **at every 2 °C rise** near the expected flash.
   - **Flash point:** temperature at which a brief flash appears over the surface.
   - Heating continues; **fire point:** temperature at which flame sustains for **≥ 5 seconds**.
3. **Block diagram:** (As shown in SECTION 4.1).
4. **Significance of components:** Heating plate provides energy; sand bath ensures uniform heat; thermometer measures sample temperature; pilot flame acts as ignition source; flame sweep provides uniform exposure.

*(7 marks — 2 apparatus, 3 procedure, 1 diagram, 1 significance)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify given values.

$$
T_{o} = 218^{\circ}C,\quad P_{a} = 98.2\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply ASTM correction formula.

$$
T_{c} = T_{o} + 0.25 \cdot (101.3 - P_{a})
$$

**Step 3:** Compute pressure difference.

$$
101.3 - 98.2 = 3.1\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate correction.

$$
T_{c} = 218 + 0.25 \times 3.1 = 218 + 0.775 = 218.775^{\circ}C
$$

**Step 5:** Round off to 0.5 °C precision.

$$
\boxed{T_{c} \approx 218.8^{\circ}C}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare with IS 1448 specification (min 210 °C corrected).

$$
218.8^{\circ}C > 210^{\circ}C \quad \Rightarrow \text{Oil PASSES the specification.}
$$

*(7 marks — 1 each for steps 1–4, 1 for rounding, 1 for specification comparison, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** method for flash point determination. Compare it with the **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** method in tabular form (minimum 6 points). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel sample has the following Antoine constants for its major component (n-tetradecane): **A = 6.99, B = 1744.0, C = 201.0** (P in mmHg, T in °C). Estimate the temperature at which the vapour pressure reaches **40 mmHg**, which corresponds to the flash condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **PMCC apparatus (ASTM D93):** Closed brass cup, lid with shutter, stirring device, electric heater, pilot flame mechanism, ASTM thermometer.
2. **Procedure:** Sample is filled; lid closed; heating at **5–6 °C/min** with continuous stirring; shutter opens at intervals to dip test flame; temperature at flash is recorded.
3. **Block diagram:** (Refer SECTION 4.2).
4. **Comparison Table:**

| S.No. | Parameter | PMCC (Closed) | COC (Open) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vapour accumulation | Contained | Escapes to atmosphere |
| 2 | Flash point value | Lower | Higher |
| 3 | ASTM standard | D93 | D92 |
| 4 | Stirring | Yes (90–120 rpm) | Optional |
| 5 | Safety | Higher (vapours contained) | Lower (vapours disperse) |
| 6 | Application | Gasoline, diesel, kerosene | Lubricating oils, bitumen |
| 7 | Heating rate near flash | 5–6 °C/min | 5–6 °C/min |

*(7 marks — 2 diagram, 2 procedure, 3 table)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Apply the Antoine equation.

$$
\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $P^{sat} = 40$ mmHg.

$$
\log_{10}(40) = 6.99 - \dfrac{1744.0}{201.0 + T}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate LHS.

$$
1.6021 = 6.99 - \dfrac{1744.0}{201.0 + T}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the fractional term.

$$
\dfrac{1744.0}{201.0 + T} = 6.99 - 1.6021 = 5.3879
$$

**Step 5:** Invert both sides.

$$
201.0 + T = \dfrac{1744.0}{5.3879} = 323.66
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for T.

$$
T = 323.66 - 201.0 = 122.66^{\circ}C
$$

**Step 7:** Final Answer.

$$
\boxed{T_{flash} \approx 122.7^{\circ}C}
$$

This temperature aligns with the **typical closed-cup flash point of diesel fuel (~125 °C)**.

*(7 marks — 1 each for steps 1–6, 1 for final answer and validation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Pressure correction sign error:** Students often write $T_c = T_o - 0.25 (101.3 - P_a)$. The correct formula is **+**; correction is added when $P_a < 101.3$ and subtracted when $P_a > 101.3$. **Lose 1–2 marks** for sign mistakes.
> 2. **Confusing flash with fire point definitions:** Fire point requires **5-second sustained burning**, not just a flash. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Open vs closed cup confusion:** Many students state "COC is for gasoline" — actually COC is for **high-flash oils**; gasoline uses **closed cup** methods.
> 4. **Skipping the barometric pressure correction** in numericals — this is the **most commonly missed step** in 14-mark problems.
> 5. **Antoine equation unit mismatch:** Always verify whether the given constants use P in **mmHg or kPa** and T in **°C or K**. Mixing up units gives wrong answers.
> 6. **No "atmospheric pressure correction" or "stirring rate"** in procedure answers — both are often asked but ignored.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flash point** = momentary ignition of vapours (lower temperature).
- **Fire point** = sustained combustion for ≥ 5 seconds (higher temperature).
- **Fire point > Flash point**, typically by **5–30 °C**.
- **Closed cup** value < **Open cup** value for the same sample.
- **PMCC** (ASTM D93) for fuels with flash **40–360 °C**; **COC** (ASTM D92) for oils with flash **> 79 °C**; **Abel** (IP 170) for liquids **< 50 °C**.
- **Standard pressure correction:** $T_{c} = T_{o} + 0.25\,(101.3 - P_{a})$ with $P_{a}$ in kPa.
- **Heating rate near flash:** **5–6 °C/min** — critical for accurate result.
- **Test flame application interval:** every **2 °C** rise near the expected flash.
- **Stirring in PMCC:** **90–120 rpm** for thermal uniformity.
- **Test flame diameter:** approximately **3.8 mm**.
- **Clausius-Clapeyron** equation governs vapour pressure rise that triggers the flash.
- **Antoine equation** is a more accurate empirical form used for precise estimates.
- **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit)** must be reached for ignition — this is the fundamental condition for flash.
- **Sample fill:** exactly to the fill mark (overfilling gives lower flash point).
- **Vapour space matters:** open cup → vapours escape → need higher temperature; closed cup → vapours accumulate → flash at lower temperature.
- **Typical values to remember:**
  - Gasoline (PMCC): **< -20 °C**
  - Kerosene (PMCC): **38–72 °C**
  - Diesel (PMCC): **52–96 °C**
  - Lubricating oil (COC): **180–230 °C**
  - Bitumen (COC): **> 230 °C**
- **Applications:** storage safety, transport classification, insurance, engine cold-start, lubricant grading, transformer oil safety.
- **Errors to avoid:** fast heating, dirty cup, contaminated sample, incorrect flame sweep, no pressure correction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist (must know before exam):**
> - Definition of flash and fire point (verbatim)
> - Formula $T_{c} = T_{o} + 0.25(101.3 - P_{a})$
> - Open vs closed cup comparison (at least 5 points)
> - Apparatus components of both PMCC and COC
> - Procedure steps in correct sequence
> - Significance of slow heating, stirring, and 2 °C flame test interval
> - Typical flash point values of common petroleum products

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of viscosity of lubricating oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of viscosity of lubricating oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Viscosity

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscosity** is the property of a fluid (liquid or gas) that **resists its own flow** due to internal friction between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities.

In the context of **lubricating oils**, viscosity is the **single most important physical property**, because it directly governs the oil's ability to form a hydrodynamic film between two rubbing surfaces (e.g., journal and bearing).

Mathematically, viscosity is defined through **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress developed in the fluid (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (or absolute) viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction (s⁻¹), also called **shear rate**

## 1.2 Two Types of Viscosity (KTU Favourite)

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Standard Unit |
|------|--------|------------|---------------|
| Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity | $\mu$ | Resistance to shear deformation | Pa·s (SI) or Poise (CGS) |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | Resistance to flow under gravity | m²/s (SI) or Stokes (CGS) |

The two are related through the density of the fluid:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a river vs. a jar of honey.**
> - **Water** flows easily — its viscosity is **low** ($\mu \approx 1$ cP at 20 °C).
> - **Honey** flows sluggishly — its viscosity is **high** ($\mu \approx 10{,}000$ cP).
> - **Engine oil (SAE 30)** at 80 °C sits in between ($\mu \approx 30$–$50$ cP).

**Imagine two parallel plates with oil in between.** If you slide the top plate, the oil layer stuck to the top plate moves with it, the layer stuck to the bottom plate stays still, and the layers in between "drag" each other — this internal friction is what we call **viscosity**. A thicker oil = stronger internal grip = higher viscosity.

## 1.4 Standard Units & Conversion Factors (MUST memorize for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Cheat-Sheet**
> - **1 Poise (P)** = **0.1 Pa·s** = **100 cP**
> - **1 Stokes (St)** = **10⁻⁴ m²/s** = **100 cSt**
> - **1 cP** (dynamic) ↔ **1 cSt** (kinematic) **only if ρ = 1 g/cm³**
> - General relation: $\mu \, (\text{cP}) = \nu \, (\text{cSt}) \times \rho \, (\text{g/cm}^3)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Redwood Viscometer — Why We Use It

The lab apparatus used is the **Redwood Viscometer**, standardized under **IS 1448**. It works on the simple principle of **measuring the time taken for a fixed volume (50 mL) of oil to flow through a standard orifice under gravity at a controlled temperature**.

There are **two types** of Redwood viscometers:

| Type | Orifice Diameter | Used For | Typical Flow Time |
|------|------------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Redwood No. 1** | 1.62 mm (small) | Low-viscosity oils (t < 2000 s) | 30 s – 2000 s |
| **Redwood No. 2** | 3.62 mm (large) | Heavy oils, residual fuels (t > 2000 s) | > 2000 s |

## 2.2 Construction (Block-Level Understanding)

The Redwood viscometer consists of:
1. **Oil cup** — A brass cylinder with a standard orifice at the bottom; holds ~50 mL oil.
2. **Water/oil bath** — Surrounds the cup; maintains a **constant test temperature** (via heater and stirrer).
3. **Thermometer** — Suspended in the oil to monitor bath temperature.
4. **Kohlrausch flask** — A **50 mL graduated flask** that receives the oil and triggers the stopwatch via a "click" sensor.
5. **Stopwatch** — Measures the time of flow.

## 2.3 Working Principle (Why the Time Matters)

Oil flows through the orifice under its **own hydrostatic head**. By **Poiseuille's law**, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ through a capillary is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

Since the oil column height is constant during the test, $\Delta P = \rho g H$ is fixed, and $Q = V/t$ (where V = 50 mL). Rearranging:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\pi r^4 g H}{8 L} \cdot \frac{1}{Q} = \frac{\pi r^4 g H \, t}{8 L V}$$

This shows $\nu \propto t$. Empirically calibrating the geometric constants $\dfrac{\pi r^4 g H}{8 L V}$ (along with a small kinetic-energy correction term) yields the **standard Redwood formulas**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most tested** content from this experiment. Memorize it.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | $\mu$ in Pa·s |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Dynamic ↔ Kinematic relation | $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 3 | $\nu \, (\text{cSt}) = 0.26\,t - \dfrac{172}{t}$ | **Redwood No. 1 formula** | $t$ in seconds, $t$ between 30–2000 s |
| 4 | $\nu \, (\text{cSt}) = 0.26\,t - \dfrac{172}{t}$ | **Redwood No. 2 formula** | $t$ in seconds, $t > 2000$ s |
| 5 | $\mu \, (\text{cP}) = \nu \, (\text{cSt}) \times \rho \, (\text{g/cm}^3)$ | Quick dynamic viscosity | Convenient form |
| 6 | $\nu \, (\text{m}^2/\text{s}) = \nu \, (\text{cSt}) \times 10^{-6}$ | Unit conversion | |
| 7 | $\mu \, (\text{Pa·s}) = \mu \, (\text{cP}) \times 10^{-3}$ | Unit conversion | |
| 8 | $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ | CGS → SI for $\mu$ | |
| 9 | $1 \text{ Stokes} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | CGS → SI for $\nu$ | |

## 2.5 Why This Experiment Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Viscosity data is used to:
> 1. **Select the right grade of lubricant** (SAE 20, 30, 40, 50) for IC engines based on climate and load.
> 2. **Predict hydrodynamic bearing performance** (using Reynolds equation, which needs $\mu$).
> 3. **Determine the Viscosity Index (VI)** — a measure of how stable the oil's viscosity is with temperature. High VI = better lubricant.
> 4. **Troubleshoot oil degradation** — viscosity drops indicate fuel dilution; sudden rise indicates oxidation/contamination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation, Procedure & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Experimental Procedure (Step-by-Step)

1. **Clean the oil cup** thoroughly with a suitable solvent (petroleum ether) and dry it.
2. **Fill the outer bath** with water (or oil for tests > 95 °C) up to the marked level.
3. **Fill the inner oil cup** with the lubricating oil sample up to the tip of the gauge.
4. **Insert the thermometer** and stir the bath to bring the oil to the **desired test temperature** (e.g., 40 °C, 60 °C, 80 °C).
5. **Place the Kohlrausch flask (50 mL)** directly below the orifice.
6. **Lift the ball valve** to start the flow, and simultaneously **start the stopwatch**.
7. **Stop the stopwatch** exactly when **50 mL of oil** has been collected (an electronic "click" sensor in modern units triggers this).
8. **Record the time $t$** and the temperature $T$.
9. **Repeat the experiment** at different temperatures to study the **viscosity–temperature relationship**.
10. **Measure the density** of the oil at each temperature using a hydrometer or pycnometer.

## 3.2 Observation Table (Standard KTU Format)

| Sl. No. | Test Temperature $T$ (°C) | Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) | Time for 50 mL $t$ (s) |
|---------|---------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 40 | ... | ... |
| 2 | 60 | ... | ... |
| 3 | 80 | ... | ... |
| 4 | 100 | ... | ... |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> *In a Redwood Viscometer No. 1 experiment, **50 mL** of a lubricating oil flows through the orifice in **480 seconds** at a test temperature of **60 °C**. The **density of the oil** at 60 °C is **0.88 g/cm³**. Calculate:*
> *(a) Kinematic viscosity in **Stokes**.*
> *(b) Kinematic viscosity in **m²/s**.*
> *(c) Dynamic viscosity in **centipoise (cP)**.*
> *(d) Dynamic viscosity in **Pa·s**.*

---

### Given Data (Step 1 of Solution)

- Volume of oil collected, $V = 50$ mL
- Time of flow, $t = 480$ s
- Density, $\rho = 0.88$ g/cm³ $= 0.88 \times 1000 = 880$ kg/m³
- Viscometer: **Redwood No. 1**

---

### Part (a): Kinematic Viscosity in Stokes — Step-by-Step

We use the **Redwood No. 1 empirical formula**:

$$\nu \, (\text{cSt}) = 0.26\,t - \frac{172}{t}$$

Substitute $t = 480$ s:

$$\nu = 0.26 \times 480 - \frac{172}{480}$$

Compute each term separately:

$$0.26 \times 480 = 124.8 \text{ cSt}$$

$$\frac{172}{480} = 0.3583 \text{ cSt}$$

Therefore:

$$\nu = 124.8 - 0.3583 = 124.4417 \text{ cSt}$$

Convert centistokes to **Stokes** (1 St = 100 cSt):

$$\nu = \frac{124.4417}{100} = 1.2444 \text{ Stokes}$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 1.244 \text{ St}}$$

---

### Part (b): Kinematic Viscosity in m²/s — Step-by-Step

Use the conversion: **1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s**

$$\nu = 1.2444 \text{ St} \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/(\text{s·St})$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 1.244 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

---

### Part (c): Dynamic Viscosity in Centipoise — Step-by-Step

Use the convenient relation:

$$\mu \, (\text{cP}) = \nu \, (\text{cSt}) \times \rho \, (\text{g/cm}^3)$$

Substitute $\nu = 124.4417$ cSt and $\rho = 0.88$ g/cm³:

$$\mu = 124.4417 \times 0.88$$

$$\mu = 109.508 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 109.51 \text{ cP}}$$

---

### Part (d): Dynamic Viscosity in Pa·s — Step-by-Step

Use the conversion: **1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s**

$$\mu = 109.508 \text{ cP} \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s/cP}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 0.1095 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

---

### Cross-Verification (Examiner's Favourite)

Check using fundamental SI relation:

$$\mu = \nu \times \rho = (1.244 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}) \times 880 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\mu = 0.1095 \text{ Pa·s} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Matches Part d)}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

| Temperature (°C) | Time $t$ (s) | $\nu$ (cSt) | $\mu$ (cP) | Observation |
|------------------|--------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| 40 | 1100 | 285.84 | 274.41 | **Highest viscosity** |
| 60 | 480 | 124.44 | 109.51 | ↓ |
| 80 | 220 | 56.42 | 49.65 | ↓ |
| 100 | 95 | 23.89 | 21.02 | **Lowest viscosity** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Viscosity of lubricating oil **decreases exponentially** with rise in temperature. This is why multi-grade oils (e.g., SAE 20W-40) are formulated with **Viscosity Index Improvers (VIIs)** to resist this drop.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Redwood Viscometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **instrument architecture** and the **flow path** of oil during the test, exactly as expected in a KTU lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Power Supply and Temperature Controller] --> nodeB[Outer Bath with Water or Heat Transfer Oil]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Heater Element and Stirrer]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Inner Oil Cup Holding 50 mL Test Sample]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Standard Orifice 1.62 mm for Redwood No 1 or 3.62 mm for No 2]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Ball Valve to Start and Stop Flow]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Vertical Oil Column Driving Head H]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Stream of Oil Falling by Gravity]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Kohlrausch Flask Calibrated at 50 mL]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Electronic Click Sensor Triggers Stopwatch]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Stopwatch Reading Equals Flow Time t in seconds]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compute Kinematic Viscosity using 0.26 t minus 172 over t]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Compute Dynamic Viscosity mu equals nu times rho]
    nodeD --> nodeN[Thermometer Probe Monitoring Test Temperature]
    nodeN --> nodeB
    nodeI --> nodeO[Measure Density rho using Pycnometer or Hydrometer]
    nodeO --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure — Sequential Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 Clean the oil cup with solvent] --> stepB[Step 2 Fill outer bath with water or oil]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Pour 50 mL of oil sample in the inner cup]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Insert thermometer and stir bath to set temperature]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Wait until temperature stabilizes at test value]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Place 50 mL Kohlrausch flask below orifice]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Lift ball valve and start stopwatch simultaneously]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Stop stopwatch when flask is exactly 50 mL full]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9 Record flow time t and temperature T]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 10 Repeat at 3 to 4 different temperatures]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 11 Measure density of oil at each temperature]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 12 Apply Redwood formula and tabulate results]
    stepL --> stepM[Step 13 Plot viscosity vs temperature curve]
```

## 4.3 Viscosity–Temperature Relationship — Conceptual Graph (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX[Temperature T in degree Celsius] --> axisY[Viscosity mu in cP]
    axisY --> curve1[Exponential Decay Curve]
    curve1 --> pointA[Low T High mu]
    curve1 --> pointB[High T Low mu]
    curve1 --> note1[Higher Viscosity Index means flatter curve hence better oil]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual experimental plot in your record should be a **smooth exponential-decay curve** with viscosity on the **Y-axis (log scale is preferred)** and temperature on the **X-axis (linear scale)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define viscosity. State Newton's law of viscosity and write its SI unit.

**Model Answer:**
Viscosity is the property of a fluid that **resists relative motion between its layers** due to internal shear stress. According to **Newton's law of viscosity**, the shear stress $\tau$ developed in a fluid is **directly proportional** to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient):

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** of the fluid, with **SI unit: Pa·s** (Pascal-second) or equivalently N·s/m². (3 marks)

---

### Q2. What is a Redwood viscometer? Differentiate between Redwood viscometer No. 1 and No. 2.

**Model Answer:**
A **Redwood viscometer** is a standard laboratory instrument used to measure the **kinematic viscosity of lubricating oils** by determining the time (in seconds) required for 50 mL of oil to flow through a standard orifice under gravity at a controlled temperature.

- **Redwood No. 1** has a **smaller orifice (1.62 mm)** and is used for **low-viscosity oils** giving flow times between 30–2000 seconds.
- **Redwood No. 2** has a **larger orifice (3.62 mm)** and is used for **high-viscosity oils** giving flow times greater than 2000 seconds. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation Style]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B*

#### (a) Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working** of a Redwood viscometer No. 1. State the formula used to calculate kinematic viscosity. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A Redwood viscometer No. 1 consists of the following parts:
1. A **cylindrical brass oil cup** with a standard orifice of diameter **1.62 mm** at the bottom, holding ~50 mL of oil.
2. An **outer water/oil bath** with heating element and stirrer to maintain constant test temperature.
3. A **thermometer** inserted through the lid to measure oil temperature.
4. A **Kohlrausch flask (50 mL capacity)** with a sensor that triggers the stopwatch.
5. A **stopwatch** to record the flow time.

**Working:** The oil cup is filled with the oil sample up to the gauge tip. The bath is heated and stirred until the oil reaches the desired test temperature (e.g., 60 °C). The ball valve is then lifted, and simultaneously the stopwatch is started. The time taken for **exactly 50 mL of oil** to be collected in the Kohlrausch flask is noted as the **flow time $t$** in seconds. This time is then substituted into the empirical Redwood No. 1 formula:

$$\nu \, (\text{cSt}) = 0.26\,t - \frac{172}{t}$$

where $\nu$ is the **kinematic viscosity in centistokes (cSt)**. (7 marks)

---

#### (b) In a Redwood viscometer No. 1 test, **50 mL of lubricating oil flows in 380 seconds** at a temperature of **80 °C**. The **density of oil at 80 °C is 0.86 g/cm³**. Calculate: (i) **Kinematic viscosity in m²/s**, (ii) **Dynamic viscosity in Pa·s**. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $V = 50$ mL, $t = 380$ s, $\rho = 0.86$ g/cm³ $= 860$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Kinematic viscosity in cSt using Redwood No. 1 formula**

$$\nu = 0.26 \times 380 - \frac{172}{380} = 98.8 - 0.4526 = 98.347 \text{ cSt}$$

**Step 2: Convert to m²/s**

$$\nu = 98.347 \times 10^{-6} = 9.835 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Dynamic viscosity in Pa·s**

$$\mu = \nu \times \rho = (9.835 \times 10^{-5}) \times 860 = 0.08458 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 9.835 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}, \quad \mu \approx 0.0846 \text{ Pa·s}}$$ (7 marks)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*

#### (a) Define **kinematic viscosity**. How is it determined experimentally using a Redwood viscometer? Discuss the **effect of temperature** on the viscosity of lubricating oil. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) is defined as the ratio of **dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** to the **density ($\rho$)** of the fluid:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

It is measured in **m²/s (SI)** or **Stokes (CGS)**. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under the action of **gravity alone**, which is why viscometers using gravity-driven flow naturally yield kinematic viscosity.

**Experimental Determination:** Using a Redwood viscometer, a fixed volume (50 mL) of oil is allowed to flow through a standard orifice under gravity at a controlled test temperature. The time $t$ (in seconds) is recorded and substituted into the empirical formula:

$$\nu \, (\text{cSt}) = 0.26\,t - \frac{172}{t}$$

**Effect of Temperature:** The viscosity of lubricating oil **decreases sharply with increase in temperature** (approximately exponential). This is because:
- At higher temperatures, **intermolecular cohesive forces weaken**, allowing fluid layers to slide past each other more easily.
- For most oils, the viscosity **halves for every 8–10 °C rise** in temperature.
- The **Viscosity Index (VI)** quantifies this rate of change — higher VI = more temperature-stable oil, preferred for engines. (7 marks)

---

#### (b) A **Redwood viscometer No. 2** is used to test a **heavy lubricating oil**. The time for 50 mL of oil at **90 °C is 2500 seconds**. The **specific gravity of the oil** at 90 °C is **0.86**. Calculate: (i) **Kinematic viscosity in cSt**, (ii) **Dynamic viscosity in centipoise**. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $t = 2500$ s, $\rho = 0.86$ g/cm³

**Step 1: Kinematic viscosity using Redwood No. 2 formula**

$$\nu = 0.26 \times 2500 - \frac{172}{2500} = 650 - 0.0688 = 649.93 \text{ cSt}$$

**Step 2: Dynamic viscosity in centipoise**

$$\mu = \nu \times \rho = 649.93 \times 0.86 = 558.94 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 649.93 \text{ cSt}, \quad \mu \approx 558.94 \text{ cP}}$$ (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning** 🚨
> 1. **Unit Trap:** Many students confuse **Poise with Stokes** and **cP with cSt**. Always remember: $\mu$ uses **Poise/cP**, $\nu$ uses **Stokes/cSt**.
> 2. **Formula Selection Trap:** Using the **Redwood No. 1 formula** for **No. 2** data (or vice versa) is a common 2-mark loss. Note: t > 2000 s → use No. 2.
> 3. **Density Unit Trap:** The shortcut $\mu(\text{cP}) = \nu(\text{cSt}) \times \rho(\text{g/cm}^3)$ requires **ρ in g/cm³**, **not kg/m³**. Mixing units here is a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **The $\frac{172}{t}$ Term:** Many students **skip this term** assuming it's negligible. It contributes about **0.3–0.5 cSt** in typical lab data. For full marks, **show the term**, even if it rounds to zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Viscosity** = internal resistance to flow; governed by **Newton's law**: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$
> - **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ has units **Pa·s** (or Poise); **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ has units **m²/s** (or Stokes).
> - **Redwood Viscometer** measures **kinematic viscosity** by timing the flow of **50 mL of oil** through a standard orifice.
> - **Redwood No. 1** — orifice **1.62 mm** — for $t$ between **30–2000 s** (light oils).
> - **Redwood No. 2** — orifice **3.62 mm** — for $t > 2000$ s (heavy oils).
> - **Master formula:** $\nu(\text{cSt}) = 0.26\,t - \dfrac{172}{t}$, valid for both types in their respective time ranges.
> - **Dynamic viscosity from kinematic:** $\mu(\text{cP}) = \nu(\text{cSt}) \times \rho(\text{g/cm}^3)$ — quickest route.
> - **Viscosity DECREASES** with temperature; **Viscosity Index (VI)** measures this sensitivity.
> - **Higher VI = better oil** — preferred in IC engines for stable lubrication.
> - **Unit conversions:** 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s, 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s, 1 cSt = 100 × 1 St.
> - **Plot:** $\mu$ (or $\nu$) vs. $T$ — exponential decay curve; use **log scale on Y-axis** for best straight-line approximation.
> - **Practical Applications:** Lubricant grading (SAE), bearing design, hydraulic system design, oil-condition monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of thermal conductivity of solids (composite wall/metal rod)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of thermal conductivity of solids (composite wall/metal rod)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Conductivity (k):** It is defined as the ability of a material to conduct heat. Mathematically, it is the rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient under steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction conditions. The SI unit is **W/(m·K)** and the CGS unit is **cal/(cm·s·°C)**.

Thermal conductivity is an intrinsic **transport property** of a material that quantifies how efficiently heat energy is transferred through the material by molecular agitation, without any actual bulk movement of the material itself. In the context of the KTU Thermal Engineering Laboratory, the term *thermal conductivity of solids* refers to the experimental determination of the **steady-state thermal conductivity (k)** of solid specimens — typically arranged as a **composite wall** (series heat conduction) or as a **metal rod** (linear heat conduction with lateral insulation).

### 1.2 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (Foundational Equation)

For one-dimensional, steady-state heat conduction through a homogeneous solid, Fourier's Law states:

$$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- **Q** = Heat transfer rate (W)
- **k** = Thermal conductivity of the material (W/m·K)
- **A** = Cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- **dT/dx** = Temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow (K/m)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The House Wall on a Hot Afternoon":**
> Imagine standing inside a concrete house on a hot summer afternoon. The outside temperature is 42 °C, and inside it is 24 °C. Heat *wants* to sneak in through the walls. Now think of the wall as a **highway for heat**. A wall made of *good conductor* (like copper or aluminium) is a **wide, six-lane expressway** — heat zooms through easily. A wall made of *poor conductor* (like cork, asbestos, or brick) is a **narrow, bumpy village road** — heat struggles and crawls. Thermal conductivity (k) measures how *wide* that highway is for heat.

In the laboratory, we cannot directly "see" heat flow, but we can:
1. Apply a known heat input (Q) at one face.
2. Wait until steady state is reached (temperatures stop changing).
3. Measure the surface temperatures at known distances.
4. Use these measurements to back-calculate **k**.

### 1.4 Why "Composite Wall" and "Metal Rod" Setups?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Wall** → Used because in *real engineering* (insulated boilers, furnace walls, building envelopes, refrigeration panels), heat almost always passes through **layers of different materials**, not a single uniform slab. The lab teaches students to handle the concept of **thermal resistance in series**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Rod (Linear Heat Conduction Apparatus)** → Used because metals are excellent conductors, so the temperature drop across them is very small and easily measurable when the rod is properly insulated laterally. This setup isolates **one-dimensional conduction** clearly and is the simplest application of Fourier's Law.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Values (For Comparative Validation)

| Material | Approx. Thermal Conductivity k (W/m·K) |
|---|---|
| Copper | ~ 385 |
| Aluminium | ~ 205 |
| Mild Steel | ~ 45 |
| Cast Iron | ~ 52 |
| Brass | ~ 109 |
| Asbestos | ~ 0.08 |
| Corkboard | ~ 0.04 |
| Glass Wool | ~ 0.04 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Physical Mechanism

Heat conduction in solids occurs via two microscopic mechanisms:

1. **Lattice Vibration (Phonons):** Atoms at the hot end vibrate vigorously and transfer this vibrational energy to neighbouring cooler atoms through their bonds.
2. **Free Electron Transport:** In metals, the abundant free electrons act as the dominant heat carriers, moving from hot to cold regions and colliding with the lattice.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **metals have high k** (abundant free electrons) while **non-metals, ceramics, and polymers have low k** (they rely only on phonon transport, which is inefficient due to lattice scattering).

### 2.2 Assumptions of the Experiment (KTU Standard Operating Procedure)

For valid application of Fourier's Law, the experiment must satisfy:

1. **Steady-State Condition:** Temperatures at all measuring points are constant with time (achieved by waiting ~45–90 minutes after switching on the heater).
2. **One-Dimensional Heat Flow:** Heat flows only along the axis (rod) or normal to the face (wall) — no lateral losses.
3. **Constant Thermal Conductivity:** k is assumed constant over the temperature range (valid for small ΔT).
4. **No Internal Heat Generation:** Heat is supplied only at the boundary.
5. **Perfect Thermal Contact:** Temperature is continuous across interfaces (no contact resistance).
6. **Homogeneous and Isotropic Material:** Properties are uniform and direction-independent.

### 2.3 Analytical Framework for Composite Wall (Series Heat Conduction)

When heat passes through multiple layers stacked in series (Layer 1 → Layer 2 → Layer 3):

- The same heat flux **Q** passes through every layer.
- Temperatures drop progressively: T₁ > T₂ > T₃ > T₄.
- Each layer offers a **thermal resistance** analogous to electrical resistance.

$$R_{th} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A}$$

For **n** layers in series, the total resistance is the algebraic sum:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots + R_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i \cdot A_i}$$

Heat flow through the composite wall:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{\sum \frac{L_i}{k_i \cdot A_i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical–Thermal Analogy:** Temperature (T) ↔ Voltage (V), Heat Flow (Q) ↔ Current (I), Thermal Resistance (R_th) ↔ Electrical Resistance (R). This analogy is **extremely high-yield** for KTU theory questions.

### 2.4 Analytical Framework for Metal Rod (Linear Heat Conduction)

For a uniform rod of cross-sectional area A, with steady-state temperatures T₁ at the hot end and T₂ at a distance L:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

Solving for **k**:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$$

In the **Linear Heat Conduction Apparatus** (a brass rod insulated on the sides), Q is measured using a heater wattmeter (Q = V × I), and T₁, T₂ are measured by thermocouples embedded at two cross-sections a distance L apart.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = -kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier's Law (differential form) | W |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1-T_2)}{L}$ | Fourier's Law (integrated form, slab) | W |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{QL}{A(T_1-T_2)}$ | k from metal rod apparatus | W/m·K |
| 4 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Thermal resistance of a slab | K/W |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T_{overall}}{\sum R_{i}}$ | Heat flow through composite wall | W |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 / R_1}{\Delta T_2 / R_2}$ | Heat flow ratio across layers | — |
| 7 | $\theta_m = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(T_1/T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature (for cylinders) | K |
| 8 | $Q = VI$ | Electrical heat input (heater) | W |
| 9 | $r = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Specific thermal resistance | m·K/W |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Insulation Design:** Composite wall theory predicts heat gain/loss through multi-layered walls (plaster + brick + air gap + insulation + plaster).
- **Furnace & Boiler Linings:** Refractory bricks backed by insulating bricks — composite analysis selects thicknesses to keep outer surface temperatures safe.
- **Electronic Packaging:** Heat sinks, chip substrates, and PCB laminates are analysed as composite layers.
- **Aerospace TPS (Thermal Protection System):** Spacecraft heat shields use composite tiles to manage re-entry heat.
- **Refrigeration Cold Storage:** Sandwich panels (SS sheet + PU foam + SS sheet) are designed via series resistance equations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Flow Through a Single Slab (Foundation for All Other Cases)

Consider a plane wall of thickness **L**, area **A**, with surface temperatures **T₁** (hot side) and **T₂** (cold side).

**Step 1:** Apply Fourier's Law in differential form:

$$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to separate variables:

$$Q \cdot dx = -k \cdot A \cdot dT$$

**Step 3:** Integrate across the slab from x = 0 (T = T₁) to x = L (T = T₂):

$$\int_{0}^{L} Q \, dx = -k \cdot A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 4:** Since Q, k, and A are constants under steady state:

$$Q \cdot L = -k \cdot A \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the heat flow:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for thermal conductivity:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** — every KTU lab question on thermal conductivity will ultimately reduce to a numerical substitution into this form.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Wall (n Layers in Series)

Consider three different solid layers stacked in series, with hot side temperature T₁ and cold side T₄. Interface temperatures are T₂ and T₃.

**Step 1:** Heat flow through Layer 1:

$$Q_1 = \frac{k_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1}$$

**Step 2:** Heat flow through Layer 2:

$$Q_2 = \frac{k_2 \cdot A_2 \cdot (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2}$$

**Step 3:** Heat flow through Layer 3:

$$Q_3 = \frac{k_3 \cdot A_3 \cdot (T_3 - T_4)}{L_3}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the steady-state conservation principle (energy balance):

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = Q_3 = Q$$

**Step 5:** Express each temperature drop in terms of Q:

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q \cdot L_1}{k_1 \cdot A_1}$$

$$T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q \cdot L_2}{k_2 \cdot A_2}$$

$$T_3 - T_4 = \frac{Q \cdot L_3}{k_3 \cdot A_3}$$

**Step 6:** Add all three equations (telescoping sum):

$$(T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_4) = Q \left[ \frac{L_1}{k_1 A_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A_3} \right]$$

**Step 7:** Simplify the left-hand side (the intermediate temperatures cancel):

$$T_1 - T_4 = Q \left[ \frac{L_1}{k_1 A_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A_3} \right]$$

**Step 8:** Solve for Q:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A_3}}$$

**Step 9:** Generalising to n layers (induction step):

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i A_i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerator is the **overall temperature difference**, and the denominator is the **sum of thermal resistances** — directly analogous to Ohm's Law: I = V/R.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Determination of k from the Composite Wall Apparatus

In the composite wall apparatus, one layer is the **test specimen** (unknown k), and the other layers are of **known materials** (e.g., asbestos, cork, or standard brass).

**Step 1:** Heat input from heater:

$$Q = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \approx V \cdot I \quad \text{(resistive heater, unity PF)}$$

**Step 2:** For a two-layer composite (Layer A = unknown, Layer B = known):

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{\dfrac{L_A}{k_A A_A} + \dfrac{L_B}{k_B A_B}}$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging to isolate the unknown k_A:

$$\frac{L_A}{k_A A_A} = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{Q} - \frac{L_B}{k_B A_B}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for k_A:

$$k_A = \frac{L_A}{A_A \left[ \dfrac{T_1 - T_3}{Q} - \dfrac{L_B}{k_B A_B} \right]}$$

> [!TIP]
> For equal cross-sectional areas (A_A = A_B = A), the formula simplifies to:

$$k_A = \frac{L_A}{A \left[ \dfrac{T_1 - T_3}{Q} - \dfrac{L_B}{k_B A} \right]}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A composite wall consists of two layers. Layer 1 is a **mild steel plate**, thickness L₁ = 10 mm, k₁ = 45 W/m·K. Layer 2 is an **asbestos sheet**, thickness L₂ = 8 mm, k₂ = 0.12 W/m·K. The cross-sectional area is A = 0.04 m². Hot-side temperature T₁ = 380 °C; interface temperature T₂ = 120 °C. Determine:

(a) Heat flow rate Q through the wall.
(b) Cold-side temperature T₃.
(c) Whether the asbestos layer is functioning as the dominant insulator.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Apply Fourier's law across Layer 1 (steel):

$$Q = \frac{k_1 \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1} = \frac{45 \times 0.04 \times (380 - 120)}{0.010}$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$Q = \frac{45 \times 0.04 \times 260}{0.010} = \frac{45 \times 10.4}{0.010} = \frac{468}{0.010}$$

$$Q = 46{,}800 \text{ W} = 46.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b):** At steady state, Q is same through Layer 2. Apply Fourier's law:

$$Q = \frac{k_2 \cdot A \cdot (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2}$$

$$46{,}800 = \frac{0.12 \times 0.04 \times (120 - T_3)}{0.008}$$

**Solve for (120 − T₃):**

$$120 - T_3 = \frac{46{,}800 \times 0.008}{0.12 \times 0.04} = \frac{374.4}{0.0048} = 78{,}000$$

This gives T₃ = 120 − 78,000 = a negative value, indicating the assumption of Q = 46.8 kW is incompatible. Let's recalculate using the **overall composite formula**:

**Correct Approach (Composite Formula):**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A}}$$

Compute each thermal resistance:

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.010}{45 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.010}{1.8} = 0.00556 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.008}{0.12 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.008}{0.0048} = 1.6667 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.00556 + 1.6667 = 1.6722 \text{ K/W}$$

**Assuming T₃ = 40 °C (typical cold wall temperature):**

$$Q = \frac{380 - 40}{1.6722} = \frac{340}{1.6722} = 203.3 \text{ W}$$

**Part (c):** Ratio of resistances:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_{total}} = \frac{1.6667}{1.6722} \times 100\% = 99.67\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The asbestos layer provides **99.67 % of the total thermal resistance**, even though it is *physically thinner* than the steel. This is a classic KTU concept — **insulation effectiveness depends on (L/k), not just L.**

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error & Precautions (For Viva & Record)

| # | Possible Source of Error | Mitigation Strategy Used in Lab |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat loss from lateral surfaces of rod | Thick lagging / glass-wool insulation around rod |
| 2 | Contact resistance at thermocouple junctions | Apply thermal paste; ensure firm seating |
| 3 | Non-steady state readings | Wait 45–60 min after heater ON before recording |
| 4 | Heat loss from ends of rod | End guards / water-cooled jackets at cold end |
| 5 | Emissivity errors in surface thermocouples | Use calibrated K-type or J-type thermocouples |
| 6 | Variation in heater voltage | Stabilise with variac and read V, I every 5 min |
| 7 | Ambient temperature drift | Measure T_ambient every 15 min |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Composite Wall Apparatus (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH[Heater Power Supply V and I] --> nodeA[Heater Element at Hot Face]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Specimen Layer 1 of unknown k]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Interface Temperature T2]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Standard Layer 2 known material]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Cooling Water Coil at Cold Face]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Cold Water Inlet and Outlet]
    nodeB --> nodeG[T1 Thermocouple]
    nodeC --> nodeT2[T2 Thermocouple]
    nodeD --> nodeT3[T3 Thermocouple]
    nodeE --> nodeT4[T4 Thermocouple]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Digital Temperature Indicator]
    nodeT2 --> nodeM
    nodeT3 --> nodeM
    nodeT4 --> nodeM
    nodeH --> nodeV[Voltmeter and Ammeter for Q Calculation]
    nodeV --> nodeM
```

**Functional Note:** The apparatus enforces one-dimensional heat flow by sandwiching the specimen between a heat source and a water-cooled sink, while all surfaces except the heat-flow direction are heavily insulated.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Linear Heat Conduction (Metal Rod) Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePS[Variac Power Supply] --> nodeHT[Heater at Hot End]
    nodeHT --> nodeR1[Brass Rod Section 1 Insulated Laterally]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2[Brass Rod Section 2 Insulated Laterally]
    nodeR2 --> nodeCC[Cooling Water Jacket at Cold End]
    nodeCC --> nodeOut[Cold Water Drain]
    nodeR1 --> nodeTC1[T1 Thermocouple at Section 1]
    nodeR2 --> nodeTC2[T2 Thermocouple at Section 2]
    nodeTC1 --> nodeDI[Digital Indicator 16 Channel]
    nodeTC2 --> nodeDI
    nodeHT --> nodeVA[Wattmeter Reading V and I]
    nodeVA --> nodeDI
```

**Functional Note:** The metal rod is wrapped in a thick layer of insulating material (asbestos rope or glass wool) so that all heat flows axially (one-dimensional conduction along the rod axis only).

### 4.3 Heat Flow Path Through a Three-Layer Composite Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1[Hot Surface T1] --> nodeL1[Layer 1 Mild Steel k1 45 W per mK]
    nodeL1 --> nodeT2[Interface T2]
    nodeT2 --> nodeL2[Layer 2 Asbestos k2 0.12 W per mK]
    nodeL2 --> nodeT3[Interface T3]
    nodeT3 --> nodeL3[Layer 3 Brick k3 0.7 W per mK]
    nodeL3 --> nodeT4[Cold Surface T4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The temperature drop is **steepest across the layer with the highest thermal resistance** (asbestos in this case). On a real temperature-vs-distance graph, this appears as a sharp cliff — a frequently asked KTU plot question.

### 4.4 Equivalent Electrical–Thermal Resistance Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeTH[Hot Reservoir T1] --> nodeR1[R1 equals L1 divided by k1 times A1]
    nodeR1 --> nodeJ1[Junction T2]
    nodeJ1 --> nodeR2[R2 equals L2 divided by k2 times A2]
    nodeR2 --> nodeJ2[Junction T3]
    nodeJ2 --> nodeR3[R3 equals L3 divided by k3 times A3]
    nodeR3 --> nodeTC[Cold Reservoir T4]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** When asked to draw the thermal circuit, label every resistance with its numerical value and use **straight lines** (not zigzags) for thermal resistors — zigzags are reserved for electrical resistors in mechanical engineering drawings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define thermal conductivity. State its SI unit and write Fourier's law of heat conduction.

**Model Answer:**
> Thermal conductivity (k) is defined as the ability of a material to transfer heat by conduction. Numerically, it is the rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient under steady-state conditions. The SI unit is **W/(m·K)**.
>
> Fourier's Law: $Q = -kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}$
>
> Where Q is heat flow (W), A is cross-sectional area (m²), and dT/dx is the temperature gradient (K/m).

**Q2.** Distinguish between thermal resistance and electrical resistance. Why is the electrical–thermal analogy useful in solving composite wall problems?

**Model Answer:**
> Thermal resistance ($R_{th} = L/kA$) and electrical resistance ($R = \rho L/A$) both follow the same form: ratio of driving potential to flow. In electrical circuits, V = IR; in thermal circuits, T₁ − T₂ = Q·R_th. This analogy allows direct application of Kirchhoff's laws to heat-flow problems, making composite wall analysis as simple as a series resistor network.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**A composite wall consists of three layers in series as follows:**

| Layer | Material | Thickness L (mm) | k (W/m·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fire-clay brick | 115 | 1.40 |
| 2 | Insulating brick | 115 | 0.21 |
| 3 | Building brick | 230 | 0.70 |

The wall has a cross-sectional area of **10 m²**. The inner surface (hot side) is at **1100 °C** and the outer surface is at **35 °C**.

**(a)** Calculate the heat loss per hour through the wall. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the interface temperatures between the layers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Using the composite wall formula:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A}}$$

Compute each thermal resistance:

$$R_1 = \frac{0.115}{1.40 \times 10} = \frac{0.115}{14.0} = 0.00821 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{0.115}{0.21 \times 10} = \frac{0.115}{2.1} = 0.05476 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{0.230}{0.70 \times 10} = \frac{0.230}{7.0} = 0.03286 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.00821 + 0.05476 + 0.03286 = 0.09583 \text{ K/W}$$

Overall temperature difference:

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_4 = 1100 - 35 = 1065 \text{ °C (or K)}$$

Heat flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{1065}{0.09583} = 11{,}114.4 \text{ W} \approx 11.11 \text{ kW}$$

Heat loss per hour:

$$Q_{hour} = Q \times 3600 = 11{,}114.4 \times 3600 = 4.00 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 40{,}012 \text{ kJ}$$

**Part (b):**

Interface temperature T₂ (between Layer 1 and 2):

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_1 = 1100 - (11{,}114.4 \times 0.00821)$$

$$T_2 = 1100 - 91.25 = 1008.75 \text{ °C}$$

Interface temperature T₃ (between Layer 2 and 3):

$$T_3 = T_2 - Q \cdot R_2 = 1008.75 - (11{,}114.4 \times 0.05476)$$

$$T_3 = 1008.75 - 608.62 = 400.13 \text{ °C}$$

Verification:

$$T_4 = T_3 - Q \cdot R_3 = 400.13 - (11{,}114.4 \times 0.03286)$$

$$T_4 = 400.13 - 365.22 = 34.91 \text{ °C} \approx 35 \text{ °C} \quad ✓$$

**Conclusion:** The insulating brick (Layer 2) absorbs the largest temperature drop (~608 °C), confirming its dominance as the insulator.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for steady-state heat conduction through a composite wall of three different solid layers. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a linear heat conduction apparatus, a **brass rod** of diameter **25 mm** has two thermocouples embedded **80 mm apart** along its length. The heater dissipates **35 W** under steady state. Thermocouple readings are **T₁ = 92 °C** and **T₂ = 78 °C**. Calculate the thermal conductivity of the brass. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *(See Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Summary for exam writing:)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional conduction.
2. Constant k for each material.
3. No contact resistance at interfaces.
4. Perfect lateral insulation (no edge losses).
5. Constant cross-sectional area.

**Final Equation:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A_3}}$$

**Part (b):**

Given:
- Diameter d = 25 mm = 0.025 m
- Length L = 80 mm = 0.080 m
- Heat input Q = 35 W
- T₁ = 92 °C, T₂ = 78 °C

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.025)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$A = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Apply Fourier's law:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$$

$$k = \frac{35 \times 0.080}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times (92 - 78)}$$

$$k = \frac{2.8}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 14}$$

$$k = \frac{2.8}{6.8722 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$k = 407.4 \text{ W/m·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Standard literature value for brass is **109 W/m·K**. The high experimental value indicates likely heat loss from the rod's lateral surfaces (insulation was imperfect). The percentage error is:
> $$\% \text{Error} = \frac{407.4 - 109}{109} \times 100 = 273\%$$ (an unusually high error pointing to **major experimental loss** — a common viva question on this experiment).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (As per KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Always state **W/m·K** explicitly. A bare number = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing mm and m** — When thickness is given in mm, *convert to metres* before substituting. This is the **single most common arithmetic error** flagged by KTU examiners.
> 3. **Skipping the steady-state justification** — When asked to derive Q, you must state "Under steady state, Q₁ = Q₂ = Q₃ = Q" as the basis of the derivation.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract contact resistance** — In experimental work, if the textbook mentions "thermocouples were bonded with thermal paste," examiners credit 1 mark for acknowledging interface contact resistance.
> 5. **Ignoring the parallel layer case** — Composite walls may have layers in **parallel** (e.g., wall with a concrete column and a brick panel side by side). Use **reciprocal resistance sum**, not arithmetic sum. KTU loves this trap.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — A 7-mark derivation question typically allocates **1 mark purely for listing the assumptions**. Skipping this = −1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Bullets — Must Memorise Before Exam:**
>
> - Thermal conductivity **k** has units **W/m·K**; never confuse with W/m²·K (which is heat flux).
> - Fourier's Law: $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$ — the **negative sign** indicates heat flows from hot to cold.
> - **Thermal resistance** in series **adds**; in parallel, **reciprocals add**.
> - In composite walls, the **same Q** passes through every layer (continuity).
> - The **largest temperature drop** occurs across the layer with the **largest R_th**.
> - In metal rod experiments, the rod **must be laterally insulated** — otherwise the calculated k is erroneously high.
> - **Steady state** is the fundamental prerequisite — at steady state, temperature at any point is constant in time.
> - Heater power: $Q = V \times I$ for resistive heating.
> - For two layers of equal area, $k_1 = \dfrac{Q L_1}{A(T_1 - T_2)}$ is the working formula for k.
> - **End losses** in the metal rod = error source. **Lateral losses** in composite wall = error source. **Heat leakage from edges** is the universal enemy of this experiment.
> - The thermal-electrical analogy allows Kirchhoff's Voltage/Current Laws to be applied directly.
> - For **cylindrical geometry** (pipes, wires), use **log-mean radius**: $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$.
> - Reported experimental k is always compared with **standard literature values**; percentage error must be calculated.
> - Common viva questions: *Why brass has higher k than steel? Why does k of a metal decrease with temperature while that of an insulator increases?*
> - Always draw a **thermal circuit diagram** before solving — KTU awards 1–2 marks for a clear diagram.
> - Units consistency check: $[Q \cdot L / (A \cdot \Delta T)] = [W \cdot m / (m^2 \cdot K)] = W/(m \cdot K)$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of heat transfer coefficients in free convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of heat transfer coefficients in free convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free (Natural) Convection** is the mode of heat transfer that occurs between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) when the fluid motion is induced **solely by buoyancy forces** arising from density differences within the fluid caused by temperature gradients. **No external agency** (pump, fan, blower) is used to drive the fluid flow.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Thermal Engineering Laboratory (PCIEL308), Module Experiment 2 typically refers to the determination of the **average/free convective heat transfer coefficient ($\bar{h}$)** of a heated test surface (commonly a **vertical brass plate**, a horizontal cylinder, or a heated wire) losing heat to the surrounding quiescent (still) air.

The governing dimensionless parameters that characterize free convection are:

- **Grashof Number (Gr)** — Ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces.
- **Prandtl Number (Pr)** — Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.
- **Rayleigh Number (Ra)** — Product of Gr and Pr.
- **Nusselt Number (Nu)** — Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer across the boundary layer.

The heat transfer coefficient is then obtained through the empirical relation:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\mathrm{Nu} \cdot k}{L}$$

where $k$ is the thermal conductivity of air and $L$ is the characteristic length of the test surface.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a hot cup of tea kept on a table in a still room.** No one is blowing on it, no fan is running — yet the tea cools down. Why?

The hot tea heats the layer of air sitting directly above its surface. This air becomes **lighter (less dense)** because hot air expands. The cooler, denser surrounding air **pushes** the lighter warm air upward (this is buoyancy — Archimedes' principle in action). As the warm parcel rises, cooler air rushes in from the sides to take its place, gets heated, and rises too. This self-sustaining, naturally circulating **"chimney effect"** sweeps heat away from the surface — that entire mechanism is **free convection**.

In the laboratory experiment, we simulate this by passing current through an electrical heating coil attached to a metallic test plate. The plate heats up. Thermocouples/mercury thermometers placed at specific points along the plate record the steady-state temperature distribution. From these temperatures, we back-calculate the heat transfer coefficient $h$.

**Key Physical Constants Used:**

- Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \;\mathrm{m/s^2}$**
- Stefan-Boltzmann constant: **$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\mathrm{W/m^2K^4}$**
- For air at film temperature (~50 °C): **$k \approx 0.02735\;\mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$**, **$\nu \approx 17.4 \times 10^{-6}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$**, **$\mathrm{Pr} \approx 0.71$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The free convection experiment relies on a chain of physical reasoning:

**Step 1 — Energy Input (Electrical Heating):**
A known electrical power $Q$ is dissipated in a heater wound on (or bonded to) the back of the test plate. By Watt's law:

$$Q = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{(For purely resistive heater, } \cos\phi = 1\text{)}$$

So, **$Q = V \cdot I$** in Watts.

**Step 2 — Heat Balance at Steady State:**
After a sufficiently long time (~45 to 90 minutes), the plate temperature becomes constant. This means all electrical heat supplied must equal the heat lost by the plate to the surroundings. Assuming the **back and edges are perfectly insulated**, the heat loss is only by free convection (and a small radiation loss) from the **front face**.

$$Q_{\text{input}} = Q_{\text{convection}} + Q_{\text{radiation}}$$

$$V \cdot I = \bar{h} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) + \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

where:
- $A_s$ = surface area of the test plate exposed to air = $L \times W$
- $T_s$ = average surface temperature of plate (K)
- $T_\infty$ = ambient air temperature (K)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of plate surface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** If the experiment allows the **radiation loss to be neglected** (low temperatures, polished surface, small temperature difference), the simplified energy balance reduces to:
> $$V \cdot I = \bar{h} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$
> $$\boxed{\bar{h} = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)}}$$

**Step 3 — Dimensionless Analysis:**
The heat transfer coefficient is correlated in terms of dimensionless numbers. For a **vertical plate in free convection**, the most widely used KTU-board-approved correlation is:

**(a) Churchill-Chu Correlation (valid for all Ra):**
$$\mathrm{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387\,\mathrm{Ra}_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + \left(\frac{0.492}{\mathrm{Pr}}\right)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

**(b) McAdams Correlation (simplified, for $\mathrm{Ra}_L$ between $10^4$ and $10^9$ — Laminar):**
$$\mathrm{Nu}_L = 0.59 \cdot \mathrm{Ra}_L^{1/4}$$

**(c) For $\mathrm{Ra}_L > 10^9$ — Turbulent:**
$$\mathrm{Nu}_L = 0.10 \cdot \mathrm{Ra}_L^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Rayleigh Number:**
$$\mathrm{Ra}_L = \mathrm{Gr}_L \cdot \mathrm{Pr} = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2} \cdot \mathrm{Pr}$$

where $\beta = \frac{1}{T_f}$ is the volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (for ideal gases), and $T_f = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$ is the **film temperature**.

**Step 5 — Iterative Solution (Because $h$ depends on properties which depend on $T_f$):**
Since all fluid properties ($k$, $\nu$, $\mathrm{Pr}$, $\beta$) are evaluated at the **film temperature**, and the film temperature depends on $T_s$, which in turn depends on $\bar{h}$ (from Step 2), we must solve iteratively:

1. Assume $T_s$ → calculate $T_f$.
2. Read properties at $T_f$.
3. Compute $\mathrm{Ra}_L$ and $\mathrm{Nu}_L$.
4. Calculate $\bar{h}_{\text{from correlation}}$.
5. Recalculate $T_s$ from the energy balance.
6. Repeat until convergence ($\Delta T < 0.1$ K).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Heat supplied | $Q = V \cdot I$ | W | Purely resistive heater |
| 2 | Plate area | $A_s = L \times W$ | m² | Front face only |
| 3 | Film temperature | $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ | K | Used for all property evaluation |
| 4 | Volumetric expansion coeff. | $\beta = 1/T_f$ | 1/K | Ideal gas assumption |
| 5 | Grashof Number | $\mathrm{Gr}_L = \dfrac{g\,\beta\,(T_s - T_\infty)\,L^3}{\nu^2}$ | dimensionless | Buoyancy vs. viscous |
| 6 | Rayleigh Number | $\mathrm{Ra}_L = \mathrm{Gr}_L \cdot \mathrm{Pr}$ | dimensionless | Combined criterion |
| 7 | Nusselt Number (vertical plate) | $\mathrm{Nu}_L = 0.59\,\mathrm{Ra}_L^{1/4}$ (laminar) | dimensionless | $10^4 < \mathrm{Ra}_L < 10^9$ |
| 8 | Nusselt Number (vertical plate) | $\mathrm{Nu}_L = 0.10\,\mathrm{Ra}_L^{1/3}$ (turbulent) | dimensionless | $\mathrm{Ra}_L > 10^9$ |
| 9 | Experimental h | $\bar{h}_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_s (T_s - T_\infty)}$ | W/m²·K | From energy balance |
| 10 | Theoretical h | $\bar{h}_{\text{theory}} = \dfrac{\mathrm{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L}$ | W/m²·K | From correlation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** All temperatures in Rayleigh/Grashof number formulas MUST be in **Kelvin (K)** to avoid sign errors. Convert °C → K by adding **273.15**.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

Accurate determination of free convection coefficients is vital in engineering design of:
- **Electronic cooling** (heat sinks for CPUs, transformers).
- **Building ventilation** and HVAC load calculations.
- **Nuclear reactor fuel rod cooling** during loss-of-flow events.
- **Solar thermal collectors** (glazing gap).
- **High-voltage transmission line conductor rating.**
- **Refrigerator and freezer cabinet heat load estimation.**

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Grashof Number (from Boussinesq Approximation)

The free convection phenomenon is governed by the **Navier-Stokes equations** with the Boussinesq approximation (density variations are neglected everywhere except in the buoyancy term). Consider a vertical hot plate at temperature $T_s$ in ambient air at $T_\infty$.

**Step A — Governing Equations:**

The vertical momentum equation:
$$\rho \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} + \mu \nabla^2 v + g(\rho_\infty - \rho)$$

The energy equation:
$$u\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \nabla^2 T$$

The continuity equation:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step B — Boussinesq Approximation for Density Variation:**

$$\rho_\infty - \rho \approx \rho_\infty \cdot \beta \cdot (T - T_\infty)$$

**Step C — Non-dimensionalisation:**

Let $X = x/L$, $Y = y/L$, $V' = vL/\alpha$, $U' = uL/\alpha$, $\theta = (T - T_\infty)/(T_s - T_\infty)$.

Substituting and dividing by $\mu \alpha / L^3$, we obtain the non-dimensional momentum equation containing a single new dimensionless group:

$$\mathrm{Gr}_L = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2}$$

**Step D — Physical Meaning:**

$$\mathrm{Gr}_L = \frac{\text{Buoyancy force}}{\text{Viscous force}}$$

Derivation of ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Buoyancy force per unit volume} &\sim \rho \, g \, \beta \, \Delta T \\[4pt]
\text{Viscous force per unit volume} &\sim \mu \, \frac{v}{L^2} \sim \mu \, \frac{\alpha}{L^3} \\[4pt]
\therefore \frac{\text{Buoyancy}}{\text{Viscous}} &= \frac{\rho \, g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\mu \cdot (\alpha / L^2) \cdot L^2 / L^2} = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is the kinematic viscosity.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A vertical brass plate of dimensions **0.30 m (height) × 0.25 m (width)** is heated electrically. The steady-state voltage is **60 V**, current is **1.5 A**, ambient temperature is **30 °C**, and the average plate surface temperature is **90 °C**. The plate is oxidized brass with emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.05$. Compute (a) the experimental heat transfer coefficient, (b) the Rayleigh number, and (c) the theoretical Nusselt number and compare with experimental Nu.
>
> Air properties at film temperature $T_f = (90+30)/2 = 60\,°C = 333\,K$:  
> $k = 0.02808$ W/m·K, $\nu = 18.97 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\mathrm{Pr} = 0.707$, $\beta = 1/333 = 3.003 \times 10^{-3}$ 1/K.

#### (a) Experimental Heat Transfer Coefficient

**Step 1:** Electrical heat input.
$$Q = V \cdot I = 60 \times 1.5 = 90 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Surface area of the plate.
$$A_s = L \times W = 0.30 \times 0.25 = 0.075 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Radiation heat loss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{rad}} &= \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4) \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.075 \times \left[(363)^4 - (303)^4\right] \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.075 \times (1.7364 \times 10^{10} - 8.4374 \times 10^{9}) \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.075 \times 8.9266 \times 10^{9} \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 669495 \\[4pt]
&= 1.898 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convection heat transfer rate.
$$Q_{\text{conv}} = Q - Q_{\text{rad}} = 90 - 1.898 = 88.102 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5:** Experimental heat transfer coefficient.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{h}_{\text{exp}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{conv}}}{A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{88.102}{0.075 \times (90 - 30)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{88.102}{0.075 \times 60} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{88.102}{4.5} \\[4pt]
&= 19.578 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Rayleigh Number

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Gr}_L &= \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9.81 \times (1/333) \times (363 - 303) \times (0.30)^3}{(18.97 \times 10^{-6})^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9.81 \times 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 0.027}{3.599 \times 10^{-10}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.769 \times 10^{-2}}{3.599 \times 10^{-10}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.325 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mathrm{Ra}_L = \mathrm{Gr}_L \cdot \mathrm{Pr} = 1.325 \times 10^{8} \times 0.707 = 9.368 \times 10^{7}
$$

**Check:** Since $10^4 < \mathrm{Ra}_L < 10^9$, flow is **laminar**. McAdams correlation is valid.

#### (c) Theoretical Nusselt Number and Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Nu}_{L,\text{theory}} &= 0.59 \cdot (\mathrm{Ra}_L)^{1/4} \\[4pt]
&= 0.59 \times (9.368 \times 10^{7})^{0.25} \\[4pt]
&= 0.59 \times 98.60 \\[4pt]
&= 58.18
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{h}_{\text{theory}} = \frac{\mathrm{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{58.18 \times 0.02808}{0.30} = 5.446 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}
$$

**Experimental Nusselt Number:**

$$
\mathrm{Nu}_{L,\text{exp}} = \frac{\bar{h}_{\text{exp}} \cdot L}{k} = \frac{19.578 \times 0.30}{0.02808} = 209.16
$$

**Percentage Deviation:**

$$
\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{\vert 209.16 - 58.18 \vert}{58.18} \times 100 = 259.5\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The large deviation indicates a discrepancy. Possible causes: neglect of edge effects, assumption that radiation is negligible in real experiment, non-uniform plate temperature, or experimental error. Board examiners often ask students to **comment on sources of deviation**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Variac AC Power Supply 0-230V"] --> nodeB["Ammeter and Voltmeter Bank"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Heater Coil Nichrome Wound"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Test Plate Vertical Brass 0.30m x 0.25m"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Thermocouples T1 to T6 at Equal Spacing"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Bakelite Insulation Back and Edges"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Digital Temperature Indicator with Selector Switch"]
    nodeH["Ambient Air T infinity"] --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeI["Surrounding Quiescent Air Enclosure"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Free Convection Boundary Layer"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Buoyancy Driven Upward Flow"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Cool Air Inflow from Sides"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Measure V and I from meters"] --> stepB["Compute Q electric = V x I"]
    stepB --> stepC["Measure T1 to Tn on plate"]
    stepC --> stepD["Compute average T surface"]
    stepD --> stepE["Measure T infinity ambient"]
    stepE --> stepF["Compute film T f = T s + T infinity over 2"]
    stepF --> stepG["Read air properties k nu Pr at T f"]
    stepG --> stepH["Compute Ra L = g beta dT L cubed by nu squared times Pr"]
    stepH --> stepI{"Ra L in range 10 to 4 to 10 to 9"}
    stepI -- yes --> stepJ["Use Nu = 0.59 Ra to the power 1/4"]
    stepI -- no --> stepK["Use Nu = 0.10 Ra to the power 1/3"]
    stepJ --> stepL["Compute h theory = Nu k over L"]
    stepK --> stepL
    stepB --> stepM["Compute Q rad = epsilon sigma A T s to the 4 minus T infinity to the 4"]
    stepM --> stepN["Compute h exp = Q minus Q rad by A times dT"]
    stepN --> stepO["Compare h theory and h exp and compute deviation percent"]
```

### 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (For Student Sketching)

For the KTU lab record, students are expected to draw:

```
            ↑ Cool air inflow
            │
   ┌────────┼────────────────────────┐
   │        │  T₁                    │
   │        │  ↓  (90°C)             │
   │        │  T₂     HEATED         │
   │   Air  │  ↓     BRASS           │
   │        │  T₃     PLATE          │  ← Insulation (Bakelite) on back & sides
   │        │  ↓                      │
   │        │  T₄                     │
   │        │  ↓                      │
   │        │  T₅                     │
   │        │  ↓                      │
   │        │  T₆                     │
   └────────┴─────────────────────────┘
   ←  W  →
            ↑
            │ Buoyancy-driven upward
            │ boundary layer (hot air rising)
```

- **Front face of plate** is exposed; thermometers/thermocouples $T_1 \dots T_6$ are equally spaced vertically (or horizontally depending on the apparatus).
- **Heater coil** is wound on the back and electrically insulated from the plate.
- **Variac** controls the voltage.
