## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Q1.** Define **Grashof number** and state its physical significance in free convection.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Grashof number $\mathrm{Gr}_L$ is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of **buoyancy forces to viscous forces** in a free convection flow. It is given by:
> $$\mathrm{Gr}_L = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2}$$
> **Physical significance:** A high Grashof number indicates that buoyancy forces dominate, leading to stronger natural circulation and a thicker convective boundary layer. It is the free-convection analogue of the Reynolds number in forced convection. (3 marks)

---

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q2.** Distinguish between **free convection** and **forced convection**. Give one practical example of each.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Free Convection | Forced Convection |
> |---|---|---|
> | Driving force | Buoyancy due to density difference | External agency (pump/fan) |
> | Governing parameter | Grashof number (Gr) | Reynolds number (Re) |
> | Velocity magnitude | Very low (~0.1 m/s) | Can be high (>10 m/s) |
> | Example | Cooling of a hot transformer in still air | Cooling of a car radiator by fan-driven air |
>
> (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup used to determine the **average free convection heat transfer coefficient** of a vertical plate. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a free convection experiment on a vertical plate of size **0.40 m × 0.30 m**, the following data were recorded at steady state:
- Voltage = 75 V, Current = 2.0 A
- Average plate temperature $T_s = 95\,°C$
- Ambient temperature $T_\infty = 32\,°C$
- Plate emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.06$

Calculate the **experimental heat transfer coefficient** taking radiation losses into account. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Description (7 marks):**
> The setup consists of:
> - A **test plate** (rectangular brass, vertical orientation) with an electrical heater (Nichrome wire) wound on its back.
> - **Bakelite insulation** covers the back and all four edges of the plate so that heat loss occurs only from the front face.
> - Six to eight **thermocouples (or mercury-in-glass thermometers)** are fixed on the front face at equal vertical spacing to record the surface temperature distribution.
> - A **Variac** (auto-transformer) supplies variable AC voltage to the heater.
> - A **voltmeter** and **ammeter** measure the input voltage and current.
> - An **ambient temperature sensor** is placed away from the plate to measure $T_\infty$.
> - The plate is mounted inside a **draft-free enclosure** to ensure quiescent air.
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction in the plate.
> 2. Constant fluid properties evaluated at the **film temperature**.
> 3. Heat loss from the back and edges is negligible (perfect insulation).
> 4. Surrounding air is quiescent — no forced drafts.
> 5. Radiation heat loss is treated as a separate, additive component (linearized if needed).
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1:** Heat supplied.
> $$Q = V \cdot I = 75 \times 2.0 = 150 \text{ W}$$
>
> **Step 2:** Surface area.
> $$A_s = 0.40 \times 0.30 = 0.12 \text{ m}^2$$
>
> **Step 3:** Radiation loss.
> $$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty = 95 - 32 = 63\,°C = 63 \text{ K}$$
> $$T_s = 368 \text{ K}, \quad T_\infty = 305 \text{ K}$$
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> Q_{\text{rad}} &= \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4) \\
> &= 0.06 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.12 \times \left[(368)^4 - (305)^4\right] \\
> &= 0.06 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.12 \times \left(1.834 \times 10^{10} - 8.651 \times 10^{9}\right) \\
> &= 0.06 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.12 \times 9.694 \times 10^{9} \\
> &= 3.957 \text{ W}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 4:** Convective heat loss.
> $$Q_{\text{conv}} = Q - Q_{\text{rad}} = 150 - 3.957 = 146.04 \text{ W}$$
>
> **Step 5:** Experimental heat transfer coefficient.
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \bar{h}_{\text{exp}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{conv}}}{A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)} = \frac{146.04}{0.12 \times 63} = \frac{146.04}{7.56}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> $$\boxed{\bar{h}_{\text{exp}} = 19.32 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the **dimensional analysis** of free convection on a vertical plate and obtain the functional relationship between **Nusselt number, Grashof number, and Prandtl number**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a vertical plate of height 0.50 m at surface temperature 100 °C and ambient temperature 30 °C, compute the **convective heat transfer coefficient** using the McAdams correlation. Air properties at film temperature: $k = 0.0295$ W/m·K, $\nu = 21.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\mathrm{Pr} = 0.70$, $\beta = 1/338$ K⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Dimensional Analysis (7 marks):**
>
> The heat transfer coefficient $h$ for free convection depends on:
> $$h = f(L, \, T_s, \, T_\infty, \, g, \, \beta, \, \rho, \, \mu, \, k, \, C_p)$$
>
> Total variables: **9** (including $h$).  
> Fundamental dimensions: **M, L, T, K, $\Theta$** → **5**.
>
> By Buckingham Pi theorem: number of $\Pi$ groups = $9 - 5 = 4$.
>
> Choosing repeating variables: $L, \, k, \, \rho, \, \mu, \, \Delta T$ (or $g$).
>
> The four dimensionless groups obtained are:
>
> 1. **Nusselt Number** — $\Pi_1 = \dfrac{h L}{k}$
> 2. **Grashof Number** — $\Pi_2 = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$
> 3. **Prandtl Number** — $\Pi_3 = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$
> 4. **Temperature ratio / buoyancy parameter** — often absorbed into Gr.
>
> Therefore the functional relationship is:
> $$\boxed{\mathrm{Nu} = f(\mathrm{Gr}, \, \mathrm{Pr})}$$
> or equivalently
> $$\boxed{\mathrm{Nu} = f(\mathrm{Ra}, \, \mathrm{Pr})}$$
>
> where $\mathrm{Ra} = \mathrm{Gr} \cdot \mathrm{Pr}$.
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1:** Film temperature.
> $$T_f = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2} = \frac{100 + 30}{2} = 65\,°C = 338 \text{ K}$$
>
> **Step 2:** Grashof number.
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \mathrm{Gr}_L &= \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2} \\
> &= \frac{9.81 \times (1/338) \times 70 \times (0.50)^3}{(21.0 \times 10^{-6})^2} \\
> &= \frac{9.81 \times 2.959 \times 10^{-3} \times 70 \times 0.125}{4.41 \times 10^{-10}} \\
> &= \frac{0.254}{4.41 \times 10^{-10}} \\
> &= 5.76 \times 10^{8}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 3:** Rayleigh number.
> $$\mathrm{Ra}_L = \mathrm{Gr}_L \cdot \mathrm{Pr} = 5.76 \times 10^{8} \times 0.70 = 4.03 \times 10^{8}$$
>
> Since $10^4 < \mathrm{Ra}_L < 10^9$, **laminar flow** → use McAdams correlation.
>
> **Step 4:** Nusselt number.
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \mathrm{Nu}_L &= 0.59 \cdot \mathrm{Ra}_L^{1/4} \\
> &= 0.59 \times (4.03 \times 10^{8})^{0.25} \\
> &= 0.59 \times 141.6 \\
> &= 83.5
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 5:** Heat transfer coefficient.
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \bar{h} &= \frac{\mathrm{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{83.5 \times 0.0295}{0.50} \\
> &= \frac{2.463}{0.50}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> $$\boxed{\bar{h} = 4.93 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Temperature unit trap (–1 mark):** Students often substitute $(T_s - T_\infty)$ in °C while $\beta$ requires Kelvin. This causes a huge error.
> 2. **Forgetting radiation (–2 marks):** Always subtract radiation loss when $\varepsilon$ is given; otherwise you overestimate $\bar{h}_{\text{exp}}$.
> 3. **Wrong correlation regime (–2 marks):** Students use the turbulent formula $\mathrm{Nu} = 0.10\,\mathrm{Ra}^{1/3}$ even when $\mathrm{Ra} < 10^9$.
> 4. **Property at wrong temperature (–2 marks):** All air properties must be evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_f$, NOT at $T_\infty$ or $T_s$.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Plate area must be in **m²** (not cm²) and length in **m**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Free Convection Experiment:**
>
> - **Aim:** Determine $\bar{h}_{\text{exp}}$ for a heated vertical plate in still air and compare with $\bar{h}_{\text{theory}}$ from empirical correlations.
> - **Apparatus:** Vertical brass plate, heater coil, variac, V/I meters, thermocouples/mercury thermometers, insulation, enclosure.
> - **Key measurement:** Average plate temperature $T_s$ (from 6 thermocouples equally spaced), ambient $T_\infty$, voltage, current.
> - **Energy balance:** $V \cdot I = \bar{h} A_s (T_s - T_\infty) + \varepsilon \sigma A_s (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$.
> - **Dimensionless groups (in order of importance):**
>   * $\mathrm{Gr}_L = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ — buoyancy/viscous.
>   * $\mathrm{Pr} = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k}$ — for air $\approx 0.71$.
>   * $\mathrm{Ra}_L = \mathrm{Gr}_L \cdot \mathrm{Pr}$.
>   * $\mathrm{Nu}_L = \dfrac{\bar{h} L}{k}$.
> - **Correlations to memorize:**
>   * **Vertical plate, laminar** ($10^4 < \mathrm{Ra} < 10^9$): $\mathrm{Nu} = 0.59\,\mathrm{Ra}^{1/4}$.
>   * **Vertical plate, turbulent** ($\mathrm{Ra} > 10^9$): $\mathrm{Nu} = 0.10\,\mathrm{Ra}^{1/3}$.
>   * **All-range** (Churchill-Chu): see Section 2.
>   * **Horizontal cylinder** (often asked): $\mathrm{Nu}_D = 0.53 \left(\mathrm{Ra}_D\right)^{1/4}$ for $10^4 < \mathrm{Ra}_D < 10^7$.
> - **Procedure:**
>   1. Set variac, wait 60–90 min for steady state.
>  2. Record V, I, $T_1 \dots T_6$, $T_\infty$.
>  3. Compute $\bar{T_s}$, then $\Delta T$, then $Q$.
>  4. Compute $\bar{h}_{\text{exp}}$ from energy balance.
>  5. Compute $\mathrm{Ra}$, then $\mathrm{Nu}$, then $\bar{h}_{\text{theory}}$.
>  6. Plot **Nu vs Ra** on log-log paper (should be a straight line for laminar).
> - **Sources of error:** Heat leakage through insulation, non-uniform plate temperature, draughts in the lab, inaccurate thermocouple calibration, neglecting radiation at higher temperatures.
> - **Viva-favourite questions:** "Why is Prandtl number for air ≈ 0.7?", "What would happen if the plate were horizontal instead of vertical?", "Why does Nu increase with Ra?", "What is the limiting case Ra → 0?".
> - **Result presentation:** Always tabulate **3 sets** of readings (e.g., low, medium, high heater input). Plot $\log \bar{h}$ vs $\log \Delta T$ — slope should be ~0.25 for laminar.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of emissivity of a specimen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL308 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of emissivity of a specimen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Determination of Emissivity of a Specimen

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Emissivity (ε)** is defined as the ratio of the thermal radiation intensity emitted by a real (gray or non-black) surface to the thermal radiation intensity emitted by a perfect **blackbody** at the same thermodynamic temperature, under identical geometric and spectral conditions.

Mathematically, the monochromatic (spectral) emissivity is:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \frac{E_{\lambda,\text{surface}}(T)}{E_{\lambda,\text{blackbody}}(T)}$$

For a **gray body** (where emissivity is constant over all wavelengths of interest), the total hemispherical emissivity simplifies to:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_{\text{surface}}(T)}{E_{\text{blackbody}}(T)} = \frac{E_{\text{surface}}(T)}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

where **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the Thermal Engineering Lab (PCIEL308), Experiment 2 deals with the **experimental determination of total emissivity** of a given test specimen (typically a metal disc such as brass, copper, or aluminum) using an **emissivity measurement apparatus** that compares radiation from the specimen against a reference blackbody cavity at identical temperature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two identical pots of water being heated on identical stoves at the same temperature. One pot is a perfectly black, matte surface (a *blackbody*), and the other pot is a shiny, polished metal pot (a *real surface*). Both are at the same temperature, but the **black pot glows brighter and radiates far more heat** than the shiny one.

The ratio of the heat given off by the shiny pot to the heat given off by the black pot, when both are at the same temperature, is the **emissivity** of the shiny pot's material. A perfect blackbody has **ε = 1**, and a perfectly reflective (ideal) surface has **ε = 0**. Real engineering materials lie somewhere in between — typically between **0.05 (polished silver)** and **0.95 (soot, oxidized copper)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant: σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
> - **Emissivity range:** 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1 (dimensionless)
> - **Blackbody emissivity:** ε = 1
> - **Typical polished metal emissivity:** 0.03 – 0.10
> - **Typical oxidized/rough surface emissivity:** 0.60 – 0.95
> - **Wien's displacement constant:** b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K

### 1.3 Significance in Engineering

Emissivity is a critical thermophysical property governing **radiative heat transfer** in:
- **Furnace and boiler design** (heat loss calculations)
- **Thermal insulation** (selection of low-emissivity foils)
- **Spacecraft thermal control** (radiator coatings)
- **Infrared thermography** (temperature measurement correction)
- **Combustion chambers** and **re-entry vehicle heat shields**

The fundamental governing law is the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law of Thermal Radiation**:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

For a real surface, the actual emitted energy is:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The experiment rests upon three foundational laws of thermal radiation, each of which must be understood before interpreting lab readings.

### 2.1.1 Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation

Planck's law describes the spectral distribution of radiation emitted by a blackbody at temperature T. The spectral emissive power per unit wavelength is:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda k T}\right) - 1 \right]^{-1}$$

where:
- **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** (Planck's constant)
- **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** (speed of light in vacuum)
- **k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (Boltzmann constant)
- **λ** = wavelength in meters
- **T** = absolute temperature in Kelvin

Integrating Planck's law over all wavelengths from 0 to ∞ yields the Stefan–Boltzmann law, providing the **theoretical bridge** from spectral theory to the total energy used in the lab.

### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which blackbody radiation peaks is inversely proportional to absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

This tells the student *which part of the spectrum* dominates at a given specimen temperature and is important when selecting detector optics (thermopile or thermistor).

### 2.1.3 Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation

Kirchhoff's law (at thermal equilibrium) states that for a body in radiative equilibrium with its surroundings:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

where α is the spectral absorptivity. **A good emitter is a good absorber; a poor emitter is a poor absorber.** This is the physical reason why **ε = 1 for a perfect blackbody** — it absorbs 100% of incident radiation at every wavelength, so by Kirchhoff's law, it must also emit 100% of the theoretical blackbody spectrum.

## 2.2 Operating Principle of the Emissivity Measurement Apparatus

The standard lab apparatus used at KTU (such as the **"Heat Transfer through Radiation"** setup by manufacturers like Edutek, P.A. Hilton, or ARMFIELD) operates on the **comparison method**:

1. A flat test specimen (disc) is mounted in a temperature-controlled heating chamber.
2. Adjacent to (or behind) the specimen is a **cavity machined into a high-emissivity block**, which approximates a blackbody source (ε ≈ 1).
3. Both the specimen and the blackbody are heated to the *same* temperature T (measured by thermocouples or RTDs).
4. A **radiation detector (thermopile)** is mounted on a sliding carriage so it can be positioned to "see" either the specimen or the blackbody.
5. The detector output (in mV) is proportional to the incident radiative flux.
6. **Emissivity = (Detector reading on specimen) / (Detector reading on blackbody)**, since at identical T the σT⁴ factors cancel.

## 2.3 Why "Why" and "How" — Reasoning Chain

- **Why** use a blackbody cavity? — Because it provides a *built-in* reference at ε = 1, eliminating the need to compute σT⁴ absolutely.
- **Why** keep both surfaces at the same T? — Emissivity is *temperature-dependent*; comparison only makes sense at matched T.
- **Why** use a thermopile? — It has a flat spectral response across a wide infrared band, integrating energy across wavelengths, suitable for *total* (not spectral) emissivity.
- **How** does the detector give a voltage? — Multiple thermocouple junctions in series (a thermopile) generate a Seebeck EMF proportional to the temperature difference between hot junctions (heated by absorbed radiation) and cold junctions (held at ambient).
- **How** are losses controlled? — The apparatus uses water-cooled shields, polished reflectors, and a small solid angle to ensure the detector "sees" only the target surface.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are high-yield. Memorize the boxed equations and the units.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in Lab |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan–Boltzmann law | Blackbody emissive power |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Real surface emission | Specimen emissive power |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = E / E_b$ | Definition of emissivity | Direct calculation |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = V_s / V_b$ | Detector voltage ratio | Lab measurement form |
| 5 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wien's law | Spectrum peak |
| 6 | $q_{\text{net}} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^{4} - T_{\text{sur}}^{4})$ | Net radiation to surroundings | Cooling corrections |
| 7 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance (opaque: τ=0, α+ρ=1) | Kirchhoff link |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Kirchhoff's law (at equilibrium) | Justifies ε measurement |
| 9 | $T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$ | K–°C conversion | Always convert to Kelvin |
| 10 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical heater power | Energy input check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions Used in Lab:**
> - Opaque specimen: $\tau_{\lambda} = 0$ → $\alpha_{\lambda} + \rho_{\lambda} = 1$
> - Specular vs. diffuse surfaces: Emissivity is *directional*; lab measures *normal* or *hemispherical* value
> - Steady state: dT/dt = 0 (achieved when thermocouple readings stabilize for 5–10 min)
> - Surroundings at ~300 K: $T_{\text{sur}} \ll T_s$ → correction often < 1% at high T

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial furnace linings:** Refractory bricks are chosen for high ε (~0.85–0.95) to maximize radiative heat transfer to the charge.
- **Low-emissivity (Low-E) window glass:** Coatings with ε ≈ 0.05 reflect infrared radiation, drastically cutting heat loss in buildings.
- **Solar absorbers:** Selective surfaces have high α for solar wavelengths (≤ 2.5 μm) but low ε in IR (≥ 2.5 μm) to retain absorbed heat.
- **Infrared pyrometers:** Correct temperature readings by multiplying measured radiance by 1/ε.
- **Reusable spacecraft:** Ceramic tiles are designed with controlled ε to manage re-entry heat dissipation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Stefan–Boltzmann Law to the Emissivity Formula

This is the **master derivation** a KTU student must be able to reproduce on paper.

### Step 1 — Start with the Stefan–Boltzmann Law

By integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths (0 to ∞) and over the entire hemisphere of emission, the total energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a blackbody at absolute temperature T is:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{\text{hemisphere}} E_{b\lambda} \cos\theta \, d\omega \, d\lambda$$

Carrying out the integration (a standard result) gives the famous **fourth-power law**:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

where the constant σ has the experimentally determined value **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**.

### Step 2 — Define Emissivity for a Real Surface

For a real (non-black) surface, we empirically introduce the dimensionless emissivity ε as the *ratio* of its emitted power to that of a blackbody at the same temperature:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_{\text{surface}}(T)}{E_b(T)} = \frac{E_{\text{surface}}(T)}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

Rearranging:

$$E_{\text{surface}}(T) = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$$

### Step 3 — Apply to the Lab Configuration

In the experiment, two surfaces sit side by side at the same temperature T:

- **Specimen disc** emits: $E_s = \varepsilon_s \, \sigma \, T^{4}$
- **Blackbody cavity** emits: $E_b = (1) \, \sigma \, T^{4} = \sigma T^{4}$

### Step 4 — Replace Energy Terms with Detector Voltages

The thermopile detector output voltage V is *linearly proportional* to the radiative flux it absorbs:

$$V = k \, E$$

where **k** is the detector's responsivity (V·m²/W). Since the same detector views both surfaces at the same geometry, **k is identical** for both measurements.

Therefore:
- $V_s = k \cdot \varepsilon_s \cdot \sigma T^{4}$
- $V_b = k \cdot 1 \cdot \sigma T^{4}$

### Step 5 — Form the Ratio (the Key Lab Result)

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{V_s}{V_b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master equation the student writes in the lab report.** Notice that **σ, T, and k all cancel** — we don't need to know the absolute temperature, only that both surfaces are at the same T when measured.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Set of Calculations for a Typical Lab Run

Below is a worked example matching the kind of data a KTU student would record. Assume the specimen is a **brass disc** and the apparatus is the standard Edutek/Hilton emissivity rig.

### Observed Data (Sample Values)

| Trial | Temperature T (°C) | Detector voltage on blackbody V_b (mV) | Detector voltage on specimen V_s (mV) |
|-------|--------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | 60 | 8.5 | 2.4 |
| 2 | 80 | 14.2 | 4.1 |
| 3 | 100 | 22.3 | 6.5 |
| 4 | 120 | 33.1 | 9.7 |
| 5 | 140 | 47.2 | 13.8 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation (per row)

**Row 1: T = 60 °C**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_s}{V_b} = \frac{2.4}{8.5} = 0.282$$

**Row 2: T = 80 °C**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{4.1}{14.2} = 0.289$$

**Row 3: T = 100 °C**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6.5}{22.3} = 0.291$$

**Row 4: T = 120 °C**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{9.7}{33.1} = 0.293$$

**Row 5: T = 140 °C**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{13.8}{47.2} = 0.292$$

### Mean Emissivity

$$\bar{\varepsilon} = \frac{0.282 + 0.289 + 0.291 + 0.293 + 0.292}{5} = \frac{1.447}{5} = 0.2894$$

### Converting to Kelvin (for plot)

$$T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$$

- 60 °C → 333.15 K
- 80 °C → 353.15 K
- 100 °C → 373.15 K
- 120 °C → 393.15 K
- 140 °C → 413.15 K

### Verification via Stefan–Boltzmann Cross-Check

At T = 100 °C = 373.15 K, blackbody emissive power:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (373.15)^{4}$$

$$= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.937 \times 10^{10} = 1098 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

Specimen emissive power:

$$E_s = \varepsilon \cdot E_b = 0.291 \times 1098 = 319.5 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

This cross-check confirms the *physical magnitude* of the result. (A polished brass at ~100 °C should emit on the order of 300–500 W/m² — our calculation is consistent.)

### Temperature-Dependence Discussion

The slight rise in ε with T (0.282 → 0.293) is a known physical phenomenon: for many metals, ε increases with temperature because of enhanced surface oxidation and micro-roughness effects. A KTU student should plot ε vs T and discuss the trend in the viva.

## 3.3 Numerical Sanity Checks a Student Should Perform

- **Check 1:** ε should lie between **0 and 1**. If it exceeds 1, the specimen may have been hotter than the reference, or detector drift occurred.
- **Check 2:** Repeatability — three readings at the same T should agree within ±5%.
- **Check 3:** Comparison with standard tables — for *polished brass*, ε ≈ 0.05; for *oxidized brass*, ε ≈ 0.60. A mid-range value (0.28) is consistent with *lightly tarnished / machined brass*.
- **Check 4:** Graph ε vs T should be a slowly rising curve, not erratic.

## 3.4 Sources of Error and Their Magnitudes

| Source | Typical Magnitude | Mitigation |
|--------|-------------------|------------|
| Temperature mismatch between specimen and blackbody | ± 2 K → ± 0.5% error | Use matched thermocouples; allow long stabilization |
| Detector drift / ambient temperature change | ± 1–3% | Re-zero before each reading |
| Specimen oxidation during heating | Changes ε over time | Use fresh polished specimen each trial |
| Reflected radiation from surroundings | ± 1–2% | Water-cooled radiation shields |
| Conduction losses from specimen edges | ± 0.5% | Guard heater on edges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Emissivity Apparatus

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the emissivity measurement setup, the detector switching logic, and the data reduction pipeline. (Lab apparatus are notoriously hard to render in Mermaid, so the architecture flow below is the recommended substitute.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply and Control Unit] --> B[Heater Element]
    B --> C[Specimen Disc Mounted on Hot Plate]
    B --> D[Blackbody Reference Cavity]
    C --> E1[Thermocouple Type K on Specimen]
    D --> E2[Thermocouple Type K on Blackbody]
    E1 --> F1[Temperature Indicator 1 in Degree C]
    E2 --> F2[Temperature Indicator 2 in Degree C]
    F1 --> G{Temperature Match Check}
    F2 --> G
    G -->|Matched within 1 K| H[Sliding Detector Carriage Positioned]
    G -->|Mismatch Greater than 1 K| A
    H -->|Position A| I[Detector Aims at Specimen]
    H -->|Position B| J[Detector Aims at Blackbody Cavity]
    I --> K[Thermopile Detector Generates EMF]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Digital Millivoltmeter Readout]
    L --> M[Compute Emissivity epsilon equals Vs divided by Vb]
    M --> N[Tabulate epsilon versus Temperature in Kelvin]
    N --> O[Plot Graph and Compare with Standard Tables]
    O --> P[Result: Emissivity of Specimen]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the Detector and Optical Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE[Radiation Source]
        S1[Specimen Disc]
        S2[Blackbody Cavity]
    end
    subgraph OPTICS[Optical Path]
        O1[Sliding Aperture]
        O2[Polished Mirror Optional]
    end
    subgraph DET[Detector Assembly]
        D1[Infrared Window ZnSe or Quartz]
        D2[Thermopile Sensing Element]
        D3[Cold Junction Heat Sink]
    end
    subgraph READ[Readout and Compute]
        R1[Nanovolt Amplifier]
        R2[Digital Display in mV]
        R3[Computation of epsilon]
    end
    S1 --> O1
    S2 --> O1
    O1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Procedural Flowchart: Lab Procedure as a State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Setup
    Setup --> HeaterOff: Power ON, Switches Checked
    HeaterOff --> HeaterOn: Set Temperature Setpoint
    HeaterOn --> Stabilizing: Monitor TC1 and TC2
    Stabilizing --> Stabilizing: Wait 10 to 15 Minutes for Steady State
    Stabilizing --> MeasureBlackbody: Temperatures Matched within 1 K
    MeasureBlackbody --> ReadVb: Record Voltage Vb in mV
    ReadVb --> MoveDetector: Slide Carriage to Specimen Position
    MoveDetector --> MeasureSpecimen: Allow 30 s to Settle
    MeasureSpecimen --> ReadVs: Record Voltage Vs in mV
    ReadVs --> ComputeEpsilon: Calculate epsilon equals Vs divided by Vb
    ComputeEpsilon --> NextSetpoint: Increment Setpoint by 20 Degree C
    NextSetpoint --> Stabilizing: Repeat for 5 Setpoints
    NextSetpoint --> Done: All Setpoints Complete
    Done --> HeaterOff: Switch OFF Heater, Allow Cooling
    HeaterOff --> [*]: Report Tabulated Results
```

## 4.4 Energy Interaction Schematic (Logical Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Electrical Heater Input Q_in] --> C[Conduction Through Specimen]
    C --> R[Radiation Emission to Detector]
    C --> CL[Conduction Loss to Mount]
    C --> CV[Convection Loss to Air]
    R --> D[Detected Signal Proportional to epsilon sigma T4]
    D --> EP[Emitted Power Computed]
    BB[Blackbody Reference at Same T] --> R2[Reference Radiation to Detector]
    R2 --> EP
    EP --> RATIO[Ratio Gives Emissivity epsilon]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for Students:** Each block in these diagrams corresponds to a *physical* or *functional* element of the actual lab rig. When the examiner asks "trace the signal path," walk from the specimen → aperture → thermopile → amplifier → display → ratio calculation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** when using σT⁴ → costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Not stating the assumption that specimen and blackbody are at the same T** when applying ε = Vs/Vb → costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Reporting ε > 1** without explanation → examiner will treat as *systematic error*, not a measurement result. Always comment.
> 4. **Confusing "emissivity" with "emitted power"** in definitions → they are *not* the same quantity; ε is dimensionless, E is W/m².
> 5. **Omitting the radiation law** (Stefan–Boltzmann) in the theory section → at least 3 marks lost in a 14-mark answer.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention Kirchhoff's law** as the theoretical basis → common 2-mark loss.
> 7. **Not tabulating** readings with units in the answer → 1-mark penalty for "presentation."

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define emissivity. State the range of emissivity for real engineering materials. [3 Marks]
**[Model Answer – KTU Pattern]**

**Emissivity** is the ratio of the thermal radiation intensity emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a perfect blackbody at the same thermodynamic temperature.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_{\text{surface}}(T)}{E_{\text{blackbody}}(T)} = \frac{E_{\text{surface}}(T)}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

- For a **perfect blackbody**, ε = 1 (theoretical maximum).
- For a **perfect reflector / ideal mirror**, ε = 0 (theoretical minimum).
- For real engineering materials, ε lies in the range **0 < ε < 1**, e.g.:
  - Polished silver: ε ≈ 0.02
  - Polished aluminum: ε ≈ 0.05
  - Polished brass: ε ≈ 0.10
  - Oxidized copper: ε ≈ 0.78
  - Soot / lampblack: ε ≈ 0.95

**Range: 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1 (dimensionless).**

> **Model Answer Word Count Target:** 60–80 words. (3 marks awarded for definition + range + 2 examples.)

---

### Q2. State and explain Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation. [3 Marks]
**[Model Answer – KTU Pattern]**

**Kirchhoff's Law** states that at thermal equilibrium, the **spectral emissivity** of a body equals its **spectral absorptivity** at the same wavelength and temperature:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

**Explanation:** A body that absorbs strongly at a given wavelength must also emit strongly at that wavelength; otherwise, in equilibrium, it would accumulate or lose energy at that wavelength — a violation of the second law of thermodynamics. The consequence is that **good absorbers are good emitters** (black surfaces, ε ≈ 1) and **poor absorbers are poor emitters** (shiny metals, ε ≈ 0.05).

> **Model Answer Word Count Target:** 70–90 words. (3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for explanation.)

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **experimental method to determine the total emissivity of a given specimen** in the lab. List the apparatus required and state the governing principle. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** During an experiment, a brass disc and a blackbody cavity are both heated to 120 °C. The thermopile detector reads **V_b = 33.1 mV** when aimed at the blackbody and **V_s = 9.7 mV** when aimed at the brass specimen. Calculate the emissivity of brass at 120 °C. Verify your result by computing the actual emissive power of the brass specimen. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution – Q. A (a) [7 Marks]

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Emissivity measurement apparatus (heating unit, specimen holder, blackbody cavity)
2. Test specimen (brass / copper / aluminum disc, typically 30–50 mm diameter)
3. Two calibrated **Type-K thermocouples** (one for specimen, one for blackbody)
4. **Thermopile detector** mounted on a sliding carriage
5. Digital millivoltmeter (or nanovoltmeter) with amplifier
6. Temperature indicator / controller
7. Cooling water supply for shields (if applicable)
8. Polishing kit and emery paper (for specimen preparation)

**Governing Principle:**
The method is based on the **comparison technique** using the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law of Thermal Radiation**. Since the specimen and the blackbody cavity are maintained at the *same* absolute temperature T, the σT⁴ and detector-responsivity factors cancel when the ratio of detector voltages is taken:

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{V_s}{V_b}$$

**Procedure (Step-wise — Examiner's Checklist):**

1. **Specimen preparation:** Polish the test disc with fine emery paper, clean with acetone, and mount it on the heating plate. The blackbody cavity (a deep cylindrical hole in a high-emissivity block) is mounted adjacent to it.
2. **Thermocouple placement:** Attach one thermocouple to the back face of the specimen and another inside the blackbody cavity. Connect both to the digital temperature indicator.
3. **Detector positioning:** Place the thermopile detector on the sliding carriage at a fixed distance (typically 100–150 mm) from the targets. Ensure the line of sight is normal to the surfaces.
4. **Heating:** Switch on the heater and set the controller to the desired setpoint (e.g., 60 °C).
5. **Steady-state wait:** Allow 10–15 minutes for both surfaces to reach thermal equilibrium. Confirm via the two thermocouples that T_specimen ≈ T_blackbody (within ± 1 K).
6. **Reading V_b:** Slide the carriage to view the blackbody cavity. Wait 30 seconds for the detector to stabilize. Record V_b in mV.
7. **Reading V_s:** Slide the carriage to view the specimen. Wait 30 seconds. Record V_s in mV.
8. **Computation:** Calculate ε = V_s / V_b.
9. **Repeat:** Increment the setpoint by 20 °C (e.g., 80, 100, 120, 140 °C) and repeat steps 5–8.
10. **Tabulation & graph:** Tabulate ε vs T(K) and plot the curve. Compare with standard emissivity tables.

**Precautions:**
- Ensure the detector is shielded from stray reflections and convection currents.
- Keep surroundings at constant ambient temperature during a trial.
- Re-zero the detector before each reading.
- Do not touch the polished specimen face with bare fingers (oils alter ε).

**Schematic (textual block diagram — to be drawn on paper):**

```
[Heater] → [Specimen Disc] ← T1 (Thermocouple) → [Temp. Indicator]
         → [Blackbody Cavity] ← T2 (Thermocouple] → [Temp. Indicator]
                                         ↘
                            [Sliding Detector Carriage] → [Thermopile] → [mV Meter]
```

> **Marking Distribution (7 marks):** Apparatus list (1), principle (2), procedure (3), precautions (1).

---

#### 📘 Model Solution – Q. A (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- T = 120 °C = 120 + 273.15 = **393.15 K**
- V_b = 33.1 mV (blackbody detector reading)
- V_s = 9.7 mV (specimen detector reading)
- σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴

**Step 1: Compute Emissivity**

Using the direct comparison formula:

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{V_s}{V_b} = \frac{9.7 \text{ mV}}{33.1 \text{ mV}} = 0.2930$$

**Emissivity of brass at 120 °C: ε ≈ 0.293**

**Step 2: Verification via Stefan–Boltzmann Law**

Blackbody emissive power at 120 °C:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (393.15)^{4}$$

Compute T⁴:

$$(393.15)^{2} = 154567$$
$$(393.15)^{4} = (154567)^{2} = 2.3891 \times 10^{10}$$

Therefore:

$$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.3891 \times 10^{10} = 1354.5 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Actual Emissive Power of Brass**

$$E_s = \varepsilon_s \cdot E_b = 0.2930 \times 1354.5 = 396.9 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 4: Comment on Result**

- A polished brass disc at room temperature has ε ≈ 0.05, but at 120 °C the value 0.293 is consistent with **lightly oxidized / machined brass** (standard table value: 0.25–0.40 at 100–200 °C). ✓
- The result lies in the permissible range **0 < ε < 1**, confirming experimental validity. ✓
- The value of ~397 W/m² is a physically reasonable radiative flux for a moderately hot metal surface.

> **Marking Distribution (7 marks):** Formula & ratio (2), numerical evaluation (2), σT⁴ verification (2), comment on result (1).

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **theoretical basis** of the emissivity experiment, deriving the relationship $\varepsilon = V_s / V_b$ starting from the Stefan–Boltzmann law. State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A metal specimen emits **500 W/m²** of thermal radiation at a surface temperature of 600 K. Determine (i) the emissivity of the specimen, and (ii) the wavelength of maximum emission. Given σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴ and b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution – Q. B (a) [7 Marks]

**Theoretical Basis:**

Thermal radiation emitted by a blackbody is governed by the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**, which states that the total hemispherical emissive power is proportional to the fourth power of absolute temperature:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

where σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴.

For a *real surface*, the emitted power is reduced by the factor ε (emissivity):

$$E_s = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$$

**Derivation of the Lab Formula:**

In the emissivity rig, the detector output voltage V is proportional to the incident radiative flux:

$$V = k \cdot E$$

where k is the detector responsivity (same geometry & optics for both targets).

**For the blackbody cavity (at temperature T):**

$$V_b = k \cdot E_b = k \cdot \sigma T^{4} \quad (\text{since } \varepsilon_b = 1)$$

**For the test specimen (at the same temperature T):**

$$V_s = k \cdot E_s = k \cdot \varepsilon_s \cdot \sigma T^{4}$$

**Taking the ratio:**

$$\frac{V_s}{V_b} = \frac{k \cdot \varepsilon_s \cdot \sigma T^{4}}{k \cdot \sigma T^{4}} = \varepsilon_s$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_s = \frac{V_s}{V_b}}$$

**Assumptions Made:**

1. The **specimen and the blackbody cavity are at the same temperature** (T_s = T_b). If they differ, σT⁴ does not cancel.
2. The detector is **linear** in its response (V ∝ E).
3. The detector responsivity **k is identical** for both targets (same geometry, same distance, same view factor).
4. Radiation from **surroundings is negligible** or symmetric (T_sur ≪ T).
5. The specimen is **opaque** (τ = 0).
6. The blackbody cavity is a *perfect* blackbody (ε_b = 1). (In practice, deep cavities with ε_wall ≥ 0.95 give ε_eff > 0.99.)

> **Marking Distribution (7 marks):** S-B law (1), real surface (1), detector linearity (1), ratio derivation (2), assumptions (2).

---

#### 📘 Model Solution – Q. B (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- E_s = 500 W/m²
- T = 600 K
- σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴
- b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K

**Part (i): Emissivity**

Blackbody emissive power at 600 K:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (600)^{4}$$

Compute step by step:

$$(600)^{2} = 360000$$
$$(600)^{4} = (360000)^{2} = 1.296 \times 10^{11}$$

$$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.296 \times 10^{11} = 7348.32 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

Emissivity:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_s}{E_b} = \frac{500}{7348.32} = 0.0680$$

**Emissivity ≈ 0.068** — this value is consistent with a **polished aluminum or stainless steel** surface at 600 K. ✓

**Part (ii): Wavelength of Maximum Emission (Wien's Law)**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{600}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} = 4.83 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 4.83 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

This wavelength lies in the **mid-infrared region**, consistent with Wien's law predictions for moderate-temperature sources. (For comparison, the sun at 5800 K peaks at ~0.5 μm, visible light; the human body at 310 K peaks at ~9.4 μm, far-IR.)

> **Marking Distribution (7 marks):** σT⁴ computation (2), ε evaluation (2), Wien's law setup (1), final λ_max (1), physical comment (1).

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these points verbatim before the exam:**

- **Definition:** ε = E_surface(T) / E_blackbody(T) — dimensionless, 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** E_b = σT⁴, σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴.
- **Real surface emission:** E = εσT⁴.
- **Lab master equation:** ε_s = V_s / V_b (works only when both targets are at the same T).
- **Wien's Law:** λ_max · T = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** ε = α (at thermal equilibrium).
- **Planck's Law:** Spectral distribution; integrates to Stefan–Boltzmann.
- **Always convert °C to Kelvin** before using σT⁴.
- **Polished metals have low ε (~0.05); oxidized/rough surfaces have high ε (~0.85).**
- **Emissivity generally increases with temperature** (especially for metals).
- **Blackbody is a perfect emitter** (ε = 1) AND a perfect absorber (α = 1).
- **Apparatus sequence:** Polish specimen → mount → heat → wait for steady state → record V_b → record V_s → compute ε.
- **Common errors:** Not waiting for steady state, touching the polished face, mismatched temperatures, ambient drift, dirty aperture.
- **Practical uses:** Furnace design, Low-E coatings, IR pyrometry correction, spacecraft thermal control.
- **Net radiation from a hot surface to cooler surroundings:** q_net = εσ(T_s⁴ − T_sur⁴).
- **Real-world sanity check:** A 100 °C blackbody emits ~1098 W/m²; a polished metal at 100 °C emits only ~50–100 W/m².

> **Final Tip:** When asked "Why does the comparison method work?", the single best answer is: **"Because σ, T⁴, and detector responsivity k are common to both measurements, they cancel in the ratio — only ε remains, which is the unknown we want."**
